\text{COPQ} = \dfrac{5.0 + 8.0}{200} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{COPQ} = \dfrac{13.0}{200} \times 100 = 6.5\%
$$

#### Step 5 — Analytical Interpretation (Marks-Winning Step)

The plant spends only **3.5 lakhs** on prevention but suffers **8.0 lakhs** in external failure. This means **failure costs are nearly 2.3 times the prevention spend**, indicating a *reactive* quality culture.

The healthy ratio, recommended by Juran, is **P : A : IF : EF = 30 : 30 : 25 : 15** (preventive-leaning). Here the actual ratio is roughly **16 : 25 : 23 : 36**, which is unhealthy.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall:** Students often *compute TQC correctly* but lose marks because they **fail to comment on the cost structure** or fail to suggest an improvement (increase P, reduce EF). Always close with a recommendation.

### 3.2 Worked Example — Process Capability Calculation

**Problem:** A bottling line fills fruit juice. The label specifies **500 ± 10 mL**. After 30 samples, mean fill $\mu = 501$ mL and standard deviation $\sigma = 2$ mL. Compute $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$ and interpret.

#### Step 1 — Identify Specification Limits

$$
\text{USL} = 510 \text{ mL}, \quad \text{LSL} = 490 \text{ mL}
$$

#### Step 2 — Compute Cp

$$
C_{p} = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}
$$

$$
C_{p} = \dfrac{510 - 490}{6 \times 2} = \dfrac{20}{12}
$$

$$
C_{p} = 1.67
$$

#### Step 3 — Compute Cpk (Centring Check)

$$
C_{pk(\text{upper})} = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{510 - 501}{3 \times 2} = \dfrac{9}{6} = 1.5
$$

$$
C_{pk(\text{lower})} = \dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{501 - 490}{3 \times 2} = \dfrac{11}{6} = 1.83
$$

$$
C_{pk} = \min\{1.5,\ 1.83\}
$$

$$
C_{pk} = 1.5
$$

#### Step 4 — Interpretation

Since $C_p \geq 1.33$ and $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$, the process is **capable and reasonably well-centred**, but $C_{pk} < C_p$ indicates a *slight upward off-centring*. A minor adjustment of the filling nozzle (to lower the mean by 1 mL) would raise $C_{pk}$ further.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The TQM Pillars — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following diagram illustrates how the eight TQM principles interlock to deliver customer satisfaction and continuous improvement.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef pillar fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2a4a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff,font-weight:bold
    classDef core fill:#c00000,stroke:#7a0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff,font-weight:bold
    classDef tool fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#1b4d20,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef out fill:#f4b400,stroke:#a07400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000

    P1[Customer Focus]:::pillar
    P2[Leadership and Vision]:::pillar
    P3[Involvement of People]:::pillar
    P4[Process Approach]:::pillar
    P5[System Approach to Management]:::pillar
    P6[Continuous Improvement Kaizen]:::pillar
    P7[Factual Approach to Decisions]:::pillar
    P8[Mutually Beneficial Supplier Relations]:::pillar

    CORE[(TQM FRAMEWORK)]:::core

    TOOLS[Seven Tools of Quality and DMAIC]:::tool

    OUT1[Customer Satisfaction]:::out
    OUT2[Reduced Quality Costs]:::out
    OUT3[FSSAI and ISO 22000 Compliance]:::out
    OUT4[Long Term Organisational Success]:::out

    P1 --> CORE
    P2 --> CORE
    P3 --> CORE
    P4 --> CORE
    P5 --> CORE
    P6 --> CORE
    P7 --> CORE
    P8 --> CORE
    CORE --> TOOLS
    TOOLS --> OUT1
    TOOLS --> OUT2
    TOOLS --> OUT3
    TOOLS --> OUT4
```

### 4.2 The PDCA Deming Wheel — Continuous Improvement Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef step fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#1b4d20,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef meta fill:#c00000,stroke:#7a0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff,font-weight:bold

    META{(Continuous Improvement)}:::meta

    PLAN[PLAN - Identify problem and design improvement]:::step
    DO[DO - Implement on a small scale]:::step
    CHECK[CHECK - Measure results against targets]:::step
    ACT[ACT - Standardise or revise the plan]:::step

    META --> PLAN
    PLAN --> DO
    DO --> CHECK
    CHECK --> ACT
    ACT --> META
```

### 4.3 The DMAIC Roadmap for Six Sigma Implementation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef phase fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2a4a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef goal fill:#f4b400,stroke:#a07400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000

    D[DEFINE - Voice of Customer and Project Charter]:::phase
    M[MEASURE - Data Collection and Baseline Sigma]:::phase
    A[ANALYSE - Root Cause Analysis with Fishbone]:::phase
    I[IMPROVE - Pilot Solution and Validate Cp Cpk]:::phase
    C[CONTROL - Statistical Process Control Charts]:::phase

    G1[(DPMO less than 3.4)]:::goal
    G2[(Customer Satisfaction more than 95 percent)]:::goal

    D --> M --> A --> I --> C --> G1
    C --> G2
```

### 4.4 The Quality Cost Optimisation Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef zone fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#1b4d20,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef zone2 fill:#c00000,stroke:#7a0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff

    Z1[Low Prevention Spend]:::zone2
    Z2[High Failure Costs - Total Quality Cost is High]:::zone2
    Z3[Optimum Investment Zone]:::zone
    Z4[High Prevention Low Failure Costs]:::zone
    Z5[Over Prevention Waste]:::zone2

    Z1 --> Z2
    Z2 -->|Increase Prevention| Z3
    Z3 --> Z4
    Z4 -->|Diminishing Returns| Z5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This curve is the visual answer to the classical KTU essay question — *"Discuss the economic justification for investing in quality."* Move from reactive (left) to optimal (centre), and avoid over-investment (right).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1. Define Total Quality Management. List any four principles of TQM. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
TQM is a customer-focused, system-wide management philosophy that involves all employees in the continuous improvement of products, services, and processes to achieve long-term success through customer satisfaction.

Any four principles:
1. Customer Focus
2. Leadership and Vision
3. Involvement of People
4. Process Approach
5. Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)
6. Factual Approach to Decision Making
7. Mutually Beneficial Supplier Relationships
8. System Approach to Management

*(Any four well-explained principles will fetch full 3 marks.)*

#### Question 2. Explain the PDCA cycle in brief. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
The PDCA cycle is a four-step iterative method for continuous improvement:
- **Plan** – Identify the problem and design an improvement plan.
- **Do** – Execute the plan on a small pilot scale.
- **Check** – Measure and compare results against targets.
- **Act** – Standardise if successful, or revise and re-enter the cycle.

It is also called the *Deming Wheel* and forms the operational backbone of TQM.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> Choose **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

#### Question A. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the various principles of TQM with suitable examples from the food industry. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the contributions of any three quality gurus to the development of TQM. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Principles of TQM in the Food Industry:**

1. **Customer Focus:** A bakery that conducts monthly sensory panels to capture customer feedback on sweetness, texture, and freshness — and adjusts the sugar level of its cookies accordingly.
2. **Leadership:** The Plant Head of a dairy plant sets a goal of *"Zero foreign-body complaints within 18 months"* and aligns all departmental KPIs with this target.
3. **Involvement of People:** Even packing-line workers are trained to perform basic visual inspections and are empowered to stop the line if contamination is suspected.
4. **Process Approach:** Mapping every stage from milk reception → pasteurisation → chilling → packaging as a controlled process with defined inputs, outputs, and controls.
5. **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen):** Reducing spice-mixing time from 12 minutes to 9 minutes through incremental operator-driven trials.
6. **Factual Approach to Decision Making:** Using microbiological count data (CFU/mL) instead of visual judgement to release pasteurised milk.
7. **Mutually Beneficial Supplier Relationships:** A long-term contract with a local wheat farmer that mandates pesticide-residue testing at the farm gate — benefiting both farmer (assured price) and the bakery (assured input quality).
8. **System Approach to Management:** Integrating HACCP, ISO 22000, and FSSAI compliance into one unified Food Safety Management System rather than treating them as siloed programmes.

**(b) Contributions of Three Quality Gurus:**

1. **W. Edwards Deming (1900 – 1993):** Introduced the PDCA cycle, the *System of Profound Knowledge*, and the **14 Points for Management**. He emphasized statistical process control and variation reduction. His work rebuilt Japanese industry post-World War II.

2. **Joseph M. Juran (1904 – 2008):** Defined quality as *"fitness for use"* and developed the **Quality Trilogy** — Quality Planning, Quality Control, and Quality Improvement. He also introduced the **Pareto Principle (80/20 rule)** into quality management.

3. **Kaoru Ishikawa (1915 – 1989):** Developed the **Cause-and-Effect (Fishbone) Diagram** and promoted **Quality Circles** — small groups of workers who meet regularly to solve quality problems. He championed company-wide quality control (CWQC) in Japan.

4. *(Optional fourth guru — Philip B. Crosby):* Advocated *"Quality is Free"* and the concept of **Zero Defects**, arguing that the cost of prevention is always less than the cost of correction.

---

#### Question B. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** What are the seven tools of quality control? Explain the **Pareto Chart** and the **Cause-and-Effect Diagram** in detail. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the concept of **Quality Costs**. Describe **Prevention Costs** and **Appraisal Costs** with suitable examples from a food processing unit. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) The Seven Tools of Quality Control:**

1. Cause-and-Effect Diagram (Ishikawa / Fishbone)
2. Check Sheet
3. Control Chart
4. Histogram
5. Pareto Chart
6. Scatter Diagram
7. Flow Chart

**Pareto Chart:**
A Pareto chart is a bar-graph combined with a cumulative-frequency line. It applies the **80/20 principle** — typically 80 % of the problems arise from 20 % of the causes. In a snack factory, if 80 % of consumer complaints come from only two issues — *"too oily"* and *"broken in packet"* — the chart visually directs management to solve these two before anything else.

**Cause-and-Effect Diagram:**
Also called the **Fishbone Diagram**, it is used to identify the root causes of a quality problem by categorising potential causes under major headings — typically **6M**: *Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Mother Nature (Environment)*. For example, to investigate *"off-flavour in flavoured milk"*, the team lists possible causes under each M — outdated flavour concentrate (Material), improper storage temp (Method), worn-out homogeniser seal (Machine), etc.

**(b) Concept of Quality Costs (Juran's P-A-I-E Model):**

Quality cost is the total cost incurred by an organisation to ensure product quality. Juran categorised these into four families — **Prevention, Appraisal, Internal Failure, and External Failure**.

**Prevention Costs** are those incurred to *prevent defects from occurring*. Examples in a food unit:
- Designing Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) for spice blending.
- Conducting GMP and HACCP training for production staff.
- Calibrating weighing scales, thermometers, and pH meters.
- Preventive maintenance of ovens and conveyors.
- Supplier audits at the farm level.

**Appraisal Costs** are those incurred to *detect and measure defects*. Examples in a food unit:
- Incoming raw-material inspection (moisture, pesticide residue).
- In-process quality checks (brix, viscosity, temperature).
- Finished-product microbiological testing (TPC, coliform, yeast & mould).
- Third-party lab testing for shelf-life studies.
- Costs of sensory evaluation panels.

**Internal Failure Costs** (for completeness): rework, scrap, downtime.
**External Failure Costs** (for completeness): returns, recalls, brand damage.

The economic goal is to **increase P such that IF and EF decrease sharply**, lowering the *Total Quality Cost* in the long run.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - In part (a), students *list* the seven tools but forget to **explain Pareto and Fishbone in detail** with diagrams — losing up to 3 marks.
> - In part (b), students *confuse Prevention with Appraisal*. Remember the mnemonic: **"Prevent first, Appraise later"** — Prevention is *before* the defect occurs; Appraisal is the *inspection* that finds the defect.
> - Always end with **real food-industry examples** (HACCP, FSSAI, microbial testing) — abstract factory examples lose marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **TQM = Total Quality Management** — an organization-wide, customer-driven, continuous-improvement philosophy.
- The **eight principles** form the structural pillars of every TQM answer.
- The **PDCA cycle** is the engine of continuous improvement; loop it indefinitely.
- The **seven tools** (Pareto, Fishbone, Histogram, Check Sheet, Control Chart, Scatter, Flow Chart) are diagnostic instruments for solving quality problems.
- **Six Sigma DMAIC** is the modern data-driven methodology aiming at **3.4 DPMO**.
- **Quality Cost** = P + A + IF + EF. The *healthy* industry spends more on Prevention than on Failure.
- **Cp ≥ 1.33** indicates a capable process; **Cpk = Cp** indicates perfect centring.
- **COPQ** benchmark for food industry: **5 – 10 %** of sales.
- **DPMO** formula: $DPMO = \dfrac{D}{U \times O} \times 10^{6}$.
- TQM directly supports **HACCP, ISO 22000, and FSSAI** compliance in food industries.
- Always include **food-industry-specific examples** in KTU answers — examiners reward context-specific understanding.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Food Standards and Specification - Global Food Safety Initiative
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCFTT503 - FOOD SAFETY AND QUALITY REGULATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Food Standards and Specification - Global Food Safety Initiative # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Global Food Safety Initiative (GFSI)** is a foundation operating under **The Consumer Goods Forum (CGF)**, a global industry network. Established in **2000** in the aftermath of several high-profile food safety crises, GFSI is officially defined as a *"business-driven initiative for the continuous improvement of food safety management systems to ensure confidence in the delivery of safe food to consumers worldwide."* > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight:** GFSI does **NOT** certify food businesses itself. It is a *benchmarking body* that compares existing private food safety standards against a single, globally accepted set of requirements. Once a standard is "GFSI-recognised," a certificate issued under that standard is accepted by GFSI's retail and manufacturing members worldwide. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Universal Translator" Imagine a world where every country has its own **driving license**. If a tourist wants to drive in a foreign country, they would have to take that country's test again and again. This is exactly what was happening in the food industry before 2000: a factory certified under one scheme (say, **BRC**) had to be re-audited under another (say, **SQF**) to supply different retailers. **GFSI acts like an International Driving Permit (IDP).** - It does not teach you to drive. - It does not test you itself. - Instead, it *verifies that your existing national license meets its global standard.* - Once recognised, the certificate is portable across hundreds of global retailers. This is the famous GFSI promise: **"Once Certified, Accepted Everywhere."** ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Key Identifiers | Parameter | Value / Reference | |---|---| | **Founding Year** | **2000** | | **Parent Body** | **The Consumer Goods Forum (CGF)** | | **Headquarters** | **Paris, France** | | **Initial Founders** | Major global retailers (Walmart, Carrefour, Tesco, Metro, etc.) | | **Core Function** | **Benchmarking** (not certification) | | **Governance Body** | **GFSI Steering Committee** | | **Technical Body** | **GFSI Technical Committee** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **GFSI Recognised Certification Schemes (as of standard public listings):** BRCGS (Brand Reputation Compliance Global Standards), FSSC 22000, IFS (International Featured Standards), SQF (Safe Quality Food), GLOBALG.A.P., and PrimusGFS. These are the "accepted licenses" that meet the GFSI "IDP" criteria. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 The "Why" Behind GFSI Before 2000, the proliferation of private food safety standards led to: - **Audit Fatigue:** Suppliers being audited 20–50 times per year. - **Duplication of Effort:** Same HACCP plan verified 30 different ways. - **Inconsistent Benchmarks:** Different standards demanded different rigor levels. - **Supply Chain Inefficiency:** Increased cost of compliance passed to consumers. GFSI was created to solve this by introducing a **single global benchmark**, eliminating redundancy and providing **equivalence of standards**. ## 2.2 The Three Pillars of GFSI The GFSI framework is built upon three interconnected pillars: **Pillar 1: Benchmarking** - Compares a private scheme's requirements against GFSI's *Benchmarking Requirements*. - Output: A scheme becomes "GFSI Recognised." **Pillar 2: Equivalence** - Once two or more schemes are GFSI-recognised, they are deemed **equivalent** in terms of food safety outcomes. - Retailers can therefore accept any recognised certificate. **Pillar 3: Continuous Improvement** - GFSI requirements are reviewed every 4 years. - Industry incidents, regulatory changes, and emerging science are incorporated. ## 2.3 The GFSI Benchmarking Process — Operational Logic The benchmarking of a scheme follows a structured, multi-stage logic: 1. **Application Submission:** The standard owner submits an application and pays a fee. 2. **Document Review:** A *GFSI Benchmarking Manager* verifies alignment with the GFSI Requirements Document. 3. **Office Audit:** An independent *GFSI-licensed Benchmarking Lead Assessor* conducts an in-office assessment of the scheme's governance, training, and accreditation arrangements. 4. **On-Site Witness Audit:** The assessor observes an actual certification audit being performed in the field to verify practical rigor. 5. **Recommendation Report:** Findings are submitted to the **GFSI Technical Committee**. 6. **Peer Review & Decision:** The committee votes on recognition; a successful vote grants **GFSI Recognition** for a defined scope and duration. 7. **Surveillance:** Recognised schemes undergo periodic surveillance to maintain their status. ## 2.4 The Scope of GFSI Recognition GFSI applies a **scoped approach** rather than a blanket "yes/no." Recognised schemes are approved for specific **Industry Scopes**, including: - **AI** — Farming of Animals - **AII** — Farming of Fish & Seafood - **B** — Farming of Plants - **C** — Animal Primary Conversion - **D** — Plant Primary Conversion - **E** — Processing of Animal Products - **F** — Processing of Plant Products - **G** — Processing of Products with Ingredients of Mixed Origin - **H** — Production of Food Additives & Ingredients - **I** — Production of Pet Food - **J** — Production of (Bio)chemicals - **K** — Storage & Distribution - **L** — Provision of Transport & Delivery Services - **M** — Production of Food Packaging Materials - **N** — Provision of Catering Services - **O** — Provision of Retail / Wholesale Services > [!IMPORTANT] > A scheme recognised for **Scope E** (Processing of Animal Products) is **NOT automatically recognised for Scope D** (Plant Primary Conversion). Each scope requires independent evidence. ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Key Point | Mnemonic / Keyword | |---|---|---| | **Full Form of GFSI** | Global Food Safety Initiative | "Global" implies **international equivalence** | | **Parent Body** | The Consumer Goods Forum (CGF) | CGF is the *parent*, GFSI is the *child* | | **Founded** | 2000 | Post-food-crisis era | | **What GFSI Does** | *Benchmarks* standards (not certifies factories) | "Benchmarker, not certifier" | | **Key Output** | GFSI Recognition + Equivalence | "Once certified, accepted everywhere" | | **Governance** | GFSI Steering Committee | Sets strategic direction | | **Technical Decisions** | GFSI Technical Committee | Decides recognition status | | **Number of Industry Scopes** | 15+ scoped categories (AI to O) | Scoped, not blanket | | **Review Cycle** | Every **4 years** | Continuous improvement | | **Pillars** | Benchmarking, Equivalence, Continuous Improvement | "BEC" | | **Founder Companies** | Walmart, Carrefour, Tesco, Metro, Ahold | Retailer-led origin | | **Major Recognised Schemes** | BRCGS, FSSC 22000, IFS, SQF, GLOBALG.A.P. | "B-I-S-G" | --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Mathematical / Logical Model of GFSI Equivalence While GFSI is qualitative, its **equivalence logic** can be expressed as a formal decision matrix. For two certification schemes, $S_1$ and $S_2$, to be mutually accepted, they must each independently satisfy the GFSI benchmark $G$ across a scope $k$: $$ E(S_1, S_2, k) \;=\; \begin{cases} \text{ACCEPTED}, & \text{if } R(S_1, k) = \text{TRUE} \;\land\; R(S_2, k) = \text{TRUE} \\ \text{REJECTED}, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ Where: - $E$ is the **Equivalence Function** between two schemes for a given industry scope. - $R(S_i, k)$ is the **Recognition Status Function** — returns TRUE if scheme $S_i$ is GFSI-recognised in scope $k$. - $\land$ denotes the logical AND operator. **Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown:** **Step 1:** A scheme $S_1$ is submitted for evaluation. - The benchmark $G$ is the formal GFSI Requirements Document (covering scopes, audit methodology, accreditation, etc.). **Step 2:** The Recognition Function is evaluated. $$ R(S_1, k) \;\rightarrow\; \text{TRUE} \quad \text{only if } \quad \text{Benchmark}(S_1, G, k) = \text{PASS} $$ **Step 3:** A second scheme $S_2$ undergoes the same evaluation. $$ R(S_2, k) \;\rightarrow\; \text{TRUE} \quad \text{only if} \quad \text{Benchmark}(S_2, G, k) = \text{PASS} $$ **Step 4:** Equivalence is logically valid only when both individual recognitions are TRUE. $$ \text{Acceptance} \;=\; R(S_1, k) \cdot R(S_2, k) $$ Where the result is treated as a binary value: $1$ (ACCEPTED) or $0$ (REJECTED). > [!NOTE] > This is the **equivalence theorem of GFSI** — equivalence is *not* a direct comparison between $S_1$ and $S_2$, but an *indirect comparison* through the common benchmark $G$. This is why GFSI is often called a "meta-standard." ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Key Elements of the GFSI Benchmarking Requirements The GFSI Benchmarking Requirements are built around **eleven core "key elements"** (also called *gating elements*) that every scheme must satisfy. The table below is a high-yield, examination-ready analysis: | # | GFSI Key Element | Why It Matters (Engineering Perspective) | Typical Evidence | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Food Safety Management System (FSMS)** | Demonstrates a structured, documented system (similar to ISO 22000). | Documented policies, procedures, manuals. | | 2 | **Good Manufacturing / Hygiene Practices (GMP/GHP)** | The baseline prerequisite programs (PRPs). | Hygiene SOPs, sanitation records, zoning. | | 3 | **HACCP / Risk Management** | Hazard identification and control. | HACCP plan with CCPs and critical limits. | | 4 | **Senior Management Commitment** | Top-down responsibility for food safety. | Signed policy, resource allocation, KPIs. | | 5 | **Food Safety Culture** | The human / behavioural dimension of safety. | Training, communication, culture assessment. | | 6 | **Hazard Analysis for Specific Scopes** | Sector-specific risk analysis. | Allergen management, environmental monitoring. | | 7 | **Quality Management** | Beyond safety, into legality and quality. | Non-conformance handling, complaints. | | 8 | **Auditor Competence** | The auditor must be qualified and registered. | Training records, exam results, calibration. | | 9 | **Certification Body Accreditation** | The body issuing the certificate must itself be accredited (e.g., by ISO 17021-1). | Accreditation certificate to ISO 17065. | | 10 | **Audit Duration & Reporting** | Minimum audit time ensures depth. | Audit report structure and minimum hours. | | 11 | **Traceability** | One-up, one-down traceability is mandatory. | Mock recall records, lot tracking. | ## 3.3 Worked Example: Establishing Equivalence Between Two Schemes **Problem:** A multinational food manufacturer (plant) in Kerala exports to a buyer in Germany. The plant holds **FSSC 22000** certification. The German buyer demands a **BRCGS** certificate. Can the plant be exempted from BRCGS? **Step-by-Step Solution using the GFSI Equivalence Logic:** **Step 1:** Identify the relevant scope. The plant processes fruit products. - Industry Scope: **F** (Processing of Plant Products). **Step 2:** Check the recognition status of both schemes for Scope F. - $R(\text{FSSC 22000}, F) = \text{TRUE}$ → FSSC 22000 is GFSI-recognised for Scope F. - $R(\text{BRCGS}, F) = \text{TRUE}$ → BRCGS is GFSI-recognised for Scope F. **Step 3:** Apply the equivalence function. $$ E(\text{FSSC 22000, BRCGS, F}) = R(\text{FSSC 22000}, F) \;\land\; R(\text{BRCGS}, F) $$ $$ E = \text{TRUE} \;\land\; \text{TRUE} \;=\; \text{TRUE} $$ **Step 4:** Conclusion. - The plant's FSSC 22000 certificate is **equivalent to BRCGS** for Scope F. - The German buyer, if it is a GFSI member, **should accept** the FSSC 22000 certificate without demanding a separate BRCGS audit. **Result:** A new audit is not required. The manufacturer has saved approximately **15–20 audit days per year** and the associated costs, demonstrating the **commercial value of GFSI equivalence**. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 GFSI Position in the Global Food Safety Ecosystem The following block-level functional architecture flow illustrates the **hierarchical relationship** between regulators, standard owners, certification bodies, and the food industry under the GFSI framework. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[International Regulators
Codex Alimentarius
FAO/WHO] --> B[GFSI Benchmarking
Requirements] C[National Regulators
FSSAI India
USFDA EFSA] --> B D[Scientific Bodies
and Research] --> B B --> E{GFSI Technical
Committee} F[Standard Owner 1
BRCGS] --> G[Scheme Benchmark
Evaluation] H[Standard Owner 2
FSSC 22000] --> G I[Standard Owner 3
IFS] --> G J[Standard Owner 4
SQF] --> G K[Standard Owner 5
GLOBALG.A.P.] --> G G --> E E -->|Recognition Granted| L[Recognised
Certification Scheme] E -->|Recognition Rejected| M[Improvement
Required] L --> N[Certification Bodies
Accredited ISO 17065] N --> O[Certified Food Businesses
Manufacturer Processor] O --> P[Global Retailers and
Consumers] P -->|Feedback| F P -->|Feedback| H P -->|Feedback| I P -->|Feedback| J P -->|Feedback| K ``` ## 4.2 The GFSI Benchmarking Process Flow (Step-by-Step) ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Step 1
Application
Submission] --> A2[Step 2
Document
Review] A2 --> A3[Step 3
Office
Audit] A3 --> A4[Step 4
On-Site
Witness Audit] A4 --> A5[Step 5
Recommendation
Report] A5 --> A6[Step 6
Technical
Committee Vote] A6 --> A7{Decision} A7 -->|Approved| A8[Step 7
GFSI Recognition
Granted] A7 -->|Rejected| A9[Step 8
Improvement
Plan Required] A8 --> A10[Step 9
Surveillance
Every 4 Years] ``` ## 4.3 Functional Architecture of GFSI Governance ```mermaid flowchart TD G1[The Consumer Goods Forum
CGF Board of Directors] --> G2[GFSI Steering Committee
Strategic Direction] G2 --> G3[GFSI Technical Committee
Recognition Decisions] G2 --> G4[GFSI Stakeholder
Working Groups] G3 --> G5[Benchmarking Managers
and Lead Assessors] G5 --> G6[Certification Bodies
Accredited to ISO 17065] G6 --> G7[Certified Food Industry
Facilities Globally] ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Pattern]** **"What is the Global Food Safety Initiative (GFSI) and what is its primary function?"** **Model Answer:** - The **Global Food Safety Initiative (GFSI)** is a foundation under *The Consumer Goods Forum (CGF)*, established in **2000**. - It was created by leading international retailers to **reduce food safety risks, harmonize private food safety standards, and reduce duplication of audits** in the global food supply chain. - Its **primary function is benchmarking** — it does not certify food businesses directly. Instead, it compares private food safety certification schemes (such as FSSC 22000, BRCGS, SQF, IFS) against a single, globally accepted benchmark known as the **GFSI Benchmarking Requirements**. - A scheme that successfully passes this benchmark is granted **"GFSI Recognition"**, which makes it equivalent to all other recognised schemes, allowing the slogan: **"Once Certified, Accepted Everywhere."** ### **Question 2 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Pattern]** **"List any three industry scopes defined under the GFSI benchmarking framework with one example activity for each."** **Model Answer:** | Scope Code | Scope Name | Example Activity | |---|---|---| | **E** | Processing of Animal Products | Slaughterhouse, dairy processing | | **F** | Processing of Plant Products | Fruit juice bottling, rice milling | | **K** | Storage and Distribution | Cold storage warehouses, logistics hubs | > [!NOTE] > A scheme is recognised for **specific scopes** and not blanket-recognised. Each scope must be evaluated independently. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) ### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU ESE Model – Dec 2023 Pattern]** **(a)** Explain the **structure and governance of the Global Food Safety Initiative (GFSI)**. Describe the roles of the *Consumer Goods Forum*, the *GFSI Steering Committee*, and the *GFSI Technical Committee* in ensuring global food safety. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **three pillars of GFSI** — Benchmarking, Equivalence, and Continuous Improvement — and explain how the **"Once Certified, Accepted Everywhere"** principle benefits the food industry. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution to Question A:** **(a) Structure and Governance of GFSI (7 Marks)** GFSI operates as a foundation under the umbrella of **The Consumer Goods Forum (CGF)**, the world's largest industry network for consumer goods, headquartered in Paris. The governance is structured into three tiers: **1. The Consumer Goods Forum (CGF) – Strategic Apex:** - Formed by the merger of CIES (Food Business Forum) and the Global Commerce Initiative in 2009. - Sets the overarching strategic vision for global consumer goods, including food safety. - Provides the platform within which GFSI operates. **2. GFSI Steering Committee – Directional Leadership:** - Comprises senior executives from GFSI member companies (retailers, manufacturers, certification bodies). - Defines strategic priorities, approves funding, and oversees alignment with CGF objectives. - Approves changes to the GFSI Benchmarking Requirements. **3. GFSI Technical Committee – Operational Authority:** - Comprises food safety technical experts from member organizations. - Responsible for the **technical evaluation of standards** submitted for benchmarking. - Conducts peer review of benchmarking reports and **votes on the recognition status** of submitted schemes. - Monitors surveillance audits of already-recognised schemes to ensure continued compliance. **Supporting Layer:** GFSI Benchmarking Managers and licensed Lead Assessors are appointed to conduct the actual document reviews, office audits, and on-site witness audits. **(b) Three Pillars of GFSI and the "Once Certified, Accepted Everywhere" Principle (7 Marks)** **Pillar 1 – Benchmarking:** - GFSI develops a single set of **Benchmarking Requirements** covering FSMS, HACCP, GMP, auditor competence, traceability, and other essential elements. - Private scheme owners submit their standards for comparison against these requirements. Schemes that meet or exceed the requirements are **recognised**. **Pillar 2 – Equivalence:** - All GFSI-recognised schemes are deemed **equivalent in terms of food safety outcomes**, even if their structure and nomenclature differ. - This equivalence is what allows a retailer that demands, for example, SQF, to accept a certificate issued under FSSC 22000. **Pillar 3 – Continuous Improvement:** - GFSI requirements are revised every **four years** to incorporate new science, regulatory developments, and lessons from real-world food safety incidents. - Recognised schemes must align with the latest version of the requirements to maintain recognition. **The "Once Certified, Accepted Everywhere" Benefit:** - For **Manufacturers/Processors:** A single GFSI-recognised certificate can open access to hundreds of global retailers, eliminating the need for multiple parallel audits. - For **Retailers:** Audit duplication is reduced, allowing them to redirect resources from second-party audits to more meaningful supply chain verification. - For **Consumers:** Harmonised rigor leads to consistently higher food safety standards worldwide. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often confuse GFSI with a *certification body* (e.g., ISO, BRCGS, SQF). GFSI **does NOT issue certificates**. If an answer describes GFSI as a certifier, full marks are typically **deducted**. Always remember: **GFSI benchmarks the certifiers, it does not certify the factories.** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice for the Same Module]** **(a)** Describe the **step-by-step GFSI Benchmarking Process** that a private food safety standard must undergo to achieve GFSI Recognition. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** List and briefly explain **five key elements of the GFSI Benchmarking Requirements** that any food safety scheme must satisfy. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution to Question B:** **(a) Step-by-Step GFSI Benchmarking Process (7 Marks)** 1. **Application Submission:** The owner of a private food safety standard (e.g., BRCGS, IFS) submits a formal application to GFSI, accompanied by the scheme's documentation and the prescribed benchmarking fee. 2. **Document Review:** A GFSI Benchmarking Manager conducts a thorough desk review of the scheme's documents, including the standard itself, auditor competency requirements, certification body accreditation rules, and audit duration guidelines. 3. **Office Audit:** A GFSI-licensed Benchmarking Lead Assessor visits the offices of the scheme owner to verify the integrity of scheme management, training programs for auditors, complaint handling, and governance structures. 4. **On-Site Witness Audit:** The assessor witnesses a **real certification audit** being conducted in a food facility. This verifies that the scheme is being applied with the rigor claimed in documentation, not just on paper. 5. **Recommendation Report:** The Lead Assessor compiles a detailed report, including findings, non-conformities, and a recommendation regarding recognition, which is then submitted to the GFSI Technical Committee. 6. **Peer Review and Decision:** The GFSI Technical Committee conducts an independent peer review of the report. A formal vote decides whether the scheme is granted GFSI Recognition, and for which **Industry Scopes**. 7. **Recognition and Surveillance:** Successful schemes receive **GFSI Recognition** for a defined period. Surveillance audits are conducted periodically to ensure ongoing compliance; the standard is also reassessed during the **4-yearly review cycle** of the GFSI Benchmarking Requirements. **(b) Five Key Elements of the GFSI Benchmarking Requirements (7 Marks)** 1. **Food Safety Management System (FSMS):** The scheme must require a documented, risk-based management system covering policy, planning, operation, and verification — the foundation of any modern food safety system. 2. **HACCP / Risk Management:** The scheme must mandate a Codex Alimentarius-aligned HACCP system with clearly identified hazards, critical control points, critical limits, monitoring, and corrective actions. 3. **Good Manufacturing / Hygiene Practices (GMP/GHP):** The scheme must require prerequisite programs covering sanitation, personal hygiene, pest control, allergen management, and equipment maintenance. 4. **Auditor Competence and Certification Body Accreditation:** Auditors must be demonstrably competent (training, exams, on-site experience), and the certification body issuing the certificate must hold valid **ISO 17065 accreditation** from a recognised accreditation body. 5. **Traceability and Recall:** The scheme must enforce a robust one-up, one-down traceability system and require documented mock-recall exercises to demonstrate rapid and effective product withdrawal capability. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Warning (for Question B):** A common error is to confuse the **GFSI Key Elements** (the *gating requirements* a scheme must meet) with the **Industry Scopes** (the *categories* of activities a scheme is recognised for). Marks are lost when students present scopes as if they were the technical requirements. Always clearly differentiate: **Key Elements = What the scheme must contain**; **Scopes = To which industries the recognition applies.** --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **GFSI stands for Global Food Safety Initiative** — a foundation under The Consumer Goods Forum, established in **2000**. - **GFSI is NOT a certification body** — it is a **benchmarking body**. - The GFSI mission is: *"Once Certified, Accepted Everywhere."* - GFSI works on **three pillars**: **Benchmarking, Equivalence, Continuous Improvement**. - **Benchmarking Requirements** are reviewed every **four years** to incorporate new science and regulations. - GFSI recognition is **scoped**, not blanket — there are **15+ Industry Scopes** (AI through O). - **Recognised schemes** include: **BRCGS, FSSC 22000, IFS, SQF, GLOBALG.A.P., PrimusGFS**. - **Governance structure:** CGF Board → GFSI Steering Committee → GFSI Technical Committee → Benchmarking Managers / Lead Assessors → Certification Bodies (ISO 17065 accredited) → Certified Food Businesses. - **Benchmarking process:** Application → Document Review → Office Audit → On-Site Witness Audit → Recommendation → Peer Review/Decision → Recognition → Surveillance. - The **eleven key elements** of the GFSI Benchmarking Requirements include FSMS, HACCP, GMP, Senior Management Commitment, Food Safety Culture, Auditor Competence, ISO 17065 Accreditation, Audit Duration, Traceability, and Hazard Analysis by Scope. - GFSI was founded by global retailers (Walmart, Carrefour, Tesco, Metro, Ahold) — its origin is **retailer-led**. - The benefit to industry is **audit fatigue reduction** and **cost efficiency** through equivalence of recognised schemes. - For KTU exams, remember the **distinction between GFSI (benchmarker) and ISO / BRCGS / SQF (certifiers)** — confusing these is a common marker of superficial preparation.

topics.title: CODEX
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT503 - FOOD SAFETY AND QUALITY REGULATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** CODEX

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Codex Alimentarius** (Latin for "Food Code") is a collection of internationally recognized standards, guidelines, and codes of practice related to food, food production, and food safety. It is established and maintained by the **Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC)**, a joint body of the **Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO)** of the United Nations and the **World Health Organization (WHO)**. Established in **1963**, the CAC is the principal international body responsible for the implementation of the Joint FAO/WHO Food Standards Programme.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** As per the KTU 2024 PCFTT503 syllabus (Module 3), the focus on CODEX requires understanding its *genesis, structure, objectives, and global regulatory influence* — particularly its reference status under the World Trade Organization (WTO) Agreements.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition (Examination-Ready):**
> The *Codex Alimentarius* is the definitive international reference point for the development of food standards, serving as a harmonization framework that protects consumer health and ensures fair practices in the international food trade.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Global Food Rulebook"

Imagine the world as a giant open-air food court where every country (chef) prepares and sells its own traditional dishes (food products). Without a common rulebook, one chef might use too much chili (additives), another might use a dirty water jug (contamination), and a third might mislabel a dish as "vegetarian" when it contains meat (fraud). Customers get sick, trade disputes arise, and trust breaks down.

**The Codex Alimentarius is the universally agreed-upon, multilingual "Master Rulebook"** that sits in every food court manager's office. It doesn't cook the food, but it dictates:
- What is safe to serve (food additives, contaminants, hygiene).
- How it should be labeled (so customers know what they are eating).
- What claims can be made (organic, GMO, halal, etc.).

When a dispute arises between countries under the WTO, this rulebook becomes the **legal reference** — much like a constitution acts as the ultimate authority in a courtroom.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Benchmarks

- **Establishment Year of CAC:** **1963** (Parent bodies: FAO [1945] and WHO [1948]).
- **Headquarters:** Rome, Italy (FAO Headquarters).
- **Current Membership:** **189 Member Countries** + **1 Member Organization** (European Union) + **231+ Observer Organizations**.
- **Core Aim:** Protect consumer health and ensure fair practices in the international food trade.
- **Binding Nature:** *Voluntary* for member states in adoption, but *binding* in international trade disputes under the WTO's **SPS (Sanitary and Phytosanitary)** and **TBT (Technical Barriers to Trade)** Agreements.

![Codex Framework Visual](https://placeholder.invalid/framework.png)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Founding Pillars of Codex

The operational philosophy of CODEX rests on three foundational pillars, all derived from the original *Statement of Purpose* drafted in 1962 by the FAO Conference:

1. **Pillar I — Consumer Health Protection (Primary Mandate)**
   - Lead the global effort to ensure food is *safe* and *wholesome*.
   - Address microbiological hazards, chemical contaminants, pesticide residues, veterinary drug residues, and food additives.
   - **Why it matters:** Prevents the international spread of foodborne diseases.

2. **Pillar II — Fair Practices in Food Trade (Secondary Mandate)**
   - Coordinate all international food standardization work.
   - Prevent disguised barriers to trade (e.g., one country banning another country's product without scientific justification).
   - **Why it matters:** Without harmonized standards, international trade collapses into legal chaos.

3. **Pillar III — Coordination of Standards (Operational Mandate)**
   - Bring together international governmental and non-governmental organizations to develop food standards.
   - Avoid duplication of work (e.g., between IDF — International Dairy Federation, OIV — wine, etc.).
   - **Why it matters:** Efficiency and global consensus.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Structure of the CAC

| Component | Sub-Component | Specific Role | Examples |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Parent Bodies** | FAO & WHO | Sponsorship, Funding, Science | JECFA, JMPR, JEMRA |
| **Executive Organ** | **Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC)** | Final decision-making, Adoption of Standards | Plenary Sessions (Annual/Biennial) |
| **Subsidiary Bodies** | General Subject Committees | Cross-cutting standards | CCFH, CCFICS, CCFAS, CCFA, CCPR, CCCF |
| **Subsidiary Bodies** | Commodity Committees | Specific commodity standards | CCFFP, CCFFV, CCPFV, CCFO, CCASIA, CCNASWP |
| **Subsidiary Bodies** | Coordinating Committees | Regional coordination | CCASIA, CCNAFRIC, CCLAC, CCEURO, CCNASWP, CCSWA |
| **Subsidiary Bodies** | Ad-hoc Task Forces | Time-bound, specific issues | Previously: GM Foods, Animal Feeding, Antimicrobial Resistance |
| **Scientific Support** | Joint FAO/WHO Expert Bodies | Independent Risk Assessment | **JECFA**, **JMPR**, JEMRA, JEMNU |
| **Legal Reference** | WTO Agreements | Trade Dispute Settlement | **SPS Agreement**, **TBT Agreement** |

### 2.3 The Scientific Wing of Codex — The "Risk Analysis Paradigm"

A critical, often-tested concept in KTU is the relationship between **Codex** and **Risk Analysis**. Codex decisions are based on a tripartite framework established with WTO:

$$
\text{Risk Analysis} = f(\text{Risk Assessment}, \text{Risk Management}, \text{Risk Communication})
$$

- **Risk Assessment** is the *scientific* job — carried out by expert bodies (e.g., JECFA evaluates food additives, contaminants, veterinary drug residues).
- **Risk Management** is the *policy* job — carried out by the CAC and its committees (sets MRLs, MPLs, etc.).
- **Risk Communication** is the *engagement* job — involving member states, NGOs, and industry.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering/Regulatory Utility

For a Food Technology or Biotechnology graduate under KTU 2024, knowledge of CODEX is non-negotiable for careers in:
- **Export-oriented food processing units** (compliance documentation).
- **FSSAI (Food Safety and Standards Authority of India)** — India's national body, which aligns Indian standards to CODEX.
- **Third-party food testing laboratories** (accreditation requirements).
- **Global MNCs** (Nestlé, Unilever, Cargill) — strict internal adherence to CODEX MRLs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Procedural Derivation

### 3.1 The Step-by-Step Standard-Setting Procedure of CODEX

The KTU examiner frequently asks: *"Describe the procedure for development of Codex Standards"* (14-mark question). The procedural derivation is as follows:

**Step 1 — Proposal and Critical Review (Initiation)**
- A national government or a subsidiary body submits a written proposal justifying *why* a new standard is needed.
- The **Executive Committee** reviews the proposal for alignment with the strategic plan, scientific necessity, and feasibility.

**Step 2 — Scientific Risk Assessment (Independent Science)**
- FAO and WHO convene their joint expert bodies (e.g., **JECFA** for additives, **JMPR** for pesticides).
- These bodies produce a *monograph* detailing NOAEL (No Observed Adverse Effect Level), ADI (Acceptable Daily Intake), ARfD, and quantitative risk characterization.

**Step 3 — Drafting by the Subsidiary Body (Risk Management Draft)**
- The relevant Codex Committee drafts the standard, typically through **8 Steps** of the formal *Uniform Procedure for the Elaboration of Codex Standards and Related Texts*.
- Drafts circulate globally for member state comments.

**Step 4 — Adoption at the CAC (Final Authority)**
- The Commission reviews the draft, considers all written comments, and formally adopts the standard.
- Adopted standards are then published in the *Codex Alimentarius* (the official compendium).

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — The 8 Steps of Codex Standard Elaboration

The Uniform Procedure involves 8 progressive steps, which can be analytically grouped into **3 Phases**:

$$
\text{8-Step Procedure} = \{\text{Phase I (Steps 1-4)}\} \cup \{\text{Phase II (Steps 5-7)}\} \cup \{\text{Phase III (Step 8)}\}
$$

| Step | Action Required | Approval Stage | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1** | Commission decides to elaborate a standard. | CAC Plenary | Formal initiation |
| **2** | Secretariat arranges preparation. | Secretariat | Administrative setup |
| **3** | Subsidiary body prepares a Proposed Draft. | Committee | Initial drafting |
| **4** | Member Governments & FAO/WHO comment on the Proposed Draft. | Public Consultation | Stakeholder input |
| **5** | Subsidiary body considers comments, redrafts as a Draft Standard. | Committee | Revision |
| **6** | Member Governments consider the Draft Standard. | Public Consultation | Wider review |
| **7** | Subsidiary body submits the Draft to the Commission. | Committee → CAC | Final recommendation |
| **8** | Commission adopts the Draft as a *Codex Standard*. | CAC Plenary | Formal adoption |

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Fast Track" — 5-Step Procedure:**
> For standards that require urgent attention or that are scientifically unambiguous (e.g., MRLs derived directly from JECFA), an *accelerated* 5-step procedure may be used. This bypasses duplicate consultations to expedite adoption.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: CODEX vs. WTO Agreements

This is a high-yield KTU comparison. The relationship is the *raison d'être* of CODEX in international trade:

$$
\text{WTO} \xrightarrow{\text{References}} \text{CODEX} \xrightarrow{\text{Provides}} \text{Scientific Basis}
$$

| Parameter | **SPS Agreement** | **TBT Agreement** | **CODEX Alimentarius** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Scope** | Food safety (pests, diseases, contaminants) | Quality, labeling, packaging, technical requirements | Source of international standards |
| **Relation to CODEX** | **Explicitly recognizes CODEX** as the international benchmark for food safety measures. | Recognizes CODEX for voluntary standards related to quality and labeling. | Acts as the *scientific reference* for both. |
| **Burden of Proof** | If a country deviates above CODEX MRL, it must justify scientifically. | If a country imposes stricter technical rules, they must be non-discriminatory. | Provides the *defense* that a measure is consistent with international norms. |

### 3.4 Analytical Derivation: ADI Calculation Used by JECFA (Foundational Math)

While CODEX is a *regulatory* body, the science underpinning it relies on toxicology. JECFA calculates the **Acceptable Daily Intake (ADI)** using the foundational safety equation:

$$
\text{ADI} = \frac{\text{NOAEL}}{\text{Safety Factor}}
$$

Where:
- $\text{NOAEL}$ = No Observed Adverse Effect Level (mg/kg body weight/day)
- $\text{Safety Factor}$ = A standard uncertainty factor of **100** (10 for interspecies extrapolation $\times$ 10 for intraspecies variability), increased to higher values (e.g., **1000, 2000**) when data is incomplete or effects are severe (e.g., for endocrine disruptors).

**Step-by-step numerical example for a hypothetical food additive:**

- **Step 1:** Long-term animal studies reveal the NOAEL = **25 mg/kg body weight/day**.
- **Step 2:** Apply standard Safety Factor of **100**.
- **Step 3:** Calculate ADI:

$$
\text{ADI} = \frac{25\ \text{mg/kg/day}}{100} = 0.25\ \text{mg/kg body weight/day}
$$

- **Step 4:** For a **60 kg adult**, the maximum acceptable daily intake becomes:

$$
\text{Acceptable Intake} = 0.25\ \text{mg/kg/day} \times 60\ \text{kg} = 15\ \text{mg/day}
$$

This ADI is then used by Codex to set **Maximum Residue Limits (MRLs)** in food commodities.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Organizational Architecture of CODEX

The following block-level functional architecture maps the interactions between FAO/WHO, the CAC, and its subsidiary bodies, as required for the KTU Module 3 understanding:

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[FAO - Food and Agriculture Organization] --> B1[Codex Alimentarius Commission - CAC]
    A2[WHO - World Health Organization] --> B1
    B1 --> C1[Executive Committee]
    C1 --> D1[General Subject Committees]
    C1 --> D2[Commodity Committees]
    C1 --> D3[Coordinating Committees]
    C1 --> D4[Ad Hoc Intergovernmental Task Forces]
    A1 --> E1[JECFA - Joint FAO/WHO Expert Committee on Food Additives]
    A1 --> E2[JMPR - Joint Meeting on Pesticide Residues]
    A1 --> E3[JEMRA - Joint FAO/WHO Meetings on Microbiological Risk Assessment]
    A2 --> E1
    A2 --> E2
    A2 --> E3
    E1 --> B1
    E2 --> B1
    E3 --> B1
    B1 --> F1[Codex Alimentarius - The Compendium of Standards]
    G1[WTO - World Trade Organization] -.References.-> F1
    H1[National Governments - e.g., FSSAI India] -.Adopts.-> F1
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — The 8-Step Standard Development Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    step1[Step 1 - Commission approves standard development] --> step2[Step 2 - Secretariat prepares preliminary work]
    step2 --> step3[Step 3 - Subsidiary body drafts Proposed Draft]
    step3 --> step4[Step 4 - Member states and FAO/WHO comment]
    step4 --> step5[Step 5 - Subsidiary body redrafts as Draft Standard]
    step5 --> step6[Step 6 - Member states consider Draft Standard]
    step6 --> step7[Step 7 - Subsidiary body submits Draft to CAC]
    step7 --> step8[Step 8 - Commission formally adopts as Codex Standard]
    step8 --> finalOut[Published in Codex Alimentarius]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — The Risk Analysis Paradigm in Codex

```mermaid
graph LR
    RA[Risk Assessment - Scientific] --> RM[Risk Management - Policy]
    RM --> RC[Risk Communication - Engagement]
    RC --> RA
    RA --> JECFA1[JECFA/JMPR Provide Science]
    RM --> CAC1[CAC and Committees Set Limits]
    JECFA1 --> CAC1
    CAC1 --> RC
```

### 4.4 Explanation of the Schematics

- **Diagram 1** illustrates the *hierarchy*: FAO and WHO are parent bodies that jointly fund and house the CAC. The CAC oversees four types of subsidiary bodies, while joint expert bodies (JECFA, JMPR, JEMRA) provide the science. The final product — the *Codex Alimentarius* compendium — is then referenced by the WTO and adopted by national governments (like India's FSSAI).
- **Diagram 2** shows the linear, sequential 8-step elaboration procedure. Note the feedback loops in the form of consultations in steps 4, 5, and 6.
- **Diagram 3** shows the iterative relationship among the three pillars of risk analysis — a foundational concept tested in KTU essays.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
Define the term *Codex Alimentarius*. Mention its parent organizations and the year of its establishment.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The *Codex Alimentarius* is a comprehensive collection of internationally recognized standards, guidelines, and codes of practice related to food, food production, and food safety. It is established by the **Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC)**, a joint body of the **Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO)** and the **World Health Organization (WHO)**. The CAC was formally established in **1963** with the primary objective of protecting consumer health and ensuring fair practices in the international food trade. (3 Marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
What is the role of **JECFA** in the Codex system?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**JECFA** stands for the *Joint FAO/WHO Expert Committee on Food Additives*. It is an independent international scientific expert body administered jointly by FAO and WHO. Its primary role is to perform *risk assessments* — scientifically evaluating the safety of food additives, contaminants, naturally occurring toxicants, and residues of veterinary drugs in food. The output of JECFA — including ADIs and PTWIs (Provisional Tolerable Weekly Intake) — provides the scientific basis upon which the CAC and its committees set *risk management* standards, such as **Maximum Residue Limits (MRLs)** and permitted use levels of additives. (3 Marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Evaluation Pattern:** For each 14-mark question, two sub-parts are typically provided. Sub-part (a) carries **7 marks** and sub-part (b) carries **7 marks**. The marks are awarded for *key points*, *diagrams*, and *structured logical flow*.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**"[KTU University Exam — Jun 2024 Model Question]**
**(a)** Explain in detail the *objectives, principles, and organizational structure* of the Codex Alimentarius Commission. (7 Marks)
**(b)** Describe the *8-step procedure* for the elaboration of Codex Standards with the help of a suitable flow diagram. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**(a) Objectives, Principles, and Organizational Structure of CAC (7 Marks):**

**Objectives (3 Marks):**
1. **Protecting the health of consumers** — ensuring food is safe and free from harmful contaminants, additives, and microbiological hazards.
2. **Ensuring fair practices in the international food trade** — preventing the use of food regulations as disguised barriers to trade.
3. **Promoting coordination of all food standardization work** undertaken by international governmental and non-governmental bodies.

**Organizational Structure (4 Marks):**
- **Parent Bodies:** FAO and WHO provide financial, administrative, and scientific support.
- **Executive Organ:** The **Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC)** — meets biennially (or annually as required), composed of all 189+ member countries. It adopts all final standards.
- **Executive Committee:** Conducts business between plenary sessions.
- **Subsidiary Bodies:**
   - **General Subject Committees** (e.g., Food Hygiene, Food Additives, Contaminants, Pesticide Residues, Food Import/Export Inspection).
   - **Commodity Committees** (e.g., Fats and Oils, Fish and Fishery Products, Fresh Fruits and Vegetables).
   - **Coordinating Committees** for six geographic regions (Africa, Asia, Europe, Latin America & Caribbean, Near East, North America & South-West Pacific).
   - **Ad-hoc Intergovernmental Task Forces** for time-bound, urgent issues.
- **Scientific Support:** JECFA, JMPR, JEMRA, and JEMNU (Nutrition).

**(b) 8-Step Procedure for Elaboration of Codex Standards (7 Marks):**

The Codex system uses a *Uniform Procedure* with 8 steps (described in Section 3.2 of these notes). The sequence is:

$$
\text{Initiation} \rightarrow \text{Drafting} \rightarrow \text{Consultation} \rightarrow \text{Revision} \rightarrow \text{Adoption}
$$

**Step 1:** The Commission decides that a Codex standard is required and refers it to a subsidiary body. *(1 mark)*
**Step 2:** The Codex Secretariat arranges for the preparation of the work, including a working paper outlining the scope. *(0.5 mark)*
**Step 3:** Members of the subsidiary body prepare a *Proposed Draft Standard* for circulation. *(1 mark)*
**Step 4:** The Proposed Draft is sent to all Member Governments and FAO/WHO for written comments. *(1 mark)*
**Step 5:** The subsidiary body considers the comments, redrafts, and finalizes a *Draft Standard*. *(1 mark)*
**Step 6:** The Draft Standard is again circulated to Member Governments for further comment. *(1 mark)*
**Step 7:** The subsidiary body submits the Draft Standard to the Commission for adoption. *(0.5 mark)*
**Step 8:** The Commission formally adopts the Draft as a *Codex Standard*, which is then published in the Codex Alimentarius compendium. *(1 mark)*

*(See Mermaid Diagram 4.2 for the visual representation.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**"[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**(a)** Discuss the relationship between the **Codex Alimentarius Commission** and the **World Trade Organization (WTO)**. Explain the role of Codex under the **SPS** and **TBT** Agreements. (7 Marks)
**(b)** Explain the concept of **Risk Analysis** as applied to food safety by the Codex system. Differentiate between **Risk Assessment** and **Risk Management**. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**(a) Relationship between CAC and WTO (7 Marks):**

Although the **Codex Alimentarius** standards are *voluntary* at the national level, they acquire *de facto binding* status in international trade through their reference in WTO Agreements.

- **The WTO Reference (2 Marks):** The WTO's **Agreement on the Application of Sanitary and Phytosanitary Measures (SPS Agreement)** explicitly identifies the *Codex Alimentarius* as the **international benchmark** for food safety. Similarly, the **Agreement on Technical Barriers to Trade (TBT Agreement)** refers to Codex for standards related to labeling, quality, and packaging that are not directly related to food safety (e.g., nutritional labeling).

- **Significance of the Reference (3 Marks):** When a country sets a national food safety measure that **conforms to a Codex Standard**, it gains a *presumption of consistency* with the SPS/TBT Agreement. Conversely, if a country imposes a **stricter measure than the Codex Standard**, it bears the **burden of scientific proof** to justify the deviation in any WTO dispute settlement case. This is why Codex standards are sometimes called "the lingua franca of international food trade."

- **Dispute Settlement (2 Marks):** The WTO Dispute Settlement Body (DSB) frequently relies on JECFA and JMPR scientific opinions (which underpin Codex standards) as authoritative evidence in trade disputes. Example: The *EC – Hormones* case, where Codex MRLs played a central role in the legal proceedings.

**(b) Risk Analysis in the Codex System (7 Marks):**

The Codex Alimentarius Commission formally adopted a **Risk Analysis Framework** in 2003, which has three interconnected components:

$$
\text{Risk Analysis} = \text{Risk Assessment} + \text{Risk Management} + \text{Risk Communication}
$$

**Risk Assessment (3.5 Marks):**
- It is the **scientific** component of risk analysis.
- Conducted by independent expert bodies: **JECFA** (food additives, contaminants, veterinary drug residues), **JMPR** (pesticide residues), **JEMRA** (microbiological hazards).
- Involves: *Hazard Identification → Hazard Characterization → Exposure Assessment → Risk Characterization*.
- Output: Quantitative metrics like the **Acceptable Daily Intake (ADI)**, expressed in mg/kg body weight/day.

**Risk Management (3.5 Marks):**
- It is the **policy** component, conducted by the CAC and its subsidiary committees.
- Based on the Risk Assessment, managers set *tolerable limits*, e.g., **Maximum Residue Limits (MRLs)** in food, permissible use levels of additives, and *Codex Codes of Hygienic Practice*.
- Risk Managers must consider the Risk Assessment, along with other legitimate factors (e.g., economic impact, consumer perception, technical feasibility).
- Output: *Codex Standards, Guidelines, and Codes of Practice*.

**Key Distinction:** *Risk Assessment is value-free and scientific*; *Risk Management is value-laden and policy-driven*. The functional separation between these two ensures scientific independence in setting food safety standards.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing the parent bodies:** Students often write that CODEX was established by the *United Nations* or *WTO*. This is **incorrect**. It is a joint body of **FAO and WHO**. The WTO only *references* Codex, it does not create it.
> 2. **Confusing JECFA, JMPR, and JEMRA:** JECFA is for *food additives and contaminants*; JMPR is for *pesticide residues*; JEMRA is for *microbiological risk assessment*. Mixing them up costs 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Saying CODEX standards are legally binding:** They are *voluntary* in adoption, but *binding in trade disputes* under the WTO. Both halves of the statement must be mentioned.
> 4. **Omitting the flow diagram in the 8-step question:** A neatly drawn flow diagram is worth **1 mark minimum** and boosts visual presentation. Always include one.
> 5. **Confusing ADI with MRL:** ADI is the *amount a person can safely consume daily*; MRL is the *maximum concentration allowed in a specific food commodity*. They are different concepts.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

For rapid last-minute revision before your KTU ESE examination, the following high-density checklist summarizes everything you need to know about CODEX:

- **Full Form:** Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC) — Latin for "Food Code."
- **Parent Organizations:** **FAO + WHO** (not UN, not WTO).
- **Year of Establishment:** **1963**.
- **Headquarters:** Rome, Italy.
- **Core Mandate:** Protect consumer health & ensure fair practices in international food trade.
- **Membership:** 189+ Member Countries + EU + 231 Observers.
- **Subsidiary Bodies (4 Types):** General Subject Committees, Commodity Committees, Coordinating Committees (6 regional), Ad-hoc Task Forces.
- **Scientific Wing:** JECFA (additives, contaminants), JMPR (pesticide residues), JEMRA (microbiology), JEMNU (nutrition).
- **WTO Linkage:** Codex is the international reference for **SPS** and **TBT** Agreements.
- **Risk Analysis Equation:** Risk Assessment (Science) + Risk Management (Policy) + Risk Communication (Engagement).
- **ADI Formula:** ADI = NOAEL ÷ Safety Factor (default = 100).
- **Standard-Setting:** Uses the **8-Step Uniform Procedure** (5-Step accelerated version also exists).
- **Binding Nature:** *Voluntary* for member states, but *binding in WTO trade disputes* as a reference benchmark.
- **FSSAI Linkage:** India aligns its Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006, with Codex standards.
- **Key Pointers for 14-Mark Answers:** Always include: (1) Definition, (2) Year/Parent Bodies, (3) Objectives (3-fold), (4) Subsidiary Bodies (4 types), (5) Scientific Wing (JECFA, JMPR), (6) WTO linkage, (7) Risk Analysis concept, (8) A neatly drawn flow diagram.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final High-Yield Tip:** If you are running short on time in the exam hall, prioritize the **3-fold objectives** of CAC and the **4 types of subsidiary bodies**. These two concepts, when structured well, can single-handedly fetch you 5–6 marks in any 14-mark question on CODEX. Good luck!

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: National Codex Committee of India
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT503 - FOOD SAFETY AND QUALITY REGULATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** National Codex Committee of India

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# National Codex Committee of India (NCCI) — Section 1: Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **National Codex Committee of India (NCCI)** is the apex national coordination body formally recognized by the Government of India that serves as the **single-point contact and consultative forum** between India and the **Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC)**, the joint intergovernmental body of the Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO) and the World Health Organization (WHO) responsible for developing international food standards, guidelines, and codes of practice.

NCCI was **constituted in the year 1969** by the Ministry of Health and Family Welfare (MoHFW), Government of India, to act as the institutional interface through which India formulates its national position, coordinates expert opinions, and channels technical recommendations upward to the international Codex system. NCCI is administratively housed under the **Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI)** and is chaired by the **Director General of Health Services (DGHS)** of the Government of India.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The National Codex Committee of India is a *frequently asked* concept for both **3-mark (short notes)** and **14-mark (descriptive)** questions. Students must be able to draw the structural hierarchy, list the composition, and describe the functions of NCCI and its Shadow Committees.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Diplomatic Embassy" Model

Imagine the **Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC)** in Geneva/Rome as the **"World Parliament on Food Safety"** — a global assembly where 189+ member countries negotiate, debate, and vote on international food standards that ultimately govern what reaches every plate on Earth.

Now, in this "World Parliament," a single country cannot simply walk in and start talking. It needs an **official delegation**, a **briefing dossier**, a **negotiation strategy**, and a **mechanism to gather input from all domestic stakeholders** (farmers, industry, scientists, consumer groups, regulators) *before* the meeting.

This is exactly what the **National Codex Committee of India (NCCI)** does for India.

| Real-World Analogy Element | NCCI Equivalent |
|---|---|
| The World Parliament | Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC) |
| India's Official Diplomatic Mission | National Codex Committee of India (NCCI) |
| Ambassador leading the delegation | DGHS (Chairperson of NCCI) |
| Pre-meeting briefings & position papers | Shadow Committee Reports |
| Domestic stakeholders being consulted | Ministries, Industry, Consumers, FSSAI, BIS, APEDA, MPEDA, IIP, CFTRI, IITs |
| International negotiation platform | Codex Committees & Task Forces |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** Just as a country's foreign policy is *not* made by a single person in a boardroom but is the result of a structured consultative process, India's *food safety diplomatic positions* are also not improvised. NCCI is the **structured engine** that converts diverse Indian voices (farmers, industry, regulators, scientists) into a **single, coherent, technically-defensible national position** at international Codex meetings.

## 1.3 Genesis and Constitutional Basis

- **Year of Constitution:** **1969**, by the Government of India's Ministry of Health and Family Welfare.
- **Trigger:** India's accession to the **Codex Alimentarius Commission in 1964** created the need for a domestic institutional structure.
- **Current Administrative Nodal Ministry:** Ministry of Health & Family Welfare (MoHFW).
- **Current Nodal Implementing Agency:** **Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI)**, headquartered at FDA Bhawan, Kotla Road, New Delhi.
- **Codex Contact Point for India:** The **FSSAI** officially functions as the designated **Codex Contact Point (CCP)** for India, which is the official communication channel between NCCI and the Codex Secretariat in Geneva.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Recall Card:** NCCI = 1969 + MoHFW + DGHS (Chair) + FSSAI (Nodal) = India's national Codex platform.

## 1.4 Key Definitions and Standard Metrics

| Term | Definition | KTU Weightage |
|---|---|---|
| **Codex Alimentarius** | Latin for "Food Code"; international collection of food standards, guidelines, and codes of practice adopted by the CAC. | High |
| **NCCI** | Apex national committee of India coordinating with CAC. | Very High |
| **Codex Contact Point (CCP)** | Official designated national body for communication with Codex Secretariat. FSSAI is India's CCP. | High |
| **Shadow Committee** | An Indian national committee that mirrors an international Codex Subject Committee and prepares India's position on that subject. | Very High |
| **CAC** | Codex Alimentarius Commission — the governing body of the Joint FAO/WHO Food Standards Programme. | High |
| **FAO** | Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations. | Medium |
| **WHO** | World Health Organization of the United Nations. | Medium |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic to remember the founding year:** *NCCI = 1 + 9 + 6 + 9 → "1 India 9 Code 6 of 9 World"* (approximate associative memory tool). The official answer is **1969**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# National Codex Committee of India (NCCI) — Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Knowledge Sheet

## 2.1 Objectives of NCCI (The "WHY" Behind the Committee)

The NCCI is not a regulatory body — it does *not* create binding Indian law. Instead, it is a **coordination, consultation, and consensus-building platform**. The objectives, in structured order, are:

1. **To coordinate India's participation** in all meetings of the Codex Alimentarius Commission, its Executive Committee, and its subsidiary bodies (Subject Committees, Task Forces, Regional Coordinating Committees).
2. **To formulate India's national position** on agenda items before every Codex meeting, after consolidating input from all relevant stakeholders.
3. **To nominate Indian delegates and experts** to represent India in international Codex meetings and working groups.
4. **To establish and supervise Shadow Committees** that mirror international Codex Committees and prepare subject-specific technical positions.
5. **To disseminate Codex standards, guidelines, and recommendations** to Indian regulators, industry, and trade bodies for harmonization.
6. **To safeguard Indian interests** (export competitiveness, consumer protection, traditional food heritage, and agricultural diversity) at the international negotiating table.
7. **To serve as the institutional bridge** between international Codex outcomes and Indian domestic regulation (FSSAI standards, BIS standards, APEDA/MPEDA regulations, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Core 'Why':** Food is global trade. Codex standards influence **WTO/SPS Agreement** disputes. If India does not actively participate in setting Codex standards, it will be forced to *accept* standards set by others — which may be unfavorable to Indian exports (basmati rice, spices, tea, fish products, ayurvedic foods, etc.). NCCI is the *defense mechanism* of Indian trade interests in the global food arena.

## 2.2 Composition of NCCI (The "WHO" Behind the Committee)

The NCCI is a **multi-stakeholder body** deliberately structured to include representation from every constituency that has a stake in Indian food policy. The composition is as follows:

| S.No. | Member | Role / Stake Represented |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Director General of Health Services (DGHS)** | **Chairperson** — Government Health Authority |
| 2 | Chairperson, **FSSAI** | Convener / Member — Food Regulator |
| 3 | Representatives from **Ministry of Health & Family Welfare** | Government Policy |
| 4 | Representatives from **Ministry of Food Processing Industries (MoFPI)** | Food Industry Policy |
| 5 | Representatives from **Ministry of Commerce / DGFT** | Trade & Export Policy |
| 6 | Representatives from **Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare** | Agricultural Stakeholders |
| 7 | Representatives from **Department of Animal Husbandry & Dairying** | Dairy & Animal Products |
| 8 | Representatives from **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)** | National Standards Body |
| 9 | Representatives from **APEDA** | Agricultural & Processed Food Export |
| 10 | Representatives from **MPEDA** | Marine Product Export |
| 11 | Representatives from **Tea Board, Coffee Board, Spices Board** | Commodity-Specific Boards |
| 12 | Representatives from **ICAR / CFTRI / IIP / NIN** | Scientific Research Institutions |
| 13 | Representatives from **Industry Associations** (CII, FICCI, ASSOCHAM, AIFPA) | Industrial Stakeholders |
| 14 | Representatives from **Consumer Organizations** | Civil Society & Consumer Voice |
| 15 | Representatives from **State Governments** | Sub-National Federalism |
| 16 | Representatives from **IITs and Agricultural Universities** | Academic & Technical Expertise |
| 17 | **Secretary, NCCI (from FSSAI)** | Administrative Coordination |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Eye:** A common mistake students make is to write "FSSAI is the chairman" or "FSSAI runs NCCI." The correct answer is **DGHS is the Chairperson**, while **FSSAI provides the secretariat and the operational backbone**. This distinction is a **favourite 1-mark trap question** in KTU exams.

## 2.3 The Shadow Committee System (The "HOW" Behind Indian Codex Participation)

This is the **operational heart** of the NCCI and the most important high-yield topic for KTU Module 3.

### 2.3.1 What is a Shadow Committee?

A **Shadow Committee** is a **technical subject-specific national committee** constituted by the NCCI that **mirrors** (i.e., shadows) an **international Codex Subject Committee** of the Codex Alimentarius Commission. Each Shadow Committee has the same area of expertise and *roughly the same name and scope* as its international counterpart.

### 2.3.2 Why "Shadow"?

Because these committees operate in **parallel to (in the shadow of)** the international Codex Committees. They:
- Track the agenda of the international Codex Committee.
- Study draft Codex standards circulated by the Codex Secretariat.
- Conduct **technical consultations** with Indian experts.
- Formulate **India's position paper** (called the "Country Position") on each agenda item.
- Recommend to NCCI the **Indian delegation's voting line** for the international meeting.

### 2.3.3 Important Shadow Committees of India (KTU High-Yield List)

| S.No. | Shadow Committee | Corresponding International Codex Committee |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Shadow Committee on Food Additives & Contaminants | Codex Committee on Food Additives (CCFA) |
| 2 | Shadow Committee on Food Hygiene | Codex Committee on Food Hygiene (CCFH) |
| 3 | Shadow Committee on Pesticide Residues | Codex Committee on Pesticide Residues (CCPR) |
| 4 | Shadow Committee on Food Labelling | Codex Committee on Food Labelling (CCFL) |
| 5 | Shadow Committee on Nutrition & Foods for Special Dietary Uses | Codex Committee on Nutrition & Foods for Special Dietary Uses (CCNFSDU) |
| 6 | Shadow Committee on Methods of Analysis & Sampling | Codex Committee on Methods of Analysis & Sampling (CCMAS) |
| 7 | Shadow Committee on Residues of Veterinary Drugs in Foods | Codex Committee on Residues of Veterinary Drugs in Foods (CCRVDF) |
| 8 | Shadow Committee on Fresh Fruits & Vegetables | Codex Committee on Fresh Fruits & Vegetables (CCFFV) |
| 9 | Shadow Committee on Fats & Oils | Codex Committee on Fats & Oils (CCFO) |
| 10 | Shadow Committee on Spices & Culinary Herbs | Codex Committee on Spices & Culinary Herbs (CCSCH) |
| 11 | Shadow Committee on Fish & Fishery Products | Codex Committee on Fish & Fishery Products (CCFFP) |
| 12 | Shadow Committee on Milk & Milk Products | Codex Committee on Milk & Milk Products (CCMMP) |
| 13 | Shadow Committee on Cereals, Pulses & Legumes | Codex Committee on Cereals, Pulses & Legumes (CCCPL) |
| 14 | Shadow Committee on Food Import & Export Inspection & Certification Systems | Codex Committee on Food Import & Export (CCFICS) |
| 15 | Shadow Committee on Contaminants in Foods | Codex Committee on Contaminants in Foods (CCCF) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **India hosts the Codex Committee on Spices & Culinary Herbs (CCSCH)** — this is a matter of national pride and a great KTU exam point. The permanent secretariat of CCSCH is hosted by **India (Kochi/Spices Board)**. Hence the Shadow Committee on Spices & Culinary Herbs is uniquely significant for India.

### 2.3.4 Working of a Shadow Committee (Step-by-Step)

1. **CAC issues provisional agenda** for an upcoming Codex Committee meeting (e.g., CCFA).
