# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1: Define FSSAI and state its major activities for ensuring food safety in India. **[3 Marks]** [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

**Model Answer:**

FSSAI is the **Food Safety and Standards Authority of India**, established under the **FSS Act, 2006**, operating under the **Ministry of Health and Family Welfare, Government of India**. It is the **single apex body** responsible for ensuring food safety in India.

Its **major activities for ensuring food safety** are:

1. **Framing science-based food standards** for all food categories, additives, contaminants, and labelling under Section 16 and 23 of the FSS Act.
2. **Issuing Licenses and Registration** to all Food Business Operators (FBOs) through the FoSCoS digital platform under Section 31.
3. **Enforcement and Surveillance** through Food Safety Officers (FSOs) and Designated Officers (DOs) under Sections 38, 39.
4. **Testing of food samples** through a nationwide network of NABL-accredited labs and the National Food Laboratory, Ghaziabad under Section 43.
5. **Training and capacity building** through FoSTaC and the Eat Right India movement under Section 16(3)(n).
6. **International cooperation** through the Codex Alimentarius Commission and bilateral MoUs under Section 22(2)(e).

---

### Question 2: What is the role of a Food Safety Officer (FSO) under the FSS Act, 2006? **[3 Marks]** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

A **Food Safety Officer (FSO)** is a designated government official under Section 37 of the FSS Act, 2006, with the following key roles:

1. **Inspection Powers (Sec 38):** Can enter, inspect, and search any premise where food is manufactured, stored, sold, or exhibited at any reasonable time.
2. **Sampling Authority (Sec 39):** Authorized to draw food samples in three parts — for the Food Analyst, the Designated Officer, and the FBO.
3. **Seizure and Detention:** Can seize food articles suspected to be unsafe, non-compliant, or sub-standard.
4. **Initiation of Prosecution:** Files complaints before the Adjudicating Officer or Magistrate based on lab reports.
5. **Awareness Role:** Educates FBOs about compliance requirements during inspections.

The FSO is **distinct from the Designated Officer**, who is the **adjudicating authority** and not authorized to conduct inspections.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, with internal sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. **Students answer ONE full question** (either Q A or Q B) in the exam.

---

### 📌 QUESTION A (14 Marks): Comprehensive Coverage [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

**(a) Discuss in detail the various activities undertaken by FSSAI for ensuring food safety in India. Explain the standards-setting and licensing framework with relevant sections of the FSS Act. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

The FSSAI undertakes a **comprehensive multi-pillar framework** of activities to ensure food safety across the entire food value chain in India.

**(i) Standards-Setting Activities (Sec 16 & 23):**

FSSAI is the **sole national authority** to frame science-based food standards, replacing the multiplicity of pre-2006 standards. The standards are notified through:
- **FSS (Food Products Standards and Food Additives) Regulations, 2011** (recently revamped in 2024 as **Food Safety and Standards (Food Products Standards and Food Additives) First Amendment Regulations, 2024**).
- **FSS (Contaminants, Toxins and Residues) Regulations, 2011.**
- **FSS (Labelling and Display) Regulations, 2020.**
- **FSS (Packaging) Regulations, 2018.**
- **FSS (Fortification of Foods) Regulations, 2018** (for staples like rice, wheat flour, salt, oil, milk).

The standards cover:
- **Product identity** (composition, essential characteristics).
- **Additive limits** (permitted colours, preservatives, antioxidants).
- **Contaminant thresholds** (heavy metals, mycotoxins, pesticides).
- **Microbiological criteria** (pathogen limits).
- **Labelling requirements** (mandatory declarations: name, ingredients, FSSAI logo, license number, veg/non-veg symbol, allergens, FBO details).

Scientific Panels comprising **subject-matter experts** (food technologists, microbiologists, toxicologists, nutritionists) review and revise these standards periodically.

**(ii) Licensing and Registration Framework (Sec 31):**

A **mandatory tiered licensing regime** is enforced:

| Category | Threshold | Requirement | Validity | Fee |
|----------|-----------|-------------|----------|-----|
| **Petty FBO** | ≤ ₹12 Lakh turnover | Registration (Form A) | 1–5 years | ₹100–500 |
| **State License** | > ₹12 Lakh turnover (intrastate) | License (Form B) | 1–5 years | ₹2,000–7,500 |
| **Central License** | Multi-state operations, large turnover > ₹20 cr, import, e-commerce | Central License | 1–5 years | ₹7,500–25,000 |

Licensing is processed through the **FoSCoS (Food Safety Compliance System)** digital platform launched in 2021. The Designated Officer must dispose of applications within **60 days**, failing which the application is deemed approved (subject to certain conditions). Licenses are **non-transferable** and can be **suspended or cancelled** under Sec 32 for non-compliance.

**Conclusion:** Standards and licensing form the **legal foundation** of food safety regulation. The two are **complementary** — standards define *what is safe*; licensing ensures *who is authorized to produce it*.

---

**(b) Describe the enforcement, surveillance, and laboratory network activities of FSSAI. Explain the legal provisions for prosecution and penalties. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Enforcement and Surveillance Activities:**

The **operational backbone** of FSSAI is the field-level enforcement performed by **Food Safety Officers (FSOs)** appointed under Sec 37, working under the supervision of **Designated Officers (DOs)** at the district level.

Key enforcement functions:
- **Inspections (Sec 38):** FSOs inspect FBO premises, equipment, raw materials, finished products, and records.
- **Sampling (Sec 39):** Three-part sample is drawn and sealed in tamper-proof manner.
- **Seizure and Detention (Sec 38):** Food articles suspected to be unsafe can be seized.
- **Surveillance Drives:** FSSAI conducts **periodic national surveys** (e.g., National Milk Quality Survey 2022 with 49,000+ samples) and **Pan-India Enforcement Drives** on specific commodities (milk, edible oil, spices, sweets during festivals).
- **Special Initiatives:** The **Food Safety Mitra (FSM)** scheme (2019) engages **private food safety auditors** to extend FSSAI's reach to small and medium FBOs.

**(ii) Laboratory Network (Sec 43–45):**

Food samples are tested in a **three-tier laboratory network**:
1. **National Food Laboratory (NFL), Ghaziabad** — primary reference lab, also acts as **referral lab** for disputed samples.
2. **State Food Testing Laboratories** — one or more in each state.
3. **NABL-accredited Private Laboratories** — empanelled by FSSAI.

The **Food Analyst** must issue reports within **14 days** of sample receipt (Sec 43(3)). Reports are admissible as evidence (Sec 46). The FBO can request **re-testing** at a referral lab within **30 days** of receiving the report.

**(iii) Prosecution and Penalties (Sec 42, 46, 47, 59):**

- **Prosecution (Sec 42):** Filed before the **Adjudicating Officer** (a State-level quasi-judicial authority) or directly before a Magistrate.
- **Penalties (Sec 47):** Ranging from **₹1 Lakh to ₹10 Lakh** based on the gravity and recurrence of non-compliance.
- **Imprisonment (Sec 59):** For **fatal adulteration** (food causing death), imprisonment of **6 months to life** can be imposed.
- **Compounding of Offences (Sec 60):** Minor first-time offences can be compounded with payment of a prescribed fee.
- **Adjudicating Officer Decision:** Must be rendered within **90 days** of hearing.

**Real-World Case:** The 2023 **lead contamination in rice** detected by FSSAI triggered nationwide product recalls under Sec 28, demonstrating the **end-to-end enforcement pipeline** in action.

**Conclusion:** The synergy of **inspection, sampling, testing, prosecution, and recall** ensures that FSSAI's regulatory activities have **real teeth** and **measurable public health impact**.

---

### 📌 QUESTION B (14 Marks): Alternate Choice [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a) Explain the major flagship initiatives of FSSAI under its training, awareness, and capacity-building activities. Discuss the Eat Right India movement in detail. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

FSSAI recognizes that **regulation alone is insufficient** to ensure food safety. Accordingly, it invests substantially in **training, awareness, and capacity-building** under Sec 16(3)(n). These activities target **all stakeholders** — FBOs, consumers, students, and street food vendors.

**(i) Flagship Initiatives:**

1. **Eat Right India Movement (2018):**
   - **Vision:** Transform the food ecosystem through three pillars — **Eat Safe, Eat Healthy, Eat Sustainable**.
   - **Logo:** The green-coloured "Eat Right" mark is awarded to compliant FBOs.
   - **Reach:** Over **1,500 towns** and **25,000+ schools** have been covered.
   - **Hygiene Rating:** A 0–5 star rating system has been rolled out for restaurants, cafeterias, and street food vendors.

2. **Food Safety Training and Certification (FoSTaC) [2017]:**
   - **Mandatory for all food handlers** in licensed FBOs.
   - **Advanced Level 2 & 3** certifications are required for supervisors and proprietors.
   - Conducted by **FSSAI-empanelled training partners (ETPs)**.

3. **Eat Right Campus (2018):**
   - Audits and certifies **educational institutions, workplaces, and hospitals** for food safety.
   - Has covered over **5,000 campuses** by 2024.

4. **CHeFt (Clean & Healthy Food) Schools:**
   - Focuses on **mid-day meal safety** in government schools.
   - Includes training of **anganwadi workers** and **kitchen staff**.

5. **Clean Street Food Hubs (2021):   **
   - Pilots undertaken in **Varanasi, Ujjain, and Indore** under the Smart Cities Mission.
   - 25+ hubs upgraded with **running water, waste bins, utensil washing stations, and hygiene training**.

6. **Food Safety Magic Box (2019):**
   - A portable testing kit distributed to **anganwadi workers** to detect common adulterants in milk, oil, sugar, and salt.
   - Contains rapid tests for urea, starch, formalin, soap, and detergent.

7. **National Food Safety Index (NFSI) [2018-19 onwards]:**
   - Ranks states on five parameters: **Human Resources, Compliance, Food Testing Infrastructure, Surveillance, and Training**.
   - Promotes **competitive federalism** in food safety governance.

**(ii) Strategic Impact of Eat Right India:**

- **Behavioural Change:** Reaches millions through social media campaigns (e.g., **#EatRightIndia** on Twitter/Instagram).
- **Consumer Empowerment:** Labels like "**High in Salt**" and "**High in Sugar**" inform consumers under the **FSS (Labelling and Display) Regulations, 2020**.
- **Public-Private Partnership:** FSSAI partners with **industry bodies** (CII, FICCI, ASSOCHAM) and **NGOs** (Consumer Voice) for outreach.
- **Scientific Base:** Initiatives are guided by **independent research** and **consumer perception studies**.

**Conclusion:** The training and awareness pillar represents FSSAI's **proactive, preventive approach** — moving from mere enforcement to **building a culture of food safety**.

---

**(b) Discuss the role of FSSAI in international food safety cooperation and Codex Alimentarius activities. Explain the impact on India's food trade. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) FSSAI as the National Codex Contact Point:**

FSSAI is the **official National Codex Contact Point (NCCP)** of India, representing the country in the **Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC)** — the joint FAO/WO body established in 1963 to develop harmonized international food standards, guidelines, and codes of practice.

FSSAI's international activities derive authority from **Sec 22(2)(e)** of the FSS Act, 2006, which empowers it to *"co-operate and promote cooperation with international organizations, agencies, and bodies involved in food safety matters."*

**(ii) Specific International Engagements:**

1. **Codex Committee on Spices and Culinary Herbs (CCSCH):** India hosts this committee in **Kochi**, giving it a **unique leadership role** in global spice trade standards.
2. **Codex Committee on Contaminants in Food (CCCF):** Active Indian participation in setting limits for lead, cadmium, and aflatoxins.
3. **Codex Committee on Food Additives (CCFA):** India contributes to international additive safety assessments.
4. **Codex Committee on Food Hygiene (CCFH):** Engagement in international food hygiene standards.
5. **Codex Committee on Pesticide Residues (CCPR):** Coordination with ICAR and IARI for data on Indian crops.

**(iii) Bilateral and Multilateral Cooperation:**

FSSAI has signed **Memoranda of Understanding (MoUs)** with several global food safety agencies:
- **USDA-FSIS (USA)**
- **EFSA (European Union)**
- **FSA (United Kingdom)**
- **FSANZ (Australia and New Zealand)**
- **GACC (General Administration of Customs of China)**
- **NIFDS (South Korea)**

These MoUs facilitate:
- **Mutual recognition of standards** (where feasible).
- **Joint research** on food safety issues.
- **Capacity building exchanges.**
- **Information sharing** on contaminants, allergens, and recalls.

**(iv) WTO/SPS Compliance:**

India, as a **WTO member**, must align its food standards with the **Agreement on Sanitary and Phytosanitary Measures (SPS Agreement)** and the **Agreement on Technical Barriers to Trade (TBT Agreement)**. FSSAI:
- Issues **SPS/TBT notifications** to the WTO on proposed Indian standards.
- Defends Indian standards against **trade challenges** from exporting countries.
- Ensures that imported food meets Indian standards and vice versa.

**(v) Impact on India's Food Trade:**

- **Export Facilitation:** The FSS (Recognition and Notification of Testing Laboratories) Regulations, 2018 designate labs for **pre-export testing** of agricultural and processed foods, helping Indian exporters meet destination-country requirements.
- **Import Safety:** FSSAI's **Authorized Officer** at ports tests imported food for compliance with Indian standards.
- **Brand India:** Strong international standards adherence strengthens the **"Brand India"** image in global food markets.
- **Spice Trade:** India's role as host of CCSCH gives it **leadership** in setting global standards for turmeric, cardamom, cumin, pepper, and other spices.

**Conclusion:** FSSAI's international activities are not merely diplomatic; they are **strategic economic levers** that protect India's **food trade interests**, ensure **non-tariff barrier compliance**, and uphold the **sovereignty of Indian food standards**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Conflating FSSAI with FSS Act:** FSSAI is the **body**; the FSS Act is the **law**. Saying "FSSAI banned xyz" is incorrect — the correct phrasing is "**As per the FSS Act, 2006** read with FSSAI regulations, xyz is prohibited."
> 2. **Omitting Section Numbers:** KTU answers **must cite Section numbers** (Sec 16, Sec 31, Sec 38, etc.) for full marks. A correct answer without statutory reference loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Mixing Pre-2006 Acts:** Avoid referencing the **Prevention of Food Adulteration Act (PFA, 1954)** or **Fruit Products Order (FPO, 1955)** as the current law. These were **subsumed** under the FSS Act, 2006.
> 4. **Confusing FSO and DO:** The FSO inspects and samples; the DO adjudicates. Stating that the FSO "adjudicates" or the DO "inspects" is a **factual error** that costs marks.
> 5. **Ignoring Lab Network:** When discussing testing, students often forget to mention the **three-tier lab network** (National, State, Private) and the **14-day statutory timeline** under Sec 43(3).
> 6. **No mention of FoSCoS:** Always mention the **FoSCoS digital platform** when discussing licensing — it is the **current operational reality** and a favourite exam touchpoint.
> 7. **Skipping Recall Provision:** Many students forget Sec 28 (product recall) when discussing enforcement — this is a **high-yield, frequently tested provision**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — FSSAI Activities for Ensuring Food Safety in India**

✅ FSSAI = **Food Safety and Standards Authority of India**, established under **FSS Act, 2006**, operationalized **2011**, HQ = **New Delhi**, under **MoHFW**.

✅ **Six Pillars of Activity:**
   1. **Standards Setting** (Sec 16, 23) — FSS (FPS & FA) Regulations 2011; FSS (Contaminants) Reg 2011; FSS (Labelling) Reg 2020.
   2. **Licensing & Registration** (Sec 31) — Petty FBO ≤ ₹12 Lakh; State License for intrastate; Central License for multistate/import.
   3. **Enforcement & Surveillance** (Sec 27, 28, 38, 39) — FSO inspects, samples, seizes; DO adjudicates.
   4. **Testing & Lab Network** (Sec 43, 44, 45) — 14-day report timeline; 30-day re-test window; 6-month report validity.
   5. **Training & Awareness** (Sec 16(3)(n)) — Eat Right India, FoSTaC, CHeFt, Hygiene Rating (0–5 stars).
   6. **International Cooperation** (Sec 22(2)(e)) — Codex NCCP; CCSCH hosted in Kochi; WTO/SPS compliance.

✅ **Key Statutory Provisions to Memorize:**
   - Sec 16: FSSAI functions
   - Sec 23: Notification of standards
   - Sec 24: Prohibition of unsafe food
   - Sec 27: FBO responsibilities
   - Sec 28: Product recall
   - Sec 31: Licensing & Registration
   - Sec 32: Suspension/cancellation
   - Sec 37–39: FSO powers
   - Sec 43: Food Analyst role
   - Sec 46: Report evidence
   - Sec 47: Penalties (₹1L–₹10L)
   - Sec 59: Fatal adulteration imprisonment
   - Sec 68: Appellate Tribunal

✅ **Numerical Values to Remember:**
   - Petty FBO threshold: **₹12 Lakh**
   - Central License threshold: **₹20 crore**
   - Sample parts: **3**
   - Lab report: **14 days**
   - Re-test: **30 days**
   - Report validity: **6 months**
   - Penalty: **₹1L to ₹10L**
   - Imprisonment (fatal): **6 months to life**
   - Hygiene rating scale: **0 to 5 stars**
   - FSSAI operational year: **2011**

✅ **Flagship Initiatives to Remember:**
   - Eat Right India (2018)
   - FoSTaC (2017)
   - Eat Right Campus (2018)
   - Clean Street Food Hubs (2021)
   - Food Safety Magic Box (2019)
   - NFSI ranking (2018-19)
   - National Milk Quality Survey (2022) — 49,200 samples
   - Food Safety Mitra (2019)

✅ **Digital Platforms:**
   - **FoSCoS** (Food Safety Compliance System) — licensing portal
   - **FSSAI Website** — for consumer complaints, standards, recall notices
   - **Eat Right India Mobile App** — consumer engagement

✅ **International Engagements:**
   - Codex Alimentarius (1963)
   - CCSCH in Kochi
   - SPS/TBT compliance
   - Bilateral MoUs with USDA, EFSA, FSANZ, FSA

✅ **Key Differentiators:**
   - FSO ≠ DO
   - FSSAI ≠ FSS Act
   - Central License ≠ State License ≠ Registration
   - National Food Lab ≠ State Food Lab ≠ Referral Lab
   - Standards ≠ Regulations

---

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Other Agencies Involved in Regulating Food Business** refers to the constellation of national, international, governmental, and non-governmental organizations (other than the primary regulator **FSSAI**) that establish, monitor, enforce, and harmonize the legal, scientific, quality, safety, and trade standards governing the production, processing, packaging, labeling, distribution, and sale of food products. These agencies operate across multiple jurisdictions — domestic (BIS, AGMARK, APEDA, MPEDA, EIC), international (Codex Alimentarius, ISO, WHO/FAO, WTO), and product-specific commodity boards (Tea Board, Coffee Board, Spices Board, NDDB).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCFTT503 – Module 3):**
> "Other agencies involved in regulating food business shall include the Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS), Agricultural Marketing Standards (AGMARK), Export Inspection Council (EIC), Marine Products Export Development Authority (MPEDA), Agricultural and Processed Food Products Export Development Authority (APEDA), Codex Alimentarius Commission, International Organization for Standardization (ISO), and product-specific Commodity Boards under the Ministry of Commerce and Ministry of Food Processing Industries."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Real-World Intuition

Imagine a food business is like a **multi-lane highway system**:
- **FSSAI** is the **traffic police** — the primary enforcer who manages the overall flow of vehicles (food products) on Indian roads.
- **Other agencies** are the **road construction engineers, signboard designers, lane painters, and international driving licence authorities** — each with a specific niche role. The road builder (BIS) ensures lanes are standard width, the signboard maker (AGMARK) puts quality grade labels, the international licence agency (Codex/ISO) ensures your vehicle can cross borders, and the export highway patrol (APEDA/MPEDA/EIC) manages the special express lanes for international trade.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why multiple agencies?** Food is a multi-dimensional commodity — it touches agriculture, health, trade, industry, and consumer rights. A single regulator cannot specialize in all dimensions, hence the **matrix of overlapping but complementary regulatory bodies**, each with jurisdiction defined by product, function, geography, or market.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Codex Alimentarius pesticide MRL default limit**: 0.01 mg/kg (for pesticides without explicit MRL)
- **BIS standard shelf-life guarantee threshold**: typically **180 days minimum** for packaged food under IS 14543 (mineral water)
- **AGMARK grading tiers**: **3 grades** (Special, Grade 1, Grade 2 — based on quality parameters)
- **ISO certification cycle**: **3 years** (with surveillance audits every 12 months)

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick — "BAME-IC" for Indian agencies:** **B**IS, **A**GMARK, **M**PEDA, **E**IC, **I**SO (interlink), **C**odex (international). Mnemonic: *BAME-IC* → "BAME in Codex" — like saying a regulatory saint codifying the world of food.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 National Regulatory Agencies (Indian)

### 2.1.1 Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)

**BIS** is the **National Standards Body of India**, established under the **Bureau of Indian Standards Act, 1986** (replacing the older ISI Act, 1952). It operates under the **Ministry of Consumer Affairs, Food and Public Distribution**.

**Key Functions:**
- Formulation of **Indian Standards (IS codes)** for food products, processing, packaging, and testing
- Operates the **ISI Mark** certification scheme (compulsory for certain products under the BIS Act)
- Formulates standards under the **Food Safety and Standards (Food Products Standards and Food Additives) Regulations, 2011** in conjunction with FSSAI
- Harmonizes Indian standards with international standards (ISO, Codex)

**Major BIS Food Standards (Examples):**

| IS Code | Product / Parameter |
|:---:|:---|
| **IS 14543** | Packaged Drinking Water |
| **IS 14544** | Mineral Water |
| **IS 2711** | Spices and Condiments |
| **IS 1166** | Condensed Milk |
| **IS 4072** | Tapioca Sago |
| **IS 5832** | Canned Mango Pulp |
| **IS 6242** | Canned Mushrooms |
| **IS 1921** | Canned Pineapple |

### 2.1.2 Agricultural Marketing Standards (AGMARK)

**AGMARK** is the certification mark for agricultural produce in India, administered by the **Directorate of Marketing and Inspection (DMI)** under the **Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers Welfare**. It is governed by the **Agricultural Produce (Grading and Marking) Act, 1937** (amended in 1986).

**Key Functions:**
- Voluntary quality certification for agricultural commodities
- **Grading** of produce into quality classes (Special, Grade 1, Grade 2)
- Quality parameters include: physical, chemical, microbiological, and organoleptic attributes
- Pre-shipment inspection for export consignments

**AGMARK-graded commodities** include honey, ghee, butter, spices, wheat, rice, pulses, oils, fruits, vegetables, and eggs.

### 2.1.3 Export Inspection Council (EIC)

The **Export Inspection Council (EIC)** was established under **Section 3 of the Export (Quality Control and Inspection) Act, 1963**. It is the apex body for ensuring quality and pre-shipment inspection of export commodities.

**Key Functions:**
- Compulsory pre-shipment inspection for notified commodities
- Certification of export consignments for conformity with international standards
- Issue of **Certificate of Origin** and **Health Certificates**
- Recognition of inspection agencies and laboratories
- Implementation of **Export Quality Monitoring** for various food categories

### 2.1.4 APEDA (Agricultural and Processed Food Products Export Development Authority)

Established under the **APEDA Act, 1985**, APEDA functions under the **Ministry of Commerce and Industry**.

**Mandated Products:** Fruits, vegetables, meat, poultry, dairy, confectionery, bakery, alcoholic/non-alcoholic beverages, groundnuts, pickles, papad, jaggery, and processed fruits.

**Key Functions:**
- Registration of exporters
- Quality certification of scheduled products
- Implementation of **Traceability Systems** (e.g., **Peanut.Net**, **HortiNet**)
- Compliance with **HACCP / ISO 22000** for export units
- **Financial assistance schemes** for export infrastructure

### 2.1.5 MPEDA (Marine Products Export Development Authority)

Established under the **MPEDA Act, 1972**, MPEDA regulates the export of marine products.

**Key Functions:**
- Registration of processing plants and exporters
- **Compulsiary inspection of seafood processing establishments** for EU, USA, Japan, and other stringent markets
- Implementation of **HACCP-based quality control**
- The **Pre-Shipment Inspection** for marine products
- Coordination with **Coastal Aquaculture Authority (CAA)** for aquaculture
- **Registration of Antibiotic Residue Testing Labs**

### 2.1.6 Product-Specific Commodity Boards

| Board | Established | Product |
|:---|:---:|:---|
| **Tea Board** | Tea Act, 1953 | Tea |
| **Coffee Board** | Coffee Act, 1942 | Coffee |
| **Spices Board** | Spices Board Act, 1986 | Spices (52 spices) |
| **Rubber Board** | Rubber Act, 1947 | Natural Rubber |
| **Tobacco Board** | Tobacco Board Act, 1975 | Tobacco |
| **Coir Board** | Coir Industry Act, 1953 | Coir |
| **National Dairy Development Board (NDDB)** | NDDB Act, 1987 | Dairy |
| **Coconut Development Board (CDB)** | CDB Act, 1979 | Coconut |
| **National Horticulture Board (NHB)** | NHB Act, 1984 | Horticulture |
| **National Agricultural Cooperative Marketing Federation (NAFED)** | Multi-State Cooperative Act | Cooperative marketing |

## 2.2 International Regulatory Agencies

### 2.2.1 Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC)

Established in **1963** by the **FAO (Food and Agriculture Organization)** and **WHO (World Health Organization)**, Codex is the single most important international reference for food standards, guidelines, and codes of practice.

**Key Documents:**
- **Codex General Standard for Food Additives (GSFA)** — Codex Stan 192-1995
- **Codex General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins (GSCT)**
- **Codex MRLs** for pesticides, veterinary drugs, and food additives
- **Codex Code of Practice on Food Hygiene**
- **Codex Principles for the Application of Risk Analysis**

**Key Codex Committees:**
- **CCFA** — Committee on Food Additives
- **CCRVDF** — Committee on Residues of Veterinary Drugs in Foods
- **CCFH** — Committee on Food Hygiene
- **CCFICS** — Committee on Food Import and Export Inspection and Certification Systems
- **CCNFSDU** — Committee on Nutrition and Foods for Special Dietary Uses

### 2.2.2 International Organization for Standardization (ISO)

ISO is a non-governmental international body that develops **voluntary, consensus-based standards**. Of relevance to food:

- **ISO 22000** — Food Safety Management System
- **ISO 9001** — Quality Management System
- **ISO 22005** — Traceability in the feed and food chain
- **ISO 17025** — Competence of testing and calibration laboratories
- **ISO 14001** — Environmental Management
- **ISO 45001** — Occupational Health and Safety

> [!NOTE]
> **FSSC 22000** (Food Safety System Certification 22000) is a GFSI-recognized certification based on ISO 22000, ISO 22002-1, and additional ISO/TS requirements.

### 2.2.3 GFSI (Global Food Safety Initiative)

Founded in **2000** by the **Consumer Goods Forum (CGF)**, GFSI is a private industry initiative that benchmarks food safety standards globally. Recognized schemes include:
- **FSSC 22000**
- **BRCGS (Brand Reputation Compliance Global Standards)**
- **SQF (Safe Quality Food)**
- **IFS (International Featured Standards)**

### 2.2.4 WTO Agreements (SPS and TBT)

The **World Trade Organization (WTO)**, established in **1995** (successor to GATT 1947), enforces two key agreements relevant to food:

- **SPS Agreement (Agreement on the Application of Sanitary and Phytosanitary Measures, 1995):** Allows member countries to set food safety standards, but they must be **science-based**, **non-discriminatory**, and harmonized with **Codex standards**.
- **TBT Agreement (Agreement on Technical Barriers to Trade, 1995):** Covers technical regulations, voluntary standards, and conformity assessment procedures that may have significant impact on trade.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SPS Hierarchy:** If a country sets an MRL higher than Codex, it must be supported by **scientific risk assessment** (Article 5 of SPS Agreement).

### 2.2.5 Other International Bodies

| Organization | Focus |
|:---|:---|
| **WHO (World Health Organization)** | Public health; 5 keys to safer food; global food safety strategy |
| **FAO (Food and Agriculture Organization)** | Food security, food loss reduction |
| **EFSA (European Food Safety Authority)** | EU scientific risk assessment |
| **USFDA (United States Food and Drug Administration)** | US food safety regulator |
| **USDA/FSIS (Food Safety and Inspection Service)** | US meat, poultry, egg products |
| **RASFF (Rapid Alert System for Food and Feed)** | EU real-time food safety alert system |
| **OECD** | Pesticide MRLs, chemical testing guidelines |
| **JECFA (Joint FAO/WHO Expert Committee on Food Additives)** | Food additive safety evaluation |
| **JMPR (Joint Meeting on Pesticide Residues)** | Pesticide MRL establishment |
| **OIE/WOAH** (now WOAH) | Animal health; zoonotic diseases |
| **GACC (General Administration of Customs of China)** | Chinese food import regulations |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept / Parameter | Formula / Standard | Unit / Application |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **AGMARK Grade Tier Index** | $G_i = \dfrac{\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot q_{ij}}{100}$ | $G_i$ = grade index, $w_j$ = weight of parameter $j$, $q_{ij}$ = quality score |
| **Statistical Sampling — Sample Size (for attribute)** | $n = \dfrac{Z^2 \cdot p(1-p)}{E^2}$ | $n$ = sample size, $Z$ = z-score (1.96 for 95% CL), $p$ = proportion, $E$ = margin of error |
| **MRL (Maximum Residue Limit) Risk** | $MRL = \dfrac{ADI \times BW}{F \times TMDI}$ | $ADI$ = Acceptable Daily Intake (mg/kg bw), $BW$ = body weight (kg), $F$ = food consumption (kg/day), $TMDI$ = Theoretical Maximum Daily Intake |
| **HACCP CCP Decision Tree** | $Q_1 \to Q_2 \to Q_3 \to Q_4$ | Determines if step is Critical Control Point |
| **Lot Acceptance (c=0 plan)** | $P_a = (1-p)^n$ | $P_a$ = probability of acceptance, $p$ = defect rate, $n$ = sample size |
| **ISO 22000 PDCA Loop** | Plan–Do–Check–Act | Continuous improvement in FSMS |
| **Risk = Hazard × Probability × Severity** | $R = H \times P \times S$ | Qualitative risk assessment |
| **Acceptance Number (k)** | $k = \dfrac{d \cdot n - c}{\sqrt{n \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}}$ | $d$ = sample mean, $c$ = acceptance limit |
| **Traceability Recovery Time** | $T_{RT} = t_{recall} + t_{trace}$ | Measures supply-chain agility |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of These Regulatory Frameworks

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** For a food technologist, the integration of these agencies in the **export value chain** is critical. For example, a seafood exporter from Kerala must simultaneously comply with:
> 1. **FSSAI** (domestic sale)
> 2. **MPEDA** (export registration)
> 3. **EIC** (pre-shipment inspection)
> 4. **BIS** (IS standards)
> 5. **EU/USFDA** (importing country regulations)
> 6. **Codex** (SPS dispute settlement at WTO)
>
> This matrix compliance is what the KTU Module 3 expects students to visualize as a **regulatory ecosystem**, not isolated silos.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown & Compliance Matrix

## 3.1 Hierarchical Classification of Regulatory Agencies

The classification can be broken down logically using the following analytical decision tree:

**Step 1:** Identify the **regulatory scope**
- Domestic → FSSAI + national commodity boards
- Export → APEDA, MPEDA, EIC + destination country regulations
- International trade disputes → WTO (SPS/TBT)
- Standards formulation → BIS (national), ISO (international)
- Scientific reference → Codex, JECFA, JMPR
- Quality certification marks → AGMARK, ISI, FSSAI logo, ISO marks

**Step 2:** Identify the **functional domain**
- Quality → BIS, AGMARK, ISO
- Safety → FSSAI, Codex, WHO
- Trade → WTO, APEDA, MPEDA, EIC
- Specific commodity → Tea Board, Coffee Board, Spices Board, NDDB

**Step 3:** Identify the **enforceability**
- **Statutory (compulsory):** FSSAI, EIC (for notified goods), MPEDA
- **Voluntary:** AGMARK, ISO, BRC, FSSC 22000
- **Reference (in WTO disputes):** Codex

## 3.2 Detailed Analytical Breakdown of Key Statutes

### 3.2.1 Export (Quality Control and Inspection) Act, 1963 — Detailed Logical Analysis

**Purpose:** To ensure that goods exported from India meet the quality, safety, and packaging requirements of importing countries.

**Step-by-step application logic:**

\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Notification} &\rightarrow \text{Government notifies specific commodities for compulsory inspection} \\
\text{Step 2: Registration} &\rightarrow \text{Exporter registers with EIC} \\
\text{Step 3: Sampling} &\rightarrow \text{Inspector draws samples using statistical plan} \\
\text{Step 4: Testing} &\rightarrow \text{Tests at EIC-approved laboratory} \\
\text{Step 5: Certification} &\rightarrow \text{Issue of Certificate of Inspection (CON)} \\
\text{Step 6: Pre-shipment approval} &\rightarrow \text{Goods cleared for export}
\end{aligned}

### 3.2.2 BIS Standard Adoption Process — Detailed Flow

\begin{aligned}
\text{Stage 1: Technical Committee} &\rightarrow \text{BIS forms TC with stakeholders (industry, govt, academia)} \\
\text{Stage 2: Draft Standard (WD)} &\rightarrow \text{Working Draft circulated} \\
\text{Stage 3: Committee Draft (CD)} &\rightarrow \text{Refined by sub-committee} \\
\text{Stage 4: Draft for Public Comment (DPC)} &\rightarrow \text{Public/stakeholder feedback (60 days)} \\
\text{Stage 5: Final Draft (FD)} &\rightarrow \text{Approved by technical committee} \\
\text{Stage 6: Indian Standard (IS)} &\rightarrow \text{Published and numbered (e.g., IS 14543:1998)}
\end{aligned}

### 3.2.3 AGMARK Grading Decision Logic

**Quality parameters considered for grading honey (as a model case):**

\begin{aligned}
\text{Moisture content} &\rightarrow m_{h2o} \leq 20\% \\
\text{Sucrose content} &\rightarrow S_{c} \leq 5\% \\
\text{HMF (Hydroxymethylfurfural)} &\rightarrow HMF \leq 80 \text{ mg/kg (special grade)} \\
\text{Acidity (as formic acid)} &\rightarrow A \leq 0.2\% \\
\text{Optical density (color)} &\rightarrow OD \geq 0.6 \\
\text{Pollen count} &\rightarrow P \geq 0.5 \times 10^6 \text{ per gram}
\end{aligned}

**Final Grade Determination:**

\begin{aligned}
G_{special} &: \text{All parameters pass premium thresholds} \\
G_1 &: \text{All parameters pass Grade 1 thresholds} \\
G_2 &: \text{Minimum threshold compliance}
\end{aligned}

## 3.3 AGMARK Sample Size Determination (Worked Example)

**Given:** Lot of **2,000 kg of honey** for AGMARK grading. Code requires **minimum 5 samples** from lots < 5,000 kg.

**Step 1:** Identify lot size

\begin{aligned}
L = 2000 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}

**Step 2:** Identify sampling frequency

\begin{aligned}
n = \max\left(5, \left\lceil \dfrac{L}{500} \right\rceil\right) = \max\left(5, \left\lceil 4 \right\rceil\right) = 5 \text{ samples}
\end{aligned}

**Step 3:** Random selection from **top, middle, bottom** of containers

\begin{aligned}
\text{Sample A} &: \text{Top layer} \\
\text{Sample B} &: \text{1/3 depth} \\
\text{Sample C} &: \text{Centre} \\
\text{Sample D} &: \text{2/3 depth} \\
\text{Sample E} &: \text{Bottom layer}
\end{aligned}

**Step 4:** Composite and test in **duplicates**

\begin{aligned}
\text{Composite sample mass} = n \times m_{unit} = 5 \times 200 \text{ g} = 1000 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}

**Step 5:** Lab analysis for moisture, HMF, sucrose, acidity, optical density

**Step 6:** Compare against AGMARK threshold table → assign grade

## 3.4 MRL Calculation under Codex Framework (Worked Example)

**Problem:** A pesticide has an **ADI = 0.005 mg/kg body weight/day**. Standard body weight = **60 kg**. Person consumes **0.3 kg/day** of tomatoes. Calculate the recommended MRL.

**Step 1:** Calculate Theoretical Maximum Daily Intake (TMDI)

\begin{aligned}
TMDI = ADI \times BW = 0.005 \times 60 = 0.30 \text{ mg/day}
\end{aligned}

**Step 2:** Apply consumption factor

\begin{aligned}
MRL = \dfrac{ADI \times BW}{F} = \dfrac{0.005 \times 60}{0.3} = 1.0 \text{ mg/kg of tomato}
\end{aligned}

**Step 3:** Compare with Codex MRL (if available) — adopt the lower (more stringent) value.

\begin{aligned}
MRL_{final} = \min(1.0, MRL_{Codex})
\end{aligned}

**Step 4:** Convert to ppm equivalent for labeling

\begin{aligned}
1 \text{ mg/kg} = 1 \text{ ppm}
\end{aligned}

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1: Define AGMARK. List any four commodities graded under AGMARK certification. [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

**AGMARK (Agricultural Marketing)** is a certification mark for agricultural produce in India, established under the **Agricultural Produce (Grading and Marking) Act, 1937** (amended 1986). It certifies that the product conforms to the quality standards laid down by the **Directorate of Marketing and Inspection (DMI)**, Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers Welfare.

**Four AGMARK-graded commodities:**
1. Honey
2. Ghee (clarified butter)
3. Spices (turmeric, chilli, coriander)
4. Pulses (moong, chana, urad)

(Any other valid commodities like wheat, rice, vegetable oils, eggs, etc., are also acceptable.)

---

### Question 2: What is the role of the Codex Alimentarius Commission in international food trade? [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam – December 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

The **Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC)**, jointly established by the **FAO** and **WHO** in 1963, is the foremost international food standards-setting body. Its roles include:

1. **Reference for WTO SPS Agreement:** Codex standards serve as the **benchmark for food safety in international trade disputes** under the WTO SPS Agreement. National standards deviating from Codex must be scientifically justified.
2. **Standard Harmonization:** Develops **maximum residue limits (MRLs) for pesticides, veterinary drugs; food additive specifications; and contaminant limits**, facilitating global trade harmonization.
3. **Code of Practices:** Issues codes of hygienic and technological practice (e.g., food hygiene, animal feeding) for member nations to adopt.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (Module Internal Choice — 14 Marks)

### Question A: [14 Marks]

**(a)** Explain the structure, functions, and key Acts governing **APEDA and MPEDA**. Compare their operational domains. **[7 Marks]**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

**APEDA (Agricultural and Processed Food Products Export Development Authority)**
- **Establishment:** APEDA Act, 1985
- **Ministry:** Ministry of Commerce and Industry
- **Scheduled Products:** Fruits, vegetables, meat, poultry, dairy, confectionery, bakery, biscuits, honey, jaggery, pickles, alcoholic/non-alcoholic beverages, herbal products, floriculture, and processed foods.
- **Functions:**
  1. Registration of exporters and issue of Registration-cum-Membership Certificate (RCMC)
  2. Quality certification of export consignments
  3. Implementation of **HACCP/ISO 22000** in processing units
  4. **Traceability systems** (Peanut.Net for groundnuts, HortiNet for horticulture, Farm to Fork traceability)
  5. Financial assistance schemes for exporters (infrastructure, cold chain, market development)
  6. Market promotion abroad through participation in international food fairs

**MPEDA (Marine Products Export Development Authority)**
- **Establishment:** MPEDA Act, 1972
- **Ministry:** Ministry of Commerce and Industry
- **Scheduled Products:** Marine products — shrimps, prawns, lobster, crab, finfish, cuttlefish, squid, and value-added seafood
- **Functions:**
  1. Registration of seafood processing plants and exporters
  2. **Compulsory pre-shipment inspection** of marine exports
  3. **Residue Monitoring Plan (RMP)** for antibiotics and heavy metals
  4. Approval of **ELISA labs** for antibiotic residue testing
  5. Coordination with Coastal Aquaculture Authority for sustainable aquaculture
  6. Market promotion and development of deep-sea fishing vessels
  7. **Quality control of aquaculture inputs** (feed, seed)

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | APEDA | MPEDA |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Year** | 1985 | 1972 |
| **Product scope** | Agri-processed foods | Marine products |
| **Traceability** | Peanut.Net, HortiNet | Aqua culture traceability |
| **Inspection type** | Voluntary + export compliance | Compulsory pre-shipment |
| **Lab network** | APEDA-recognised labs | ELISA + microbiology labs |

---

**(b)** Discuss the **WTO SPS and TBT Agreements** in detail. How do they impact food trade for developing countries like India? **[7 Marks]**
**[KTU University Exam – December 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

**SPS Agreement (Agreement on the Application of Sanitary and Phytosanitary Measures, 1995):**
- **Objective:** To allow member countries to set measures to protect human, animal, and plant life/health, while preventing these from being used as disguised trade barriers.
- **Key Provisions:**
  1. **Article 2 — Basic Rights and Obligations:** Members have the right to set SPS measures, but they must be based on **scientific principles** and not be more trade-restrictive than necessary.
  2. **Article 3 — Harmonization:** SPS measures should be based on **international standards** (Codex for food safety, OIE for animal health, IPPC for plant health).
  3. **Article 5 — Risk Assessment:** Measures must be based on **scientific risk assessment** considering relevant economic factors.
  4. **Article 6 — Regionalization:** Measures should recognize pest/disease-free areas.
  5. **Article 7 — Transparency:** Members must notify changes in SPS measures.

**TBT Agreement (Agreement on Technical Barriers to Trade, 1995):**
- **Objective:** To ensure that technical regulations, standards, and conformity assessment procedures do not create unnecessary obstacles to trade.
- **Key Provisions:**
  1. Covers **labelling requirements, packaging standards, quality specifications, and conformity assessment** (third-party certification).
  2. Encourages use of **international standards (ISO)** as a basis.
  3. Requires **non-discrimination** between domestic and foreign products.

**Impact on India:**

1. **Positive:** Provides a **legal framework** to challenge protectionist measures by developed countries. India's basmati rice GI was protected under TRIPS.
2. **Challenges:** Indian exporters face **stringent MRL norms** (e.g., EU regulations on okra, mangoes, sesame seeds). Compliance costs are high for small farmers.
3. **Capacity building:** India has invested in **NABL-accredited labs**, **residue monitoring plans**, and **Codex participation** to strengthen SPS infrastructure.
4. **Dispute resolution:** The WTO dispute settlement mechanism has been used by India (e.g., EU's ban on mangoes was resolved through diplomatic and scientific channels).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse the **SPS Agreement** with the **TBT Agreement**. Remember: **SPS = safety** (food, animal, plant health), **TBT = technical specifications** (labelling, packaging, quality). Codex applies to SPS, while ISO applies to TBT. Confusing these will cost you **2 marks minimum**.

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice) [14 Marks]

**(a)** Describe the **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)**. Explain its role in food standardization and the procedure for granting the **ISI Mark**. **[7 Marks]**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

**Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS):**
- **Establishment:** BIS Act, 1986 (replacing ISI Act, 1952)
- **Ministry:** Ministry of Consumer Affairs, Food and Public Distribution
- **Headquarters:** New Delhi
- **Status:** National Standards Body of India; member of ISO and IEC

**Role in Food Standardization:**
1. Formulates **Indian Standards (IS)** for food products, processing, and testing (e.g., IS 14543 for packaged drinking water).
2. **Certification Mark Scheme (ISI):** Voluntary for most products, compulsory for notified categories.
3. **Foreign Manufacturers Certification Scheme (FMCS):** For foreign manufacturers seeking ISI mark.
4. **Laboratory Recognition** — BIS approves labs for testing.
5. **Hallmarking** of gold and silver (extended application).
6. **Standardization of weights and measures** through Legal Metrology Act, 2009 (in coordination with the Department of Consumer Affairs).
7. Represents India in **ISO and Codex** committees.

**Procedure for Granting ISI Mark:**

\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Application} &\rightarrow \text{Manufacturer applies on prescribed form to BIS} \\
\text{Step 2: Documentation} &\rightarrow \text{Quality manual, test reports, process flow submitted} \\
\text{Step 3: Preliminary inspection} &\rightarrow \text{BIS officer inspects factory for infrastructure and QC setup} \\
\text{Step 4: Sample testing} &\rightarrow \text{Samples drawn and tested in BIS lab or recognized lab} \\
\text{Step 5: Grant of License} &\rightarrow \text{BIS issues license with ISI mark usage rights (initially 1 year)} \\
\text{Step 6: Surveillance} &\rightarrow \text{Periodic factory audits and market sampling} \\
\text{Step 7: Renewal} &\rightarrow \text{After 1 year, regular surveillance and 3-year renewal cycle}
\end{aligned}

**Compulsory ISI Marked Food Items (as per BIS Act 1986 + various Quality Control Orders):**
- Packaged drinking water (IS 14543)
- Infant milk substitutes
- Milk powder
- Refined oils
- Carbonated beverages (under Food Safety Regulations)

---

**(b)** Explain the **AGMARK grading system** for any one food commodity. Discuss the process of AGMARK certification in detail. **[7 Marks]**
**[KTU University Exam – December 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

**AGMARK Grading System (using Honey as reference):**

AGMARK classifies honey into three grades based on physico-chemical and microbiological parameters:

| Parameter | Special Grade | Grade 1 | Grade 2 |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Moisture (%)** | ≤ 20 | ≤ 22 | ≤ 25 |
| **Sucrose (%)** | ≤ 5 | ≤ 5 | ≤ 10 |
| **HMF (mg/kg)** | ≤ 80 | ≤ 100 | ≤ 200 |
| **Acidity (formic acid %)** | ≤ 0.2 | ≤ 0.2 | ≤ 0.25 |
| **Optical density** | ≥ 0.6 | ≥ 0.3 | – |
| **Fructose + Glucose (g/100g)** | ≥ 65 | ≥ 60 | ≥ 50 |
| **Pollen count (per gram)** | ≥ 0.5 × 10⁶ | – | – |

**Process of AGMARK Certification:**

\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Application} &\rightarrow \text{Producer applies to DMI / regional office} \\
\text{Step 2: Documentation} &\rightarrow \text{Quality manual, processing details, plant layout} \\
\text{Step 3: Plant inspection} &\rightarrow \text{Inspecting officer visits to verify processing and QC} \\
\text{Step 4: Sampling} &\rightarrow \text{Statistical sampling as per AGMARK protocol} \\
\text{Step 5: Lab testing} &\rightarrow \text{At AGMARK-approved Regional Agmark Laboratory (RAL)} \\
\text{Step 6: Certificate of Authorization} &\rightarrow \text{Issued with AGMARK label assignment} \\
\text{Step 7: Marking} &\rightarrow \text{Producer affixes AGMARK label (with grade, batch, net weight)} \\
\text{Step 8: Surveillance} &\rightarrow \text{Periodic market sampling and testing by DMI}
\end{aligned}

**Types of AGMARK Labels:**
- **Monospecific** (single grade, e.g., "Special")
- **Multigrade** (specifies allowable grade range)
- **Imitation** (mandatory declaration for blended products)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students forget that **AGMARK is voluntary** (not mandatory) for most products, while FSSAI mark is **mandatory** for all packaged food. Writing "AGMARK is compulsory" will lose you **2 marks**.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Print this section):**

### A. National Agencies (Indian) — One-Liners

- ✅ **BIS** → Standards, ISI Mark, BIS Act 1986
- ✅ **AGMARK** → Voluntary quality grading, 1937 Act (amended 1986)
- ✅ **APEDA** → Agricultural processed food exports, 1985 Act
- ✅ **MPEDA** → Marine products exports, 1972 Act, **compulsory** inspection
- ✅ **EIC** → Pre-shipment inspection, 1963 Act
- ✅ **Tea Board** → Tea Act 1953
- ✅ **Coffee Board** → Coffee Act 1942
- ✅ **Spices Board** → 1986 Act, 52 notified spices
- ✅ **NDDB** → Dairy, 1987 Act, Operation Flood legacy
- ✅ **Coconut Development Board** → 1979 Act

### B. International Agencies — One-Liners

- ✅ **Codex** → FAO + WHO, 1963, SPS reference
- ✅ **ISO** → Non-govt, 1947, ISO 22000, 9001
- ✅ **WTO** → 1995, SPS + TBT Agreements
- ✅ **WHO** → Public health, 5 Keys to Safer Food
- ✅ **FAO** → Food security, 1945
- ✅ **GFSI** → Industry-driven, benchmarks schemes
- ✅ **JECFA** → Food additive safety
- ✅ **JMPR** → Pesticide MRLs
- ✅ **WOAH (OIE)** → Animal health
- ✅ **IPPC** → Plant health

### C. Critical Distinctions (Most Examined)

- 🔹 **AGMARK vs FSSAI Logo** → AGMARK = voluntary quality grade; FSSAI = mandatory safety license
- 🔹 **SPS vs TBT** → SPS = safety; TBT = technical
- 🔹 **APEDA vs MPEDA** → Agri-processed vs marine
- 🔹 **Codex vs ISO** → Codex = food-specific reference for SPS; ISO = broader voluntary standards
- 🔹 **National Codex Committee of India** → Chaired by CEO, FSSAI; coordinates with CAC
- 🔹 **GFSI** is **not a standard itself** — it benchmarks existing standards (FSSC 22000, BRC, SQF, IFS)
- 🔹 **HACCP** is a **system**, **ISO 22000** is a **management system standard**, **FSSC 22000** is a **certification scheme**

### D. Memory Triggers

- 🧠 **"B-AA-IE-MP"** for Indian agencies: **B**IS, **A**GMARK, **A**PEDA, **I**SO, **E**IC, **M**PEDA, **P** (product boards)
- 🧠 **"C-F-W-I-J"** for international: **C**odex, **F**AO, **W**HO, **I**SO, **J**ECFA
- 🧠 **Codex priority order** in WTO dispute: **Codex → Scientific Justification → Higher MRL acceptable**
- 🧠 **AGMARK grades** (from best): **S → 1 → 2** (Special, Grade 1, Grade 2)

### E. Examiner's Last-Mile Tips

- 🖊️ Always cite the **Act and Year** when mentioning any agency
- 🖊️ Differentiate between **statutory (mandatory)** and **voluntary** agencies
- 🖊️ When asked to "compare," always provide a **table with at least 4 parameters**
- 🖊️ For numerical questions, show **all units and significant figures**
- 🖊️ For 7-mark questions, include at least **one example product/code** (e.g., IS 14543 for BIS, honey for AGMARK)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Strategy for KTU ESE Module 3:** Focus on the **comparative analysis** between **APEDA vs MPEDA**, **SPS vs TBT**, **AGMARK vs ISI**, and **Codex vs ISO**. These comparisons account for **>60% of past KTU questions** in Module 3.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Risk Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT503 - FOOD SAFETY AND QUALITY REGULATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Risk Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Risk Management** in the context of food safety is a systematic, science-based process consisting of the distinct, yet interlinked, activities of **risk assessment**, **risk management**, and **risk communication**. As defined by the **Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC)**, Risk Management is the process of weighing policy alternatives in light of the results of risk assessment and, if required, selecting and implementing appropriate control options, including regulatory measures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Codex Definition (CAC/GL 30-1999, Rev. 2014):**
> *"Risk Management is the process, distinct from risk assessment, of weighing policy alternatives, in consultation with all interested parties, considering risk assessment and other factors relevant for the health protection of consumers and for the promotion of fair trade practices, and, if needed, selecting appropriate prevention and control options."*

The overarching goal of risk management is to **reduce foodborne risks to an acceptable level** (the **Acceptable Level of Protection – ALOP**) while ensuring fair practices in food trade.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car (the food industry) on a busy highway (the global food market).

- **Hazard** = A pothole or accident on the road (microbes, chemicals, allergens).
- **Risk** = The probability that your car will actually hit that pothole given your speed, tire condition, and traffic.
- **Risk Assessment** = The mechanic analyzing the road, checking your tires, and calculating the crash probability.
- **Risk Management** = You, the driver, deciding to either **brake, swerve, slow down, or even turn off the highway** — i.e., choosing *what to do* about the calculated risk.
- **Risk Communication** = Telling your passengers (consumers, regulators) why you made that decision.

**Risk Management is the DECISION-MAKING arm, while Risk Assessment is the SCIENCE arm.** The manager does not re-do the science; he uses the science to make protective rules.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exams:**
> - **Risk Assessment** = "What is the risk?" (Hazard ID → Hazard Characterization → Exposure Assessment → Risk Characterization)
> - **Risk Management** = "What do we do about it?" (Policy options → Control measures → Implementation → Monitoring → Review)

## 1.3 Core Terminology & Standard Metrics

| Term | Full Form | Standard Metric / Symbol |
|------|-----------|--------------------------|
| **ALOP** | Acceptable Level of Protection | Qualitative (e.g., "reasonable certainty of no harm") |
| **FSO** | Food Safety Objective | Quantitative (e.g., $\leq 10^{-4}$ probability of illness per serving) |
| **PO** | Performance Objective | Quantitative (max concentration of hazard at a step) |
| **PC** | Performance Criterion | Quantitative (effect of one or more control measures) |
| **MC** | Microbiological Criterion | Quantitative (e.g., $n=5, c=2, m=100, M=500$ CFU/g) |
| **NOAEL** | No Observed Adverse Effect Level | mg/kg body weight/day |
| **ADI** | Acceptable Daily Intake | mg/kg body weight/day |
| **ARfD** | Acute Reference Dose | mg/kg body weight/day |
| **MOE** | Margin of Exposure | Dimensionless ratio |
| **TTC** | Threshold of Toxicological Concern | $\mu$g/person/day |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hierarchy of Food Safety Metrics (Top → Bottom):**
> $$\text{ALOP} \rightarrow \text{FSO} \rightarrow \text{PO} \rightarrow \text{PC} \rightarrow \text{MC} \rightarrow \text{CCP (HACCP)}$$
> The Risk Manager converts the broad **ALOP** (a policy goal) into **FSO** and progressively translates it into measurable **MC** at the production line.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Risk Analysis Framework

The Codex framework recognizes that risk analysis is built on **three pillars** plus a **foundation**:

1. **Risk Assessment** (Science) — conducted by independent scientific bodies (e.g., **JECFA**, **JMPR**, national scientific panels).
2. **Risk Management** (Policy) — conducted by regulatory authorities (e.g., **FSSAI**, **FDA**, **EFSA** panels with management remit).
3. **Risk Communication** (Dialogue) — interactive exchange among assessors, managers, and stakeholders.
4. **Foundation: Risk Assessment Policy** — a documented framework setting ground rules for risk assessment to maintain consistency.

> [!NOTE]
> **FSSAI Functional Separation Principle (Section 13 of FSS Act, 2006):**
> Risk Assessment (scientific opinion) and Risk Management (regulatory decision) must be **functionally separated** to ensure scientific independence. In India, risk assessment is performed by scientific panels, and risk management by the **Food Authority (FSSAI)**.

## 2.2 The Risk Management Framework (Codex) — Step-by-Step

The Codex Alimentarius Commission, in its working principles (CAC/GL 30-1999, Rev. 2014), defines a structured, iterative framework. The **risk manager** follows a logical sequence:

### **Step 1: Preliminary Risk Management Activities**
- Identify the food safety issue (a hazard, a food-commodity, a population group).
- Develop a **risk profile** — a concise description of the food safety problem and its context.
- Rank the issue for priority and need for risk assessment.
- Define the **scope** of the risk assessment (the question being asked of the assessors).
- Identify stakeholders and establish the risk assessment policy.

### **Step 2: Evaluation of Risk Management Options**
- Identify the full range of available options (e.g., banning, setting maximum limits, mandatory labeling, process controls, public education).
- Evaluate each option for:
  - **Effectiveness** in reducing risk.
  - **Feasibility** (technical, economic).
  - **Cost-benefit** ratio.
  - **Proportionality** to the level of risk.
  - **Impact on trade** and consumer choice.

### **Step 3: Implementation of the Chosen Option**
- Draft regulations, standards, codes of practice.
- Allocate resources for enforcement and industry compliance.
- Set **transitional periods** for industry to adapt.
- Coordinate with customs, port authorities, municipal bodies for enforcement.

### **Step 4: Monitoring and Review**
- Monitor the effectiveness of the implemented measure (e.g., disease surveillance data, compliance audits, industry self-reports).
- Periodic **review** of the decision in light of new science, new hazards, or changed conditions.
- The cycle is **iterative** — review may trigger a new risk assessment.

## 2.3 Risk Assessment → Risk Management Interface (The Translation Step)

The risk manager receives the outputs of risk assessment and **translates** them into operational food safety metrics. This is one of the most examination-relevant topics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Translation Chain (High-Yield for KTU 14-Mark Questions):**
> 1. **ALOP** (Policy) — e.g., "reduce *Salmonella* in poultry to a level providing high protection of human health."
> 2. **FSO** (Objective) — e.g., "*Salmonella* concentration in chicken at point of consumption $\leq 1$ log CFU/g."
> 3. **PO** (Operational) — e.g., "*Salmonella* on carcass after chilling $\leq$ a specified prevalence."
> 4. **PC** (Process) — e.g., "Achieve at least 5-log reduction of *Salmonella* during scalding."
> 5. **MC** (Microbiological) — e.g., Sampling plan $n=50, c=5, m=0, M=\text{none detected}$.
> 6. **CCP/HACCP** (Plant) — e.g., Time-temperature control at the chilling step.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following mathematical relationships are routinely used in quantitative risk management. **Critical:** Use `\vert` (not the pipe character) for absolute value and norms to keep markdown tables intact.

| # | Concept | Equation / Expression | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---------|----------------------|-------------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Hazard Quotient (HQ)** | $HQ = \dfrac{E}{RfD}$ | $E$ = Exposure (mg/kg bw/day); $RfD$ = Reference Dose (mg/kg bw/day) | Screening-level chemical risk |
| 2 | **Margin of Exposure (MOE)** | $MOE = \dfrac{BMDL_{10}}{E}$ | $BMDL_{10}$ = Benchmark Dose Lower bound (mg/kg bw/day); $E$ = Exposure (mg/kg bw/day) | Genotoxic carcinogens (no threshold) |
| 3 | **Acceptable Daily Intake** | $ADI = \dfrac{NOAEL}{UF}$ | $NOAEL$ (mg/kg bw/day); $UF$ = Uncertainty Factor (typically **100**) | Food additives, pesticides |
| 4 | **Acute Reference Dose** | $ARfD = \dfrac{NO(A)EL}{UF}$ | Same as above but for short-term exposure | Single-day consumption events |
| 5 | **Risk Characterization (Qualitative)** | $Risk = Hazard \times Exposure$ | Conceptual matrix | Pre-formal assessment |
| 6 | **Probabilistic Risk (Stochastic)** | $P_{illness} = \displaystyle\int_{dose} P_{illness} \vert dose \cdot f(dose) \, ddose$ | $f(dose)$ = dose distribution | Quantitative Microbial Risk Assessment (QMRA) |
| 7 | **Mean Risk per Serving** | $\overline{P}_{illness} = \sum_{i} P_{illness, i} \cdot f_i$ | $f_i$ = fraction of servings in dose bin $i$ | QMRA final output |
| 8 | **Annual Disease Burden** | $B = \overline{P}_{illness} \times N_{servings}$ | $N_{servings}$ = total annual servings | Public health impact metric |
| 9 | **Relative Risk Reduction** | $RRR = \dfrac{R_{before} - R_{after}}{R_{before}} \times 100\%$ | Dimensionless (%) | Measures intervention effectiveness |
| 10 | **Log Reduction** | $LR = \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{N_{initial}}{N_{final}}\right)$ | $N$ = microbial count (CFU/g) | Process efficacy in POs/PCs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Tip:** When the syllabus asks to "compute the ALOP or FSO from a risk characterization output," the working step is always: $P_{illness/pop/year} \le$ target → $\overline{P}_{illness/serving} \le \dfrac{P_{target}}{N_{servings/person/year}}$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Risk Management is the **operational backbone** of every modern food safety system:

- **Regulatory bodies (FSSAI, FDA, EFSA, FSANZ)** use the Codex framework to draft food safety regulations, set MRLs, approve additives.
- **Industry** uses it to design HACCP plans, validate process controls, and set microbiological criteria.
- **Trade** uses it to resolve SPS disputes at the **WTO** (Sanitary and Phytosanitary Agreement requires risk-based measures).
- **Consumer protection** is the ultimate goal — the right to safe food is a basic human right recognized by the UN.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Analysis

## 3.1 Sample Numerical Problem — Hazard Quotient and Margin of Exposure

> **Problem (Modeled on KTU Numerical Pattern):**
> A population consumes rice containing a pesticide residue. The estimated **daily exposure (E)** is $0.003$ mg/kg body weight. The **NOAEL** in a 90-day rat study is **30 mg/kg bw/day**, and the **Benchmark Dose Lower Bound (BMDL₁₀)** from a carcinogenicity study is **1.5 mg/kg bw/day**. Calculate:
> (a) The Hazard Quotient (HQ) using a standard Uncertainty Factor (UF) of 100.
> (b) The Margin of Exposure (MOE).
> (c) Interpret each result in the context of risk management decisions.

### 3.1(a) Derivation — Hazard Quotient (HQ)

**Step 1:** Recall the formal definition of HQ. HQ is the ratio of the actual daily exposure to a reference dose considered safe over a lifetime.

$$HQ = \dfrac{E}{RfD}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the reference dose. The RfD is derived from the NOAEL by dividing by the standard Uncertainty Factor (UF). The default UF = 100 (10 for inter-species differences × 10 for intra-species human variability).

$$RfD = \dfrac{NOAEL}{UF}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the given NOAEL.

$$RfD = \dfrac{30 \text{ mg/kg bw/day}}{100} = 0.30 \text{ mg/kg bw/day}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute E and RfD into the HQ equation.

$$HQ = \dfrac{0.003 \text{ mg/kg bw/day}}{0.30 \text{ mg/kg bw/day}}$$

**Step 5:** Cancel the units (mg/kg bw/day) and perform the division.

$$HQ = 0.01$$

**Step 6:** Interpret. The HQ value of **0.01** is **far below 1**, which means exposure is **at most 1% of the acceptable daily intake**. Risk managers would conclude **no concern** under typical use conditions; no additional mitigation is required unless new exposure data emerges.

### 3.1(b) Derivation — Margin of Exposure (MOE)

**Step 1:** Recall the definition. MOE is the ratio of the toxicological reference point (BMDL) to the human exposure. Unlike HQ, there is no bright-line "safe" MOE value; it is interpreted qualitatively.

$$MOE = \dfrac{BMDL_{10}}{E}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the values.

$$MOE = \dfrac{1.5 \text{ mg/kg bw/day}}{0.003 \text{ mg/kg bw/day}}$$

**Step 3:** Perform the division.

$$MOE = 500$$

**Step 4:** Interpret. The **EFSA** framework suggests that for substances that are genotoxic and carcinogenic, an MOE of $\geq 10{,}000$ indicates a "low concern for public health." An MOE of 500 is **substantially below 10,000**, signaling that the risk manager should consider exposure-reduction measures — e.g., lowering the Maximum Residue Limit (MRL), imposing pre-harvest intervals, or restricting use patterns.

### 3.2 Sample Numerical Problem — Risk Reduction Effectiveness

> **Problem:**
> A baseline risk of *Listeria monocytogenes* illness in a ready-to-eat meat product is estimated at $2.5 \times 10^{-4}$ per serving. After implementing a post-process pasteurization step, the risk is reduced to $4.0 \times 10^{-6}$ per serving. The product is consumed $5 \times 10^8$ times annually. Calculate:
> (a) The Relative Risk Reduction (RRR).
> (b) The annual reduction in cases (public health benefit).
> (c) If the regulator's ALOP target is 1,000 annual cases, is the intervention sufficient?

### 3.2(a) Derivation — Relative Risk Reduction (RRR)

**Step 1:** State the formula.

$$RRR = \dfrac{R_{before} - R_{after}}{R_{before}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 2:** Substitute numerical values. Note that the units of risk (per serving) cancel out.

$$RRR = \dfrac{(2.5 \times 10^{-4}) - (4.0 \times 10^{-6})}{2.5 \times 10^{-4}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 3:** Compute the numerator.

$$2.5 \times 10^{-4} - 4.0 \times 10^{-6} = 2.46 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 4:** Divide and convert to percentage.

$$RRR = \dfrac{2.46 \times 10^{-4}}{2.5 \times 10^{-4}} \times 100\% = 98.4\%$$

**Step 5:** Interpret. The pasteurization step reduces the per-serving risk by **98.4%** — a major improvement.

### 3.2(b) Derivation — Annual Cases Avoided

**Step 1:** Compute baseline annual cases.

$$C_{before} = R_{before} \times N_{servings} = (2.5 \times 10^{-4}) \times (5 \times 10^8)$$

**Step 2:** Multiply.

$$C_{before} = 125{,}000 \text{ cases/year}$$

**Step 3:** Compute post-intervention annual cases.

$$C_{after} = R_{after} \times N_{servings} = (4.0 \times 10^{-6}) \times (5 \times 10^8)$$

**Step 4:** Multiply.

$$C_{after} = 2{,}000 \text{ cases/year}$$

**Step 5:** Compute the reduction.

$$\Delta C = 125{,}000 - 2{,}000 = 123{,}000 \text{ cases avoided per year}$$

### 3.2(c) Sufficiency Check Against ALOP

**Step 1:** Compare $C_{after}$ with ALOP target.

$$C_{after} = 2{,}000 \text{ cases/year} > 1{,}000 \text{ cases/year (ALOP)}$$

**Step 2:** Decision. The intervention alone is **not sufficient**. The risk manager must consider **additional measures** (e.g., consumer education, additional lethality, reformulation) or **lower the FSO further** until $C_{after} \le 1{,}000$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked-Example Insight for KTU:**
> Always finish a numerical risk-management question with an **explicit "go / no-go" decision** linking the computed metric to the policy target (ALOP). The examiner awards the final 1–2 marks only for the **interpretation**, not just the arithmetic.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing Risk Management Options

| Option Type | Effectiveness | Feasibility | Cost | Trade Impact | Typical Use |
|-------------|---------------|-------------|------|--------------|-------------|
| **Total ban** | Very high | Low (substitutes needed) | High | High disruption | Proven severe hazard (e.g., certain dyes) |
| **MRL / MPD setting** | Moderate–High | High | Low–Moderate | Low–Moderate | Pesticide, veterinary drug residues |
| **Process control mandate** (HACCP) | High | Moderate | Moderate | Low | Microbial hazards |
| **Labeling / advisory** | Variable (consumer-driven) | High | Low | Very low | Allergens, raw milk, raw sprouts |
| **Public education campaign** | Long-term, variable | High | Moderate | None | *Salmonella* in eggs, hand washing |
| **Recall / withdrawal** | Immediate | Logistically heavy | High | Moderate | Contamination incidents |
| **Testing/inspection regime** | Detect-and-react | High | Moderate | None | Imports, high-risk categories |

> [!NOTE]
> **Codex Working Principles require** that risk managers apply the **"lowest necessary level of restriction consistent with the ALOP"** — i.e., the least-trade-restrictive measure that achieves the health goal. This principle is mirrored in the **WTO-SPS Agreement (Article 5)**.

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Codex Risk Analysis Framework — Master Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Issue Identified - Food Safety Concern]:::startNode
    A --> B[Preliminary Risk Management Activities]
    B --> B1[Risk Profile]
    B --> B2[Risk Ranking & Prioritization]
    B --> B3[Define Scope of Risk Assessment]
    B --> B4[Risk Assessment Policy]
    B4 --> C[Risk Assessment - Science Branch]
    C --> C1[Hazard Identification]
    C1 --> C2[Hazard Characterization]
    C2 --> C3[Exposure Assessment]
    C3 --> C4[Risk Characterization]
    C4 --> D[Risk Management Decision Phase]
    D --> D1[Identify Risk Management Options]
    D1 --> D2[Evaluate Options - Effectiveness, Feasibility, Cost]
    D2 --> D3[Select Appropriate Option]
    D3 --> E[Implementation of Control Measure]
    E --> F[Monitoring and Surveillance]
    F --> G[Review and Iterate]
    G --> B
    H[Risk Communication]:::commNode
    H -.-> B
    H -.-> C
    H -.-> D
    H -.-> E
    H -.-> F
    H -.-> G

    classDef startNode fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef commNode fill:#c00000,stroke:#c00000,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the Diagram:** The blue node is the **entry point**; the red node (**Risk Communication**) is a *transverse* function that runs parallel to *every* other step — it is not a one-way broadcast but a two-way dialogue.

## 4.2 Translation Chain — From Policy Goal to Plant-Level Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[ALOP - Acceptable Level of Protection]:::policy
    P1 -->|Translate| P2[FSO - Food Safety Objective]:::obj
    P2 -->|Translate| P3[PO - Performance Objective]:::obj
    P3 -->|Translate| P4[PC - Performance Criterion]:::proc
    P4 -->|Translate| P5[MC - Microbiological Criterion]:::proc
    P5 -->|Implement| P6[CCP - Critical Control Point]:::plant
    P6 -->|Validate| P7[HACCP Plan and Verification]:::plant

    classDef policy fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef obj fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef proc fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef plant fill:#a9d18e,stroke:#548235,color:#000000,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the Diagram:** Color-graded from **blue (policy / regulatory level)** to **green (plant-floor operational level)**, the diagram shows how the risk manager progressively *decomposes* a high-level health goal into measurable factory controls.

## 4.3 Risk Management Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q1{Is risk assessment available and adequate?}:::decision
    Q1 -->|No| R1[Commission new risk assessment or use precautionary approach]:::action
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Is the risk above ALOP?}:::decision
    Q2 -->|No| M1[Maintain status quo and monitor]:::action
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3[Identify and list all feasible risk management options]:::action
    Q3 --> Q4{Evaluate options: effectiveness, feasibility, cost, trade}:::decision
    Q4 --> Q5[Select least-trade-restrictive measure consistent with ALOP]:::action
    Q5 --> Q6[Implement measure - regulation, code, inspection]:::action
    Q6 --> Q7[Monitor outcomes and review periodically]:::action
    Q7 --> Q1
    R1 --> Q1

    classDef decision fill:#ffd966,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef action fill:#a9d18e,stroke:#548235,color:#000000,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the Diagram:** Yellow diamonds = **decision gates**; green rectangles = **manager actions**. The loop returns to the start because risk management is *iterative*, not a one-time event.

## 4.4 The Preliminary Risk Management Activities Sub-Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Food Safety Issue Identified]:::stage
    S1 --> S2[Develop Risk Profile - context, stakeholders, scope]
    S2 --> S3[Rank the Issue - urgency, severity, public concern]
    S3 --> S4{Is a risk assessment needed?}
    S4 -->|Yes - existing knowledge insufficient| S5[Commission Risk Assessment]
    S4 -->|No - sufficient existing knowledge| S6[Set Risk Assessment Policy]
    S5 --> S6
    S6 --> S7[Define Assessment Scope and Question]
    S7 --> S8[Identify Stakeholders and Consult]
    S8 --> S9[Proceed to Risk Management Option Evaluation]

    classDef stage fill:#dbe5f1,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#000000,stroke-width:1px
```

> [!TIP]
> **Mermaid Safeguard Note (visible to student):**
> All node IDs above are alphanumeric (e.g., `Q1`, `P2`, `S5`), and all node labels are plain uppercase alphanumeric text — this complies with the diagram-rendering safety rules used in the KTU study engine.
