
### 4.3 GMP Mock Recall Traceability Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Consumer Complaint Received] --> B[Quarantine Suspected Batch]
    B --> C[Trace Forward: Customer List]
    C --> D[Trace Backward: Supplier List]
    D --> E[Identify All Affected Lots]
    E --> F{More Than 4 Hours?}
    F -- YES --> FAIL[Non Conformance Report]
    F -- NO --> G[Issue Public Recall Notice]
    G --> H[Product Retrieval and Destruction]
    H --> I[Root Cause Analysis CAPA]
    I --> J[Update FSSAI and Close]

    style FAIL fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000
    style J fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**Define GMP. Why is it considered a mandatory prerequisite for HACCP implementation in food industries?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**GMP (Good Manufacturing Practices)** is defined by WHO as the part of quality assurance that ensures products are consistently produced and controlled to quality standards appropriate to their intended use, governing operational and environmental conditions to prevent contamination, mix-ups, and errors.

GMP is **mandatory before HACCP** because:
1. **HACCP depends on a stable environment** — without hygienic premises, sanitised equipment, and trained personnel, hazard control is impossible.
2. **Codex Alimentarius (CAC/RCP 1)** makes GMP the foundational layer; HACCP sits on top of it.
3. **FSSAI Schedule 4** legally mandates GMP; HACCP is mandatory only for high-risk product categories (milk, meat, fish, infant food).

(3 marks awarded for: 1 mark definition + 2 marks justification.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**List any six essential elements of GMP as prescribed under FSSAI Schedule 4.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — list any 6):**

1. Suitable premises, location, construction, and layout
2. Adequate sanitation and pest control measures
3. Personal hygiene of personnel
4. Equipment and container design (food-grade, non-toxic)
5. Production and quality control procedures
6. Documentation and record-keeping (one-up one-down traceability)
7. Warehousing, distribution, and recall procedures
8. Training of food handlers

(½ mark each × 6 = 3 marks.)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — GMP Framework & Compliance**

**[KTU University Exam – July 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the 12 pillars of GMP under the Codex Alimentarius framework. Discuss the role of documentation in GMP compliance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A frozen seafood export unit is audited for 6 GMP clauses with the following data. Calculate the **Audit Compliance Score (ACS)** and state whether the unit will be certified under BRC Grade AA (cut-off = 85%). **(7 marks)**

| Clause | Type | Weight | Score |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cold storage ≤ –18 °C | Critical | 5 | 95 |
| Metal detection at packing | Critical | 5 | 70 |
| Hand-wash compliance | Major | 3 | 85 |
| Sanitizer concentration log | Major | 3 | 60 |
| Floor drainage slope | Minor | 1 | 80 |
| Visitor register | Minor | 1 | 100 |

**Model Answer:**

**(a) — 7 Marks:**

The 12 pillars of GMP under **Codex CAC/RCP 1-1969, Rev. 2003** are:

| # | Pillar | One-line Functional Role |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Premises Design | Zoning, lighting, drainage |
| 2 | Equipment | Food-grade SS 304/316, cleanability |
| 3 | Sanitation | SSOPs, CIP/COP |
| 4 | Pest Control | Bait stations, fumigation logs |
| 5 | Personal Hygiene | Hand-wash, health declaration |
| 6 | Training | SOP induction + annual refresher |
| 7 | Raw Material Control | Approved supplier + incoming checks |
| 8 | Production Control | Batch records, line clearance |
| 9 | Quality Control | Lab testing, retain samples |
| 10 | Warehousing | FIFO, temperature mapping |
| 11 | Documentation | SOPs, logbooks, traceability |
| 12 | Recall | Mock recall within 4 hours |

**Role of Documentation (1 mark bonus integrated):**
Documentation converts *invisible discipline* into *verifiable evidence*. The auditor's golden rule: *"If it is not documented, it did not happen."* Documentation includes SOPs, logbooks, calibration certificates, batch records, supplier approvals, and traceability registers.

**(b) — 7 Marks: Numerical Solution**

**Step 1 — Weighted scores:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Clause 1: } & 5 \times 95 = 475 \\
\text{Clause 2: } & 5 \times 70 = 350 \\
\text{Clause 3: } & 3 \times 85 = 255 \\
\text{Clause 4: } & 3 \times 60 = 180 \\
\text{Clause 5: } & 1 \times 80 = 80 \\
\text{Clause 6: } & 1 \times 100 = 100 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Sum of weighted scores:**

$$
N = 475 + 350 + 255 + 180 + 80 + 100 = 1440
$$

**Step 3 — Sum of weights:**

$$
\sum w_i = 5 + 5 + 3 + 3 + 1 + 1 = 18
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate ACS:**

$$
ACS = \dfrac{N}{\sum w_i} = \dfrac{1440}{18} = 80.00 \,\%
$$

**Step 5 — Verdict:**

$$
80.00 \,\% < 85 \,\% \implies \text{Unit FAILS BRC Grade AA certification.}
$$

> **However**, since **Clause 2 (Metal Detection)** is a *Critical* clause with score 70/100, even if ACS had been ≥ 85%, the unit would have received an **automatic Critical Non-Conformance (CNC)** disqualification. **Final Verdict: Audit FAILED**; corrective action required on metal detection and sanitizer logs before re-audit.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: GMP vs HACCP & Implementation**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Compare GMP and HACCP in a structured matrix. Explain why GMP is the prerequisite foundation of HACCP with a real-world dairy industry example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the step-by-step implementation of GMP in a new ready-to-eat (RTE) food manufacturing unit. Highlight the critical documentation requirements under FSSAI Schedule 4. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) — 7 Marks:**

**GMP vs HACCP Matrix (4 marks):**

| Parameter | GMP | HACCP |
|---|---|---|
| Nature | Prescriptive "what to do" | Risk-based "what could go wrong" |
| Origin | WHO 1969, Codex | NASA/Pillsbury 1971 |
| Scope | Universal across all foods | Product-and-process specific |
| Critical Control Points | Not used | 7 Codex principles |
| Statutory status (FSSAI) | Mandatory for all FBOs | Mandatory for high-risk |
| Hierarchy | Foundation | Built on top of GMP |

**Dairy Industry Example (3 marks):**

A dairy plant processing pasteurised milk must first establish **GMP** — hygienic premises, SS 304 pipelines, CIP system, cold chain at **≤ 4 °C**, trained workers, sanitation logs. Only after this foundation is laid can **HACCP** identify the *Critical Control Points* (e.g., pasteurisation at **72 °C for 15 s**, metal detection at packing, cold-chain maintenance). Without GMP (e.g., leaking valves, untrained staff), HACCP monitoring itself fails because CCP measurements are unreliable. Thus, **GMP is the operational bed-rock; HACCP is the risk overlay.**

**(b) — 7 Marks: GMP Implementation in an RTE Unit**

**Step 1 — Gap Analysis (1 mark):** Benchmark current status against FSSAI Schedule 4, Codex CAC/RCP 1, and BIS standards. Output: Gap report with critical/major/minor findings.

**Step 2 — Document Development (2 marks):**
- Master File, SOP library, SSOPs, logbooks (temperature, cleaning, sanitiser).
- Allergen management plan, traceability matrix.
- Recall SOP and mock recall protocol.

**Step 3 — Infrastructure Readiness (1 mark):**
- SS 304 equipment, drainage slope ≥ 1:40, lighting ≥ 540 lux, ventilation ≥ 10 ACH.
- Separate high-risk (RTE) zone with positive air pressure.

**Step 4 — Training (1 mark):**
- 16 hours induction + 8 hours annual refresher per FSSAI Schedule 4, Part II.
- Documented training records and competency assessments.

**Step 5 — Internal Audit and Certification (2 marks):**
- Mock audit → CAPA closure → Apply to certifying body (BRC, SQF, FSSC).
- Maintain retain samples ≥ shelf life + 30 days.
- Mock recall with traceability ≤ 4 hours.

> **Critical Documentation:** FSSAI Schedule 4 mandates at minimum: (i) Food Safety Management System (FSMS) plan, (ii) Sanitation SOPs, (iii) Pest control records, (iv) Water analysis reports, (v) Medical fitness certificates of food handlers, (vi) Calibration certificates of instruments, (vii) Batch coding and traceability register.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "Voluntary" with "Optional":** Writing that GMP is "voluntary" costs **2 marks** — GMP is **mandatory** under FSSAI Schedule 4. The *standards* (BRC, FSSC) are voluntary; *the practice* is statutory.
> 2. **Skipping the ACS formula derivation:** Many students write only the final answer. KTU awards step marks only when **each calculation step is shown**. Always show: weighted scores → sum → divide → verdict.
> 3. **Forgetting Critical Non-Conformance rule:** Even if ACS ≥ 85%, a single Critical clause failure disqualifies the audit. Students who ignore this lose 2 marks.
> 4. **Mixing up temperatures:** Cold chain = **≤ 4 °C**, frozen = **≤ –18 °C**, hot holding = **≥ 63 °C**. These are **favourite 1-mark filler questions.**
> 5. **Not mentioning documentation:** Every answer on GMP should end with *"Documentation is the proof of compliance."* Examiners allocate a mandatory 1 mark for documentation awareness.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — GMP (Module 4)**

- **Definition:** GMP = Quality Assurance + consistent production + control to standards (WHO).
- **Origin:** WHO 1969, codified in **Codex CAC/RCP 1-1969 (Rev. 2003)**.
- **Indian Law:** **FSSAI Schedule 4** of the **Food Safety & Standards (Licensing & Registration) Regulations, 2011**.
- **12 Pillars:** Premises, Equipment, Sanitation, Pest Control, Personnel Hygiene, Training, Raw Material, Production, QC Lab, Warehousing, Documentation, Recall.
- **Mandatory** under FSSAI; **Voluntary standards** (BRC, SQF, FSSC 22000, ISO 22000) sit **above** GMP.
- **Hierarchy:** ISO/FSSC → HACCP → OPRP → PRP (GMP/GHP) → Management commitment.
- **Audit Scoring Formula:** $ACS = \dfrac{\sum w_i s_i}{\sum w_i}$ — Critical clause failure = auto-disqualification.
- **Temperatures:** Cold chain ≤ 4 °C; Frozen ≤ –18 °C; Hot holding ≥ 63 °C; Cooking ≥ 74 °C (poultry).
- **Sanitizer Concentrations:** Quaternary Ammonium Compounds (QAC) **200–400 ppm**; Chlorine **50–200 ppm** free.
- **Lighting:** ≥ 540 lux at inspection points; Ventilation ≥ 10 ACH (high-risk zones).
- **Documentation Principle:** *"If it is not documented, it did not happen."*
- **Mock Recall:** Traceability must be achievable in **≤ 4 hours**.
- **Retain Sample:** Shelf life **+ 30 days**.
- **EHEDG / 3-A / ASME BPE:** Equipment hygienic design standards (engineering aspect).
- **Difference from HACCP:** GMP = prescriptive universal; HACCP = risk-based product-specific.
- **FSSAI Section 26:** Misrepresentation on label of voluntary claims = punishable offence.

> **Last-Mantra for KTU Exam Hall:** *GMP is the law. HACCP is the science. ISO is the choice. Documentation is the proof. Critical non-conformity is the death of certification.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: HACCP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT503 - FOOD SAFETY AND QUALITY REGULATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** HACCP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🍱 Module 4 — HACCP: The Global Backbone of Food Safety Assurance

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

**HACCP (Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Point)** is a **systematic, science-based, and preventive** food safety management system that identifies, evaluates, and controls hazards which are significant for food safety. It is built upon **seven internationally recognized principles** published by the **Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC/RCP 1-1969, Rev. 2020)** and forms the regulatory cornerstone of food safety regimes across the world, including the **Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI)** under the **Food Safety and Standards (Food Safety Management System) Regulations, 2011**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Codex Definition:**
> *"HACCP is a system which identifies, evaluates, and controls hazards which are significant for food safety."* — Codex Alimentarius Commission, FAO/WHO.

**Origin & Historical Timeline (Mandatory for KTU 2-Markers):**

| Year | Milestone | Significance |
|------|-----------|--------------|
| **1959** | Pillsbury Company (USA) develops concept | Originally for **100% safety of astronaut food** in the NASA space programme |
| **1971** | HACCP presented at the **National Conference on Food Protection (USA)** | First public introduction |
| **1993** | **Codex Alimentarius Commission** adopts HACCP guidelines | International harmonization |
| **1997** | Codex issues revised *Guidelines for the Application of HACCP System* | Modern 7-principle framework |
| **2005** | FSSAI precursor bodies adopt HACCP in India | National rollout begins |
| **2020** | Codex Revision (CAC/RCP 1-1969, Rev. 2020) | Latest global standard |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🧠

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Road Trip" Analogy:**
> 
> Imagine you are driving a car from **Kochi to Delhi** with your family. Before starting, you don't just *hope* the journey is safe — you **map the route**, identify **dangerous intersections** (accident-prone zones), set a **speed limit at each danger point**, install a **speedometer to monitor speed**, plan **alternate routes** if a road is blocked, **double-check the car's brakes** periodically, and **maintain a logbook** of the trip.
> 
> **HACCP does exactly this for food!**
> - The **route** = Food process flow (raw material → consumer).
> - The **dangerous intersections** = **Critical Control Points (CCPs)**.
> - The **speed limit** = **Critical Limits** (e.g., temperature ≤ 4 °C).
> - The **speedometer** = **Monitoring procedures**.
> - The **alternate route** = **Corrective actions**.
> - The **brake check** = **Verification**.
> - The **logbook** = **Records & Documentation**.

---

## 1.3 Scope & Coverage of HACCP

HACCP addresses **three classes of hazards** that may compromise food safety:

$$
\text{HACCP Hazard Categories} = \{B_{\text{iological}}, \; C_{\text{hemical}}, \; P_{\text{ysical}}\}
$$

- **Biological Hazards** — Pathogenic bacteria (*Salmonella*, *Listeria monocytogenes*, *E. coli* O157:H7), viruses (Norovirus, Hepatitis A), parasites, prions, toxins (botulinum, aflatoxin).
- **Chemical Hazards** — Pesticide residues, veterinary drug residues, heavy metals (Pb, Hg, Cd, As), food additives in excess, cleaning agents, allergens (mustard, peanut, gluten, milk).
- **Physical Hazards** — Glass fragments, metal shavings, stones, bone fragments, plastic pieces, hair, insect parts, wood splinters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Note that **radiological hazards** and **allergens** are now explicitly recognized as distinct categories in **CAC/RCP 1-1969, Rev. 2020** — older answer keys may still classify them under "chemical". Use the modern 3-bucket classification unless the question specifies a year.

---

## 1.4 Key Regulatory Anchor

HACCP is **mandatory** under the following frameworks in India:

- **FSSAI** — Food Safety and Standards (Food Safety Management System) Regulations, **2011** (Schedule 4).
- **Codex Alimentarius** — *CAC/RCP 1-1969, Rev. 2020*.
- **ISO 22000:2018** — Combines HACCP with Prerequisite Programmes (PRPs) and ISO 9001 quality management principles.
- **BRC Global Standard for Food Safety (Issue 9)** and **SQF Edition 9** — Commercial retail standards that explicitly require HACCP.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🧪 Deep Theoretical Analysis — The 12 Implementation Steps & 7 Principles

The **Codex framework** structures HACCP implementation as a **5-step preliminary phase** followed by the **7 principles**, totaling **12 logical steps**. A KTU 14-mark question will almost certainly require you to **draw and explain this entire sequence**.

## 2.1 Phase A — Preliminary Steps (Steps 1 to 5)

> [!NOTE]
> These are **NOT** part of the "7 Principles" of HACCP. They are **preparatory groundwork**. A frequent KTU pitfall is to merge them with the 7 principles.

### Step 1: Assemble the HACCP Team
- Multidisciplinary team with expertise in: production, sanitation, engineering, microbiology, chemistry, quality assurance, and food regulation.
- Team must be **trained** on HACCP methodology.
- External consultants may assist but **must not replace** in-house ownership.

### Step 2: Describe the Product
A complete product description must include:
- Composition (ingredients, raw materials, additives).
- Origin of raw materials.
- Processing/packaging methods.
- **pH**, **water activity (a_w)**, preservatives.
- Storage and distribution conditions.
- Shelf life.
- Intended use and consumer target group (infants, elderly, immunocompromised, etc.).

### Step 3: Identify Intended Use
- Who will consume the product? (General public, infants, hospital patients?)
- How will they consume it? (Raw, cooked, reheated?)
- Are vulnerable groups (allergy sufferers, pregnant women) at risk?

### Step 4: Construct the Process Flow Diagram
- A **block-by-block schematic** of every step from raw material receipt to consumer use.
- Must include: receiving, storage, sorting, washing, peeling, blanching, cooking, cooling, packaging, labeling, storage, distribution.

### Step 5: On-Site Verification of the Flow Diagram
- The HACCP team must **physically walk** the production facility and **verify** the diagram is accurate.
- Confirmed with a **dated and signed** version of the diagram.

---

## 2.2 Phase B — The Seven Principles (Steps 6 to 12)

### 🔴 Principle 1: Conduct a Hazard Analysis (HA)
- **Identify** every potential hazard (B, C, P) at **each process step**.
- Assess **severity** (consequence) and **likelihood** (probability) of occurrence.
- Identify **preventive control measures** for each significant hazard.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A hazard is "**significant**" if its elimination or reduction to an acceptable level is **essential** to food safety — i.e., reasonable likelihood of occurrence AND serious adverse health consequence.

**Hazard Analysis Worksheet (Typical KTU Diagram):**

| Process Step | Hazard Identified | Type | Severity | Likelihood | Significant? | Control Measure |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Raw milk receipt | *Salmonella* | Biological | High | Medium | ✅ Yes | Pasteurization |
| Raw milk receipt | Antibiotic residue | Chemical | Medium | Low | ✅ Yes | Supplier COA, testing |
| Pasteurization | Inadequate temperature | Biological | High | Low | ✅ Yes | Time-temp control (CCP-1) |
| Packaging | Glass fragment | Physical | High | Low | ✅ Yes | Metal detector (CCP-2) |

---

### 🔴 Principle 2: Determine the Critical Control Points (CCPs)

**Definition:** A CCP is a **step at which control can be applied** and is **essential to prevent or eliminate** a food safety hazard, or to **reduce it to an acceptable level**.

**The Codex CCP Decision Tree (must be drawn in 14-mark questions!):**

```
Q1: Do preventive control measures exist for the identified hazard?
   ├── NO  → Modify process / product / use PRPs to create a control → RE-EVALUATE
   └── YES ↓

Q2: Does this step eliminate or reduce the hazard to an acceptable level?
   ├── YES → CCP  ✅
   └── NO  ↓

Q3: Could contamination with the identified hazard(s) occur in excess
    of acceptable level(s), or could it increase to unacceptable level(s)?
   ├── NO  → NOT a CCP ❌
   └── YES ↓

Q4: Will a subsequent step eliminate or reduce the identified hazard
    to an acceptable level?
   ├── YES → NOT a CCP ❌
   └── NO  → CCP  ✅
```

---

### 🔴 Principle 3: Establish Critical Limits (CLs)

- **Quantitative / qualitative** criteria that separate **acceptability from unacceptability**.
- Must be applied at **each CCP**.
- Examples:
  - Pasteurization: **72 °C for 15 seconds** (or equivalent).
  - Cold storage: **≤ 4 °C**.
  - Hot holding: **≥ 63 °C**.
  - Water activity (a_w): **< 0.85** (inhibits most pathogens).
  - pH: **< 4.6** (inhibits *Clostridium botulinum*).
  - Metal detector sensitivity: **Fe ≤ 2.0 mm, Non-Fe ≤ 2.5 mm, SS ≤ 3.0 mm**.

---

### 🔴 Principle 4: Establish Monitoring Procedures

| Element | Description |
|---|---|
| **What** | Parameter to be monitored (temperature, time, pH, a_w, visual) |
| **How** | Method and equipment (thermometer, pH meter, metal detector) |
| **When** | Frequency (continuous, hourly, per batch) |
| **Who** | Designated trained person responsible |
| **Where** | Exact location of measurement |

> [!NOTE]
> **Monitoring must be continuous** wherever feasible. Non-continuous monitoring is acceptable only when documented procedures clearly justify it.

---

### 🔴 Principle 5: Establish Corrective Actions

When monitoring shows that a **critical limit is exceeded** (deviation), the HACCP plan must specify:

1. **Identification and isolation** of the affected product.
2. **Disposition** — destroy, rework, or release with regulatory approval.
3. **Root cause analysis** — why did the deviation occur?
4. **Preventive action** — to prevent recurrence.
5. **Documentation** of the entire event.

---

### 🔴 Principle 6: Establish Verification Procedures

Verification answers the question: *"Is the HACCP system working effectively?"*

- **Methods:** Internal audits, microbiological testing, end-product testing, calibration of monitoring equipment, review of records.
- **Frequency:** At least **annually**, or when there is a product change, process change, or new hazard information.
- **Performed by someone other than the monitor** (independence principle).

---

### 🔴 Principle 7: Establish Record-Keeping and Documentation

**Mandatory records include:**
1. HACCP team composition and qualifications.
2. Product description and intended use.
3. Verified process flow diagram.
4. Hazard analysis worksheet.
5. CCP determination (with decision tree).
6. Critical limits for each CCP.
7. Monitoring records.
8. Corrective action records.
9. Verification records (audit reports, calibration).
10. Training records.
11. Distribution records.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Records must be **retained for at least one year past the shelf life** of the product (for perishable goods) and **two years** for non-perishable — per FSSAI Schedule 4.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity / Concept | Symbol / Threshold | Standard Reference | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Critical Control Point | **CCP** | Codex CAC/RCP 1 | Identifies mandatory control nodes |
| Critical Limit | **CL** | Codex / FSSAI | Quantitative acceptability boundary |
| Water Activity Limit for Pathogens | **a_w < 0.85** | FDA / Codex | Determines product safety in intermediate-moisture foods |
| pH Limit (Clostridium botulinum) | **pH < 4.6** | FDA / Codex | Acidified food safety |
| Pasteurization | **72 °C × 15 s** (HTST) | FSSAI / Codex | Milk safety CCP |
| Cold chain holding | **≤ 4 °C** | FSSAI | Chilled food CCP |
| Hot holding | **≥ 63 °C** | Codex | Hot food service |
| Metal detector sensitivity | **Fe 2.0 mm, SS 3.0 mm** | BRC v9 | Physical hazard CCP |
| Record retention (perishable) | **≥ 1 yr post-shelf-life** | FSSAI 2011 | Documentation compliance |
| Record retention (non-perishable) | **≥ 2 yr** | FSSAI 2011 | Documentation compliance |
| Hazard Severity × Likelihood | **Risk Score = S × L** | Codex Risk Matrix | HACCP prioritization |

---

## 2.4 Why HACCP? — Real-World Engineering Utility

HACCP moves food safety from a **reactive (end-product testing)** paradigm to a **proactive (in-process control)** paradigm. This is analogous to **Total Quality Management (TQM)** in manufacturing and **Predictive Maintenance (PdM)** in mechanical engineering. Benefits:

1. **Cost reduction** — fewer recalls, lower rejection rates.
2. **Consumer protection** — measurable public health outcome.
3. **Regulatory compliance** — FSSAI, ISO 22000, BRC, SQF, GFSI.
4. **International trade facilitation** — Codex compliance enables global export.
5. **Risk-based decision making** — analogous to **FMEA** (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis) in mechanical engineering.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📐 Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — Hazard Analysis Risk Scoring

## 3.1 The Risk Matrix Method (Standard KTU Application)

HACCP hazard analysis can be quantified using a **Risk Score** based on Codex risk analysis methodology. Below is a **fully derived, exhaustive** step-by-step demonstration of the calculation.

### Step 1: Define the Severity (S) Scale

The Codex severity classification is:

| Severity Level | Descriptor | Numerical Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Catastrophic** | Life-threatening, fatal, long-term chronic effects | **S = 4** |
| **Major** | Severe illness, hospitalization required, long-term sequelae | **S = 3** |
| **Moderate** | Mild to moderate illness, self-resolving | **S = 2** |
| **Minor** | Discomfort only, no medical intervention needed | **S = 1** |

### Step 2: Define the Likelihood (L) Scale

| Likelihood Level | Descriptor | Numerical Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Frequent** | Almost certain, occurs daily or per batch | **L = 5** |
| **Probable** | Likely to occur several times per year | **L = 4** |
| **Occasional** | Possible, has occurred in the industry | **L = 3** |
| **Remote** | Unlikely, but theoretically possible | **L = 2** |
| **Improbable** | Practically impossible | **L = 1** |

### Step 3: Calculate the Risk Score

The risk score for each hazard at each step is given by:

$$
R = S \times L
$$

where $R$ is the dimensionless risk score, $S$ is the severity (range 1–4), and $L$ is the likelihood (range 1–5).

Theoretical maximum:

$$
R_{\max} = S_{\max} \times L_{\max} = 4 \times 5 = 20
$$

### Step 4: Risk Categorization

| Risk Score (R) | Category | Action |
|---|---|---|
| **R ≥ 15** | **High Risk** | **Must become a CCP** (mandatory control) |
| **8 ≤ R < 15** | **Medium Risk** | Controlled via **PRPs (Prerequisite Programmes)** or operational PRPs (oPRPs) |
| **R < 8** | **Low Risk** | Acceptable; review periodically |

### Step 3 (Worked Numerical Example)

**Scenario:** A pasteurization step in a milk plant.

**Given:**
- Hazard: Survival of *Mycobacterium tuberculosis* and *Coxiella burnetii*.
- Severity: Catastrophic (tuberculosis) $\Rightarrow S = 4$.
- Likelihood: Probable without pasteurization $\Rightarrow L = 4$.

**Substitution into the risk equation:**

$$
R = S \times L
$$

$$
R = 4 \times 4
$$

$$
R = 16
$$

**Interpretation:** Since $R = 16 \geq 15$, the hazard is classified as **High Risk** and the pasteurization step must be designated as a **CCP** (CCP-1 in the final plan).

**Comparison scenario — Metal detection step:**
- Hazard: Metal fragment in finished product.
- Severity: Major (choking, GI injury) $\Rightarrow S = 3$.
- Likelihood: Remote (rare) $\Rightarrow L = 2$.

$$
R = 3 \times 2 = 6
$$

**Interpretation:** $R = 6 < 8$, but metal detection is still **legally and operationally** classified as a CCP because regulatory standards (FSSAI Schedule 4, BRC) mandate physical hazard control independent of numerical risk score. This illustrates that **the CCP decision tree (qualitative) and the risk matrix (quantitative) are complementary, not substitutes**.

---

## 3.2 The Hazard Analysis Critical Control Point (HACCP) Master Matrix

Below is the **complete integrated matrix** that examiners expect in a 14-mark answer.

| Process Step (from Flow Diagram) | Hazard Type | Hazard Description | Severity (S) | Likelihood (L) | Risk Score (R = S×L) | Significant? | Control Measure | CCP? | Critical Limit | Monitoring | Corrective Action | Verification | Records |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Raw milk receipt | Biological | *Salmonella* spp. | 3 | 3 | **9** | Yes | Pasteurization | **No** (controlled downstream) | — | — | — | Supplier audit | COA |
| Raw milk receipt | Chemical | Antibiotic residue | 3 | 2 | **6** | Yes | Supplier COA + testing | **No** (PRP) | Nil detection | Sampling per batch | Reject batch | Lab testing | Lab reports |
| Pasteurization | Biological | Pathogen survival | 4 | 4 | **16** | Yes | Time-temperature | **✅ CCP-1** | **72 °C / 15 s** | Continuous chart recorder | Re-pasteurize / reject | Daily calibration, microbial test | Temp charts |
| Pasteurization | Chemical | None identified | — | — | — | — | — | — | — | — | — | — | — |
| Pasteurization | Physical | None identified | — | — | — | — | — | — | — | — | — | — | — |
| Filling & sealing | Physical | Glass / metal fragment | 3 | 2 | **6** | Yes | Metal detector | **✅ CCP-2** | Fe ≤ 2.0 mm | Continuous detector | Reject, hold, investigate | Test piece hourly | Detector log |
| Cold storage | Biological | *Listeria* growth | 3 | 3 | **9** | Yes | Cold chain | **✅ CCP-3** | **≤ 4 °C** | Continuous probe | Transfer to alternate chiller, assess product | Daily temperature audit | Temp logs |
| Dispatch | All | Distribution breach | 2 | 2 | **4** | No | Vehicle inspection | **No** (PRP) | — | — | — | Vehicle audit | Dispatch records |

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the 7-Principle Sequential Logic

A KTU 14-mark question may ask *"Justify the sequence of the 7 principles"*. The derivation:

1. **Principle 1 (Hazard Analysis)** must come first because the very **existence** of CCPs depends on the hazards identified. No hazards → no CCPs.
2. **Principle 2 (Determine CCPs)** logically follows because once hazards are known, the team must decide **where** to control them. CCPs are the **nodes** in the flow.
3. **Principle 3 (Critical Limits)** depends on CCPs being defined; you cannot set a limit on a non-existent control point.
4. **Principle 4 (Monitoring)** requires the critical limit to be known — monitoring measures deviation **from the critical limit**.
5. **Principle 5 (Corrective Actions)** is a logical downstream consequence of monitoring: when monitoring detects a deviation, an action must exist **pre-defined** (not improvised).
6. **Principle 6 (Verification)** is a **meta-control** — it checks whether Principles 1–5 are functioning correctly. It must come after the system is in place.
7. **Principle 7 (Record-Keeping)** is the **foundational evidence layer** that supports all other principles — without records, verification is impossible and regulatory compliance fails. It is sequenced last because the system must be **operating** before there is anything to record.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This sequence is **logical, not arbitrary**. The **Codex** deliberately orders the principles this way to ensure that each principle has the necessary inputs from the previous one. In an exam, **mentioning this logical dependency earns you extra marks**.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Pasteurized Milk HACCP Plan

**Product:** Pasteurized Toned Milk (Composition: Cow milk 80% + Skimmed milk powder 20% reconstituted; Fat ≥ 3.0%, SNF ≥ 8.5%)

**Process Flow:** Raw milk receipt → Pre-heating → Standardization → Pasteurization (72 °C × 15 s) → Cooling to 4 °C → Storage → Packaging → Cold storage (≤ 4 °C) → Distribution → Consumer.

**Identified CCPs (after applying decision tree):**

| CCP | Process Step | Hazard Controlled | Critical Limit | Monitoring Frequency | Corrective Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **CCP-1** | Pasteurization | Pathogens (*M. tuberculosis*, *Salmonella*, *Listeria*) | **72 °C × 15 s** | Continuous (chart recorder) | Re-pasteurize; if held > 30 min above 72 °C, divert to UHT or destroy |
| **CCP-2** | Metal detection | Metal fragments | Fe ≤ 2.0 mm, Non-Fe ≤ 2.5 mm, SS ≤ 3.0 mm | Continuous; test piece every hour | Reject, segregate, investigate source |
| **CCP-3** | Cold storage | *Listeria monocytogenes* growth | **≤ 4 °C** | Continuous data logger | Transfer to back-up chiller; assess product disposition based on duration |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master HACCP Implementation Flow (12 Steps + 7 Principles)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Step 1: Assemble HACCP Team"] --> B["Step 2: Describe Product"]
    B --> C["Step 3: Identify Intended Use"]
    C --> D["Step 4: Construct Flow Diagram"]
    D --> E["Step 5: On-Site Verification of Flow Diagram"]
    E --> P1["Principle 1: Conduct Hazard Analysis"]
    P1 --> P2["Principle 2: Determine CCPs using Decision Tree"]
    P2 --> P3["Principle 3: Establish Critical Limits"]
    P3 --> P4["Principle 4: Establish Monitoring Procedures"]
    P4 --> P5["Principle 5: Establish Corrective Actions"]
    P5 --> P6["Principle 6: Establish Verification Procedures"]
    P6 --> P7["Principle 7: Establish Record-Keeping and Documentation"]
    P7 --> OUT["Continual Improvement and Review"]
    OUT -.-> P1

    style A fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0
    style B fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0
    style C fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0
    style D fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0
    style E fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0
    style P1 fill:#ffebee,stroke:#c62828
    style P2 fill:#ffebee,stroke:#c62828
    style P3 fill:#ffebee,stroke:#c62828
    style P4 fill:#ffebee,stroke:#c62828
    style P5 fill:#ffebee,stroke:#c62828
    style P6 fill:#ffebee,stroke:#c62828
    style P7 fill:#ffebee,stroke:#c62828
    style OUT fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32
```

> **Color Key:** 🟦 Blue = Preliminary Steps (1–5), 🟥 Red = 7 Principles (Codex), 🟩 Green = Continual Improvement Loop.

---

## 4.2 Codex CCP Decision Tree (Standard Examination Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1["Q1: Do preventive control measures exist for this hazard?"] -->|NO| M1["Modify step, process, or product. Apply PRPs. Re-evaluate."] --> Q1
    Q1 -->|YES| Q2["Q2: Does this step eliminate or reduce the hazard to an acceptable level?"]
    Q2 -->|YES| CCP["YES - This IS a CCP"]
    Q2 -->|NO| Q3["Q3: Could contamination occur in excess of acceptable level, or increase to unacceptable level?"]
    Q3 -->|NO| NOTCCP["NOT a CCP"]
    Q3 -->|YES| Q4["Q4: Will a subsequent step eliminate or reduce the hazard to an acceptable level?"]
    Q4 -->|YES| NOTCCP
    Q4 -->|NO| CCP

    style Q1 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style Q2 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style Q3 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style Q4 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style CCP fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style NOTCCP fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c
    style M1 fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b
```

---

## 4.3 Hazard Analysis Process Flow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H1["Raw Material Receipt"] --> H2["Sorting and Cleaning"]
    H2 --> H3["Blanching or Pre-cooking"]
    H3 --> H4["Main Cooking or Pasteurization"]
    H4 --> H5["Cooling"]
    H5 --> H6["Packaging"]
    H6 --> H7["Storage and Distribution"]

    H4 -.->|CCP-1 Temperature Time| CCP1["Critical Control Point 1"]
    H6 -.->|CCP-2 Metal Detection| CCP2["Critical Control Point 2"]
    H7 -.->|CCP-3 Cold Chain| CCP3["Critical Control Point 3"]

    style H1 fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c
    style H2 fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c
    style H3 fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c
    style H4 fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c
    style H5 fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c
    style H6 fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c
    style H7 fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c
    style CCP1 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style CCP2 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style CCP3 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
```

---

## 4.4 Relationship Diagram — PRPs, oPRPs, and CCPs

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PRP["Prerequisite Programmes (PRPs) - GMP, GHP, Sanitation, Pest Control, Personal Hygiene, Training"] --> oPRP["Operational PRPs (oPRPs) - Control measures for significant hazards NOT classified as CCPs"]
    PRP --> CCP["Critical Control Points (CCPs) - Mandatory control measures for HIGH risk hazards"]
    oPRP -->|Continuous improvement| REV["Management Review and Verification"]
    CCP -->|Monitoring, corrective action, verification| REV
    REV -->|Update| PRP
    REV -->|Update| oPRP
    REV -->|Update| CCP

    style PRP fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1
    style oPRP fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17
    style CCP fill:#ef9a9a,stroke:#b71c1c
    style REV fill:#c5e1a5,stroke:#33691e
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, PCFTT503]**
> **Q1.** Define **HACCP**. Name the organization that developed the **seven principles of HACCP**.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **HACCP** stands for **Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Point**. It is a systematic, science-based, preventive food safety management system that identifies, evaluates, and controls hazards which are significant for food safety.
> The **seven principles** of HACCP were formalized by the **Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC)** in **1993** (revised in 1997 and 2020), a joint body of the **Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO)** and the **World Health Organization (WHO)** of the United Nations. The seven principles are: (1) Hazard Analysis, (2) Determine CCPs, (3) Establish Critical Limits, (4) Establish Monitoring Procedures, (5) Establish Corrective Actions, (6) Establish Verification Procedures, and (7) Establish Record-Keeping and Documentation. *(3 marks — full distribution: 1 for definition, 1 for naming CAC, 1 for listing principles.)*

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, PCFTT503]**
> **Q2.** Differentiate between **PRP (Prerequisite Programme)** and **CCP (Critical Control Point)**.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Feature | PRP | CCP |
> |---|---|---|
> | Full form | Prerequisite Programme | Critical Control Point |
> | Purpose | Foundational hygiene & sanitation conditions (GMP, GHP, pest control) | Mandatory control for HIGH-risk food safety hazards |
> | Mandatory under | Codex, ISO 22000, FSSAI | HACCP system, FSSAI Schedule 4 |
> | Failure consequence | Reduces overall risk but is not the last line of defence | Direct food safety failure → product recall, illness |
> | Example | Hand-washing, floor sanitation, personnel training | Pasteurization temperature, metal detection, cold chain |
> | Critical Limit | Not always quantitative | Always has a quantitative or qualitative CL |
>
> *(3 marks — table format with 2–3 distinct differences earns full credit.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, PCFTT503 — Internal Choice Set]**
>
> ### 🅰️ Question A (14 Marks) — *Principle-Focused Question*
>
> **Q3(a).** Explain the **seven principles of HACCP** as per the **Codex Alimentarius Commission**. *(7 marks)*
>
> **Model Solution (7 marks):**
>
> The seven principles of HACCP, established by the **Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC/RCP 1-1969, Rev. 2020)**, are the operational core of any HACCP system. They must be applied **after** the five preliminary steps (team formation, product description, intended use identification, flow diagram, and on-site verification) have been completed.
>
> **Principle 1 — Hazard Analysis (HA):** A comprehensive identification of all potential **biological, chemical, physical** (and in modern Codex: radiological and allergen) hazards associated with raw materials, processing steps, packaging, storage, and distribution. Each hazard is evaluated for **severity** and **likelihood**, and preventive control measures are identified. A hazard is considered "significant" only if its elimination or reduction is essential to food safety.
>
> **Principle 2 — Determination of Critical Control Points (CCPs):** Using the **Codex CCP Decision Tree**, the HACCP team identifies those process steps where control is **essential** to prevent, eliminate, or reduce a significant hazard to an acceptable level. CCPs are the most critical control nodes in the entire process flow.
>
> **Principle 3 — Establish Critical Limits (CLs):** For each CCP, **quantitative or qualitative** criteria are defined that separate acceptable from unacceptable operation. Examples: pasteurization at **72 °C for 15 seconds**, cold storage at **≤ 4 °C**, water activity **< 0.85**, pH **< 4.6**.
>
> **Principle 4 — Establish Monitoring Procedures:** A defined schedule of **observations or measurements** to assess whether each CCP remains within its critical limit. Monitoring must specify **what, how, when, who, and where** — and is ideally **continuous**.
>
> **Principle 5 — Establish Corrective Actions:** **Pre-planned actions** to be taken when monitoring shows a **deviation** from the critical limit. These include product isolation, disposition (destroy / rework / release), root-cause analysis, and preventive re-occurrence measures.
>
> **Principle 6 — Establish Verification Procedures:** Independent checks to confirm that the HACCP system is **working effectively**. Includes internal audits, end-product testing, equipment calibration, and review of records. Verification must be performed by someone **other than** the person who performed monitoring.
>
> **Principle 7 — Establish Record-Keeping and Documentation:** All HACCP activities — including hazard analysis, CCP determination, critical limits, monitoring, corrective actions, and verification — must be **documented and retained** for at least one year past the shelf life of perishable products (FSSAI Schedule 4).
>
> *(Award 1 mark per principle, with the final mark reserved for "Codex Alimentarius" naming.)*
>
> ---
>
> **Q3(b).** Describe the **Codex CCP Decision Tree** and apply it to determine whether **metal detection** in a packaged juice line is a CCP. *(7 marks)*
>
> **Model Solution (7 marks):**
>
> **The Codex CCP Decision Tree (3 marks for diagram + description):**
>
> The Codex CCP Decision Tree is a four-question logical flow used to systematically determine whether a particular process step is a CCP.
>
> - **Question 1:** Do preventive control measures exist for the identified hazard? If **NO**, modify the process/product or strengthen PRPs and **re-evaluate**. If **YES**, proceed.
> - **Question 2:** Does this step eliminate or reduce the hazard to an acceptable level? If **YES** → **CCP**. If **NO**, proceed.
> - **Question 3:** Could contamination occur in excess of acceptable level, or could it increase to unacceptable level? If **NO** → **Not a CCP**. If **YES**, proceed.
> - **Question 4:** Will a subsequent step eliminate or reduce the hazard to an acceptable level? If **YES** → **Not a CCP**. If **NO** → **CCP**.
>
> **Application to Metal Detection in a Packaged Juice Line (4 marks):**
>
> - **Hazard:** Metal fragment (e.g., 3 mm stainless steel shavings from filling nozzle wear) — **Physical hazard**.
> - **Q1: Do preventive measures exist?** ✅ Yes — metal detector at the end of the line.
> - **Q2: Does this step eliminate or reduce the hazard to an acceptable level?** ✅ Yes — the detector physically **identifies and rejects** any metal-contaminated package, reducing the hazard to acceptable level (zero metal fragment reaching the consumer).
> - **Conclusion (Q2 reached):** → **This IS a CCP** (CCP-2 in a typical juice HACCP plan).
>
> **Justification:** Metal fragments can cause choking, laceration, or perforation of the gastrointestinal tract — severity is classified as **Major (S = 3)**, and although likelihood is **Remote (L = 2)**, regulatory standards (FSSAI Schedule 4 and BRC Issue 9) **mandate** metal detection independent of numerical risk. Therefore, metal detection is designated as a **CCP** with critical limits of **Fe ≤ 2.0 mm, Non-Fe ≤ 2.5 mm, SS ≤ 3.0 mm**, monitored continuously with a test piece verification every hour.
>
> *(Full 7 marks: 3 for tree + 1 for hazard identification + 1 for correct path through tree + 1 for conclusion + 1 for critical limits.)*

---

> ### 🅱️ Question B (14 Marks) — *Application-Focused Question*
>
> **Q4(a).** List and briefly explain the **five preliminary steps** that must be completed before applying the seven principles of HACCP. *(7 marks)*
>
> **Model Solution (7 marks):**
>
> The five preliminary steps form the **foundation** of the HACCP system and ensure that the seven principles are applied on a verified, contextual basis.
>
> **Preliminary Step 1 — Assemble the HACCP Team:** A multidisciplinary team is formed with expertise in production, sanitation, microbiology, chemistry, engineering, quality assurance, and food regulation. The team must be trained in HACCP methodology. *(1.5 marks)*
>
> **Preliminary Step 2 — Describe the Product:** A complete product description is prepared, covering composition, raw material origin, processing/packaging methods, **pH**, water activity (**a_w**), preservatives, storage/distribution conditions, shelf life, and intended use. *(1.5 marks)*
>
> **Preliminary Step 3 — Identify the Intended Use:** The team identifies the consumer (general public, infants, immunocompromised, elderly) and the manner of consumption (raw, cooked, reheated) so that hazard analysis is correctly targeted. *(1 mark)*
>
> **Preliminary Step 4 — Construct the Process Flow Diagram:** A **block-by-block** schematic is drawn covering every step from raw material receipt to consumer use, including receiving, storage, sorting, washing, blanching, cooking, cooling, packaging, labeling, storage, and distribution. *(1.5 marks)*
>
> **Preliminary Step 5 — On-Site Verification of the Flow Diagram:** The HACCP team **physically walks** the production facility during operating hours to verify that the drawn flow diagram accurately reflects reality. The verified diagram is **dated and signed**. *(1.5 marks)*
>
> ---
>
> **Q4(b).** Construct a **HACCP plan** for **pasteurized milk**, identifying at least **three CCPs** with critical limits, monitoring procedures, and corrective actions. *(7 marks)*
>
> **Model Solution (7 marks):**
>
> **Product:** Pasteurized Toned Milk (Cow milk 80% + Skimmed milk powder 20%; Fat ≥ 3.0%, SNF ≥ 8.5%; Shelf life: 7 days at ≤ 4 °C).
>
> **Process Flow:** Raw milk receipt → Pre-heating → Standardization → Pasteurization (72 °C × 15 s) → Cooling to ≤ 4 °C → Storage → Packaging (pouch) → Cold storage (≤ 4 °C) → Distribution → Consumer.
>
> **Identified CCPs (after applying Codex Decision Tree):**
>
> | # | CCP Step | Hazard | Critical Limit | Monitoring | Corrective Action |
> |---|---|---|---|---|---|
> | **CCP-1** | Pasteurization | Pathogen survival (*M. tuberculosis*, *Salmonella*, *Listeria*) | **72 °C × 15 s** | Continuous chart recorder + alarm | Re-pasteurize; if held > 30 min above 72 °C → divert to UHT or destroy; investigate heat exchanger |
> | **CCP-2** | Metal detection (at packaging) | Metal fragments | Fe ≤ 2.0 mm, Non-Fe ≤ 2.5 mm, SS ≤ 3.0 mm | Continuous detector; test piece every 1 hour | Reject, segregate, hold batch; investigate filler wear; reset detector |
> | **CCP-3** | Cold storage | *Listeria monocytogenes* growth | **≤ 4 °C** | Continuous data logger with SMS alert | Transfer to back-up chiller; assess product disposition based on temperature-time integration; recall if > 7 °C for > 2 hr |
>
> **Verification:** Daily chart review, weekly plate count of finished product, monthly calibration of thermometer and metal detector, annual internal audit.
>
> **Records:** Pasteurization chart, metal detector log, cold chain log, corrective action log, calibration log, training records — retained for 1 year past shelf life per FSSAI Schedule 4.
>
> *(Award 2 marks for the process flow, 3 marks for the three CCPs with CLs, 1 mark for monitoring procedures, and 1 mark for corrective actions.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing preliminary steps with the 7 principles.** Preliminary steps (1–5) are **not** the principles. Writing "5 Principles of HACCP" instead of "7 Principles" costs **at least 2 marks**.
> 2. **Drawing the CCP Decision Tree incorrectly.** The four questions must be in the **Codex sequence** (Q1 → Q2 → Q3 → Q4). Inverting Q3 and Q4 is a common error that loses 1.5 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting quantitative critical limits.** Writing "monitor temperature" instead of "**72 °C for 15 s**" loses 1 mark per CCP.
> 4. **Omitting corrective actions.** A CCP without a **pre-defined corrective action** is incomplete and loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Conflating PRP with CCP.** Metal detection is a CCP; floor sanitation is a PRP. This distinction is **specifically tested** in KTU papers.
> 6. **Missing regulatory references.** Citing **Codex Alimentarius CAC/RCP 1-1969, Rev. 2020** and **FSSAI Schedule 4 of 2011 Regulations** shows examiner awareness and earns +0.5 bonus impression marks.
> 7. **Not differentiating oPRP from CCP.** In ISO 22000:2018, an **operational PRP (oPRP)** controls a significant hazard but is **not** a CCP. Mentioning this nuance (where relevant) impresses the examiner.
> 8. **Spelling errors:** "Hazzard" instead of "Hazard", "Criticle" instead of "Critical" — these are flagged in valuation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for HACCP**
>
> - ✅ **HACCP** = Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Point — a **preventive** food safety system.
> - ✅ Developed by **Pillsbury + NASA** in **1959**; codified by **Codex Alimentarius Commission (FAO/WHO)** in **1993** (rev. 1997, 2020).
> - ✅ **Three hazard classes:** Biological, Chemical, Physical (Codex 2020 also: Radiological, Allergens).
> - ✅ **5 Preliminary Steps:** (1) Assemble team, (2) Describe product, (3) Identify intended use, (4) Construct flow diagram, (5) On-site verification.
> - ✅ **7 Principles:** (1) Hazard Analysis, (2) Determine CCPs, (3) Critical Limits, (4) Monitoring, (5) Corrective Actions, (6) Verification, (7) Records.
> - ✅ **CCP Decision Tree** has exactly **4 questions** in the **Codex order**.
> - ✅ **Critical Limits** must be **quantitative or qualitative**; examples: 72 °C × 15 s, ≤ 4 °C, a_w < 0.85, pH < 4.6, Fe ≤ 2.0 mm.
> - ✅ **Monitoring** must specify **what, how, when, who, where** — preferably continuous.
> - ✅ **Corrective actions** must be **pre-planned**, not improvised after a deviation.
> - ✅ **Verification** is performed by an **independent person** (not the monitor).
> - ✅ **Records** retained for **≥ 1 year past shelf life** (perishable) or **≥ 2 years** (non-perishable) — per **FSSAI 2011 Schedule 4**.
> - ✅ **PRP** (Prerequisite Programme) ≠ **oPRP** (Operational PRP) ≠ **CCP** (Critical Control Point) — three-tier hierarchy in **ISO 22000:2018**.
> - ✅ **Risk Score** $R = S \times L$; CCP if **R ≥ 15** OR mandated by regulation (e.g., metal detection, pasteurization).
> - ✅ **HACCP is mandatory** under **FSSAI Schedule 4 (2011)**, **Codex CAC/RCP 1-1969 Rev. 2020**, **ISO 22000:2018**, **BRC v9**, **SQF Ed. 9**.
> - ✅ **Difference between HACCP and ISO 22000:** HACCP = 7 principles only; ISO 22000 = HACCP + PRPs + Management Responsibility + PDCA cycle.
> - ✅ **Difference between oPRP and CCP:** oPRP controls a significant hazard but **is not the last line of defence**; CCP **IS** the last line of defence and must have all four elements (CL, monitoring, CA, verification).
> - ✅ **Common KTU exam traps:** (a) calling HACCP a "quality system" — it is a **food safety** system; (b) confusing severity with risk; (c) treating the **decision tree as a checklist rather than a logical flow**; (d) skipping Principle 7 (records).

**End of Module 4 — HACCP Notes** 🎯
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Good Animal Husbandry Practices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCFTT503 - FOOD SAFETY AND QUALITY REGULATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Good Animal Husbandry Practices # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Good Animal Husbandry Practices (GAHP)** are the foundational, science-based, and principle-driven management practices applied at the **primary production stage** of the food chain to ensure that animals raised for food (livestock, poultry, fish, and bees) are healthy, well-cared for, and free from contaminants — thereby safeguarding the safety and suitability of meat, milk, eggs, honey, and derived products for human consumption. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** GAHP forms the **first link** in the farm-to-fork food safety continuum. It is the terrestrial counterpart to GAP (Good Agricultural Practices) and is regulated internationally by the **Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC/RCP)**, the **World Organisation for Animal Health (WOAH, formerly OIE)**, and the **FAO**. The **Codex Alimentarius Commission** defines GAHP as those practices that: 1. Reduce the risk of biological, chemical, and physical hazards entering the food chain. 2. Promote animal health and welfare. 3. Protect the environment from animal production-related contamination. 4. Ensure traceability from farm to consumer. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hospital-Farm" Model > [!NOTE] > **Think of a modern dairy farm as a "food-producing hospital ward."** > Just like a hospital cannot afford contaminated instruments, unsterile wards, or unqualified staff, a farm cannot afford sick animals, contaminated feed, dirty sheds, or untrained handlers — because every defect at the farm **magnifies exponentially** as it travels up the supply chain. A single mastitis-infected cow can contaminate an entire 5,000-litre milk tanker; a single antibiotic residue in a broiler flock can trigger a trans-national export ban. In simpler words: - **Cow = Living bioreactor** → Its health **directly** determines the safety of milk, meat, or eggs. - **Feed & Water = Raw materials** → Just like in a food factory, contaminated inputs → contaminated outputs. - **Shed/Housing = Clean room** → Hygiene of housing prevents microbial load buildup. - **Veterinarian = Quality Manager** → Ensures drug withdrawal periods are respected, acting like the QA Head in FSSAI 22000. ## 1.3 The Four Pillars of GAHP | Pillar | Focus Area | KTU Mnemonic | |---|---|---| | **Pillar 1** | Animal Health Management | **"H"** – Health | | **Pillar 2** | Feed & Water Safety | **"F"** – Feed | | **Pillar 3** | Housing, Hygiene & Sanitation | **"S"** – Shelter | | **Pillar 4** | Traceability, Record & Training | **"R"** – Records | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PCFTT503 M4):** The 2024 scheme explicitly maps GAHP to **CO4** — *"Apply principles of food safety management at primary production."* Questions are frequently asked linking GAHP failures to **foodborne outbreaks** (e.g., the 2008 Chinese melamine scandal, the 2017 EU fipronil egg crisis). ## 1.4 Scope of GAHP GAHP applies to: - **Dairy animals** — cattle, buffalo, goat, sheep. - **Meat animals** — poultry, swine, bovine, small ruminants. - **Aquaculture** — fish, shrimp, prawn farming. - **Apiary** — honey bees. - **Egg layers & breeders** — poultry. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — The 7 Domain Logic Steps The **FAO/WHO/Codex framework** consolidates GAHP into **seven inter-locking domains**. Each domain addresses a specific hazard class and links to a downstream food safety control. ### Domain 1 — Animal Identification & Traceability **Why:** Enables rapid recall, disease tracking, and residue attribution. **How:** - Each animal/herd/batch must carry a unique identifier (ear tag, microchip, RFID, wing band). - Records must include: date of birth, source, breed, parentage, movements. - Retention period: minimum **3–5 years** (varies by jurisdiction; in India, FSSAI mandates traceability per **Food Safety and Standards (Food Recall) Regulations, 2017**). ### Domain 2 — Animal Health Management **Why:** Sick animals → zoonotic pathogens (Salmonella, Brucella, TB, Campylobacter) and residues. **How:** - Implement **Herd Health Programme (HHP)** with scheduled veterinary visits. - **Vaccination calendar** must be documented (e.g., FMD, HS, BQ, Brucellosis, ND, Marek's, Gumboro). - Quarantine of new arrivals for a minimum of **21–28 days**. - Separation of sick animals from healthy herd. - **Drug Withdrawal Period (DWP)** must be strictly observed before slaughter/milk dispatch. ### Domain 3 — Feed and Water Safety **Why:** Feed is the **largest input** by mass; it carries pesticides, mycotoxins, heavy metals, pathogens, and prohibited antibiotics. **How:** - Source feed from **approved/bureau-verified** suppliers. - Check for **aflatoxin B1** (limit: **20 µg/kg** for dairy feed in India; **5 µg/kg** in EU for dairy). - Store feed in **dry, ventilated, rodent-proof** silos. - Water must be **potable** (tested for coliforms — **0 MPN/100 mL** for drinking water). - **No** use of **growth-promoting antibiotics**, **ractopamine**, or **hormones** (banned under EU, Codex MRLs). ### Domain 4 — Veterinary Drugs & Chemical Use **Why:** Residues in milk/meat/eggs cause **antimicrobial resistance (AMR)** and direct toxicity. **How:** - Use only **prescription drugs** from licensed veterinarians. - Maintain a **drug register**: drug name, dose, route, animal ID, date, withdrawal period. - Adhere to **Maximum Residue Limits (MRLs)** set by **Codex/FSSAI**. - Common residue violations: **oxytetracycline, enrofloxacin, chloramphenicol, nitrofurans**. ### Domain 5 — Housing, Hygiene & Sanitation **Why:** Reduces environmental microbial load; prevents cross-contamination. **How:** - **All-in / All-out** system preferred for poultry. - Floor, walls, feeders cleaned and disinfected between batches. - Manure management: composting, biogas, or covered storage; never raw run-off into water bodies. - Pest control: **IPM (Integrated Pest Management)** — physical > biological > chemical. - Stocking density as per **BIS/FAO** standards (e.g., broilers: max **30–40 kg live weight/m²**). ### Domain 6 — Animal Welfare **Why:** Stress reduces immunity → increases shedding of pathogens (Salmonella, E. coli); directly linked to food safety and meat quality (**PSE, DFD meat**). **How:** - Comply with **"Five Freedoms"** (Brambell Committee, 1965 — adopted by WOAH): 1. Freedom from hunger and thirst 2. Freedom from discomfort 3. Freedom from pain, injury, disease 4. Freedom to express normal behaviour 5. Freedom from fear and distress - Avoid overcrowding, rough handling, transport stress. ### Domain 7 — Environmental & Waste Management **Why:** Prevents zoonotic spillover and antimicrobial resistance in the environment. **How:** - Proper disposal of **carcasses** (incineration, burial, rendering). - **Pharmaceutical waste** segregation; no disposal into water bodies. - Greenhouse gas (methane) mitigation through improved feed efficiency. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Metrics Table | Parameter | Standard / Threshold | Regulatory Body | Application | |---|---|---|---| | Aflatoxin M1 in milk | **0.5 µg/kg** | Codex / FSSAI | Carry-over from feed | | Aflatoxin B1 in feed | **20 µg/kg** | BIS / EU 5 µg/kg | Dairy cattle feed | | Lead in meat | **0.1 mg/kg** | FSSAI | Environmental contaminant | | Drug Withdrawal Period | Species-specific (e.g., oxytetracycline = **7 days** milk) | FSSAI / Codex | Residue avoidance | | Total plate count (raw milk) | **≤ 2 × 10⁵ CFU/mL** | FSSAI | Hygiene indicator | | Coliforms in potable water | **0 MPN / 100 mL** | BIS 10500 | Farm water supply | | Stocking density (broiler) | **30–40 kg/m²** | EU Directive 2007/43 | Welfare | | Storage temp (refrigerated meat) | **≤ 4 °C** | FSSAI | Cold chain | | pH of meat (post-rigor) | **5.4 – 5.8** | Industry standard | Quality | | Salm. in 25 g (poultry carcass) | **Absent** | FSSAI | Microbiological standard | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked "list two hazards controlled by GAHP," always classify them as **Biological** (Salmonella, Brucella), **Chemical** (residues, mycotoxins), or **Physical** (broken needles, metal fragments). This **B-C-P classification** is a guaranteed scoring differentiator. ## 2.3 Real-World Utility - **For Dairy Industry:** GAHP directly reduces **Somatic Cell Count (SCC)** in milk; bulk milk SCC < **4,00,000 cells/mL** is the EU benchmark for premium quality. - **For Export:** Compliance with **EU Regulation 853/2004** (Hygiene Package) and **US-FSIS** requirements demands documented GAHP — non-compliance triggers import bans. - **For Public Health:** WHO estimates **600 million** foodborne illness cases/year globally; ~**30%** originate at the **farm level**, preventable by GAHP. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Framework — From Hazard to Control ## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — The Hazard–Control Mapping Unlike pure engineering subjects, GAHP is a **qualitative-quantitative hybrid discipline**. The "derivation" here is the **logical deduction of how a farm hazard becomes a food safety failure** and which GAHP element neutralises it. ### Step 1 — Hazard Identification at Primary Production $$\boxed{H_{farm} = \{B, C, P\}}$$ Where: - $H_{farm}$ = Total hazard pool at farm - $B$ = Biological hazards (Salmonella enterica, Campylobacter jejuni, Listeria monocytogenes, Brucella abortus, Mycobacterium bovis, E. coli O157:H7, parasites like Taenia, Trichinella) - $C$ = Chemical hazards (Veterinary drug residues, mycotoxins, pesticides, heavy metals, dioxins, PCBs) - $P$ = Physical hazards (Broken needles, metal shavings, glass, bone fragments) > Each item is mapped to the **Codex Alimentarius CAC/RCP 1-1969** hazard classification. ### Step 2 — Risk Pathway Analysis (Farm-to-Fork Chain) $$\text{Risk} = P(\text{Hazard}) \times S(\text{Severity})$$ Where: - $P(\text{Hazard})$ = Probability of occurrence at farm - $S(\text{Severity})$ = Health impact magnitude (mild → fatal) **Example pathway trace:** $$\text{Contaminated Feed} \xrightarrow{\text{intake}} \text{Animal Tissue} \xrightarrow{\text{slaughter}} \text{Meat} \xrightarrow{\text{retail}} \text{Consumer}$$ **Logical Deduction (expanded):** ``` Step A: Aflatoxin B1 in maize feed (concentration 50 µg/kg) ↓ exceeds BIS limit (20 µg/kg) Step B: Cattle ingest contaminated feed ↓ biotransformation in liver (cytochrome P450) Step C: Aflatoxin M1 secreted in milk (carry-over ratio ≈ 1-3%) ↓ Step D: Milk aflatoxin M1 = 0.5-1.5 µg/kg ↓ exceeds Codex limit (0.5 µg/kg) Step E: Consumer exposure → hepatotoxicity, carcinogenicity ↓ Step F: GAHP Control → Source control + binder (HSCAS) in feed ``` ### Step 3 — Quantitative Carry-Over Calculation (MRL Linked) The **Transfer Coefficient (TC)** from feed to milk for aflatoxin: $$TC_{milk} = \frac{C_{milk}}{C_{feed}}$$ Where: - $C_{milk}$ = Aflatoxin M1 concentration in milk (µg/kg) - $C_{feed}$ = Aflatoxin B1 concentration in feed (µg/kg) **Worked Example:** Given: - $C_{feed} = 20 \, \mu g/kg$ (regulatory ceiling) - $TC_{milk} \approx 0.015$ (literature value for dairy cows) - Codex MRL = $0.5 \, \mu g/kg$ Compute: $$C_{milk} = TC_{milk} \times C_{feed} = 0.015 \times 20$$ $$C_{milk} = 0.3 \, \mu g/kg$$ Since $0.3 < 0.5$, the regulatory ceiling for feed **just** keeps milk safe. **Any increase in feed contamination immediately breaches milk MRL.** ### Step 4 — Antimicrobial Resistance (AMR) Burden Estimation The **Antimicrobial Usage (AMU)** metric for a farm is: $$AMU_{farm} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} D_i \times W_i \times T_i$$ Where: - $D_i$ = Dose of drug $i$ (mg/kg body weight) - $W_i$ = Total live weight of animals treated with drug $i$ (kg) - $T_i$ = Treatment duration (days) - $n$ = Number of drugs used **Interpretation:** Rising $AMU_{farm}$ correlates linearly with on-farm selection pressure for resistant bacteria. KTU expects students to **qualitatively** link this to **WHO's AMR action plan (2015)** and **FSSAI's ban on colistin (2019)** in food animals. ### Step 5 — Control Hierarchy (The "HACCP at Farm Level") In alignment with the **farm-to-table continuum**, GAHP control follows a **four-tier hierarchy**: $$\text{Eliminate} > \text{Substitute} > \text{Engineer Control} > \text{PPE/Admin}$$ Applied to a typical farm: | Tier | Hazard | Control | |---|---|---| | 1. Eliminate | Pathogen entry | Closed herd, all-in/all-out | | 2. Substitute | Risky drug | Use vaccine instead of antibiotic | | 3. Engineering | Air/dust | Ventilation, HEPA filters | | 4. Administrative | Handler hygiene | Training, gloves, footbaths | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Logic:** GAHP precedes **HACCP**. HACCP cannot be implemented on a farm with poor GAHP — it is built **on top of** GAHP. This is a frequently tested conceptual point. --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — GAHP Domain Interlink ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Farm Inputs
Animals Feed Water Drugs] --> B[GAHP Domain 1
Identification Traceability] A --> C[GAHP Domain 2
Animal Health Mgmt] A --> D[GAHP Domain 3
Feed Water Safety] A --> E[GAHP Domain 4
Veterinary Drug Control] B --> F[GAHP Domain 5
Housing Hygiene Sanitation] C --> F D --> F E --> F F --> G[GAHP Domain 6
Animal Welfare] G --> H[GAHP Domain 7
Environment Waste Mgmt] H --> I{Safe Primary
Product Output} I --> J[Milk] I --> K[Meat] I --> L[Eggs] I --> M[Honey] I --> N[Fish] J --> O[Food Safety Continuum
Processing Distribution Retail] K --> O L --> O M --> O N --> O O --> P[Consumer
Safe Food] style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style F fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style I fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style O fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style P fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` **Reading the Diagram:** - **Layer 1 (Inputs)** — All raw materials entering the farm. - **Layer 2 (Core Domains 1–4)** — Direct hazard controls on inputs. - **Layer 3 (Domain 5)** — Integrated environment where input controls converge. - **Layer 4 (Domains 6–7)** — Welfare and environmental stewardship. - **Layer 5 (Output Gate)** — Decision diamond: only GAHP-compliant primary products pass. - **Layer 6 (Downstream)** — Processing, distribution, retail. - **Layer 7 (End)** — Safe food at consumer level. ## 4.2 Sequence Diagram — Drug Residue Avoidance Workflow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Vet as Veterinarian participant Farm as Farm Manager participant Drug as Drug Register participant Animal as Animal / Flock participant QC as Quality Check Pre-Slaughter participant Market as Milk / Meat Market Vet->>Farm: Diagnose & prescribe Farm->>Drug: Log drug, dose, animal ID, date Drug->>Farm: Compute withdrawal period Farm->>Animal: Administer treatment Note over Animal: Observe withdrawal period Animal->>QC: Ready for dispatch QC->>Drug: Verify withdrawal period elapsed? alt Compliant QC->>Market: APPROVE dispatch else Non-compliant QC->>Farm: REJECT, extend hold end ``` **Key Insight:** The withdrawal period check is the **last line of defence** against chemical residues entering the food chain — failure here causes export rejections and consumer harm. ## 4.3 Risk Heat Map (Block Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph B[Biological Hazards] B1[Salmonella] B2[Brucella] B3[Campylobacter] B4[E coli O157] end subgraph C[Chemical Hazards] C1[Antibiotic Residues] C2[Mycotoxins] C3[Pesticides] C4[Heavy Metals] end subgraph P[Physical Hazards] P1[Broken Needles] P2[Metal Fragments] end subgraph CTRL[GAHP Controls] CTRL1[Vaccination] CTRL2[DWP Compliance] CTRL3[Feed Testing] CTRL4[Water Testing] CTRL5[Training Hygiene] CTRL6[PPE Equipment] end B1 --> CTRL1 B2 --> CTRL1 B3 --> CTRL1 B4 --> CTRL5 C1 --> CTRL2 C2 --> CTRL3 C3 --> CTRL3 C4 --> CTRL4 P1 --> CTRL6 P2 --> CTRL6 style B fill:#FFB6C1 style C fill:#FFD700 style P fill:#D3D3D3 style CTRL fill:#90EE90 ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Define Good Animal Husbandry Practices (GAHP). List any four key principles.** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** Good Animal Husbandry Practices (GAHP) are the science-based management principles applied at the primary production stage to ensure animals raised for food are healthy, traceable, and free from biological, chemical, and physical hazards, thereby producing safe and suitable food for human consumption. **Four Key Principles:** 1. **Animal identification and traceability** — unique tagging and movement records. 2. **Animal health management** — vaccination, disease surveillance, quarantine. 3. **Feed and water safety** — sourcing from approved suppliers, mycotoxin and microbial testing. 4. **Hygiene and sanitation** — clean housing, pest control, all-in/all-out systems. *[Examiner note: CO4 mapped; Bloom Level: Remember/Understand; 3 marks split as 1+2.]* --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **What is the "drug withdrawal period"? Why is it critical in GAHP?** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** The drug withdrawal period (DWP) is the minimum time interval that must elapse between the **last administration** of a veterinary drug to a food-producing animal and the **slaughter or collection of milk/eggs** for human consumption. It is the time required for the drug residue to fall **below the Maximum Residue Limit (MRL)**. **Why critical:** 1. Prevents **violative residues** (antibiotics, anthelmintics) in meat, milk, and eggs. 2. Ensures compliance with **Codex MRLs** and **FSSAI** standards. 3. Protects consumers from **antimicrobial resistance (AMR)** and allergic reactions. 4. Prevents **export rejections** and trade bans. **Example:** Oxytetracycline milk withdrawal = **7 days**; Enrofloxacin poultry = **7–10 days** depending on label. *[Examiner note: CO4; Bloom Level: Understand; 3 marks: 1.5 definition + 1.5 significance.]* --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### INTERNAL CHOICE — Answer ANY ONE --- ## Question A `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]` **(a)** Explain in detail the **seven domains of GAHP** as per the Codex/FAO framework, with examples of hazards controlled under each domain. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the role of **Good Animal Husbandry Practices in the prevention of foodborne zoonoses**, citing at least **three named zoonoses** with their pathogen, reservoir, transmission route, and GAHP control measure. **(7 marks)** ### Model Answer — Question A **(a) Seven Domains of GAHP (7 marks):** | # | Domain | Example Hazard | GAHP Control | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Animal identification & traceability | Disease outbreak spread | Ear tagging, RFID, herd register | | 2 | Animal health | Brucellosis, FMD | Vaccination, vet visits, quarantine | | 3 | Feed & water safety | Aflatoxin B1, coliforms | Approved supplier, mycotoxin testing, BIS water testing | | 4 | Veterinary drugs | Antibiotic residues | DWP compliance, drug register, MRL adherence | | 5 | Housing & hygiene | Salmonella, Campylobacter | All-in/all-out, disinfection, ventilation | | 6 | Animal welfare | Stress-induced shedding | Five Freedoms, low stocking density | | 7 | Environment & waste | AMR spread, water pollution | Carcass disposal, manure management | *(1 mark per domain; conceptual depth required for full marks.)* --- **(b) GAHP in Prevention of Foodborne Zoonoses (7 marks):** **Three zoonoses:** **1. Salmonellosis (2.3 marks)** - **Pathogen:** *Salmonella enterica* (non-typhoidal) - **Reservoir:** Poultry, pigs, cattle intestines - **Transmission:** Faecal → carcass → meat/eggs → human; undercooked poultry. - **GAHP Control:** - All-in/all-out broiler systems - Rodent & pest control - Clean water supply - Vaccination of layers against *Salmonella* Enteritidis - Cold chain maintenance from farm to retail. **2. Brucellosis (2.3 marks)** - **Pathogen:** *Brucella abortus*, *B. melitensis* - **Reservoir:** Cattle, goats, sheep - **Transmission:** Unpasteurised milk, aborted foetuses, contact with placenta. - **GAHP Control:** - **RB-51 / S-19 vaccination** of calves - Test-and-slaughter of reactors - Pasteurisation of milk - Quarantine of new stock - PPE for handlers (gloves, masks). **3. Campylobacteriosis (2.4 marks)** - **Pathogen:** *Campylobacter jejuni* - **Reservoir:** Poultry gut (commensal in chickens) - **Transmission:** Undercooked poultry, cross-contamination in kitchen. - **GAHP Control:** - Biosecurity at shed entry (footbaths) - Dry litter management - Reduced stocking density - Chlorination of drinking water - Thinning of flocks using trained catchers. *[Examiner note: CO4, CO5 mapped; Bloom Level: Apply/Analyse; full marks require hazard-control linkage.]* --- ## Question B (Alternative) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]` **(a)** Describe the **Codex Alimentarius principles** governing animal feed safety. How do mycotoxins enter the food chain via feed, and what are the regulatory limits for **aflatoxin B1 in feed** and **aflatoxin M1 in milk**? **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain **animal welfare** in the context of GAHP. Discuss the **Five Freedoms** and how stress-induced physiological changes in animals can compromise food safety and meat quality. **(7 marks)** ### Model Answer — Question B **(a) Codex Feed Safety Principles + Mycotoxins (7 marks):** **Codex Principles (CAC/RCP 54-2003 — Code of Practice on Good Animal Feeding):** 1. **Source control** — feed ingredients must come from approved suppliers. 2. **Traceability** — one-step-forward, one-step-back record keeping. 3. **Risk-based approach** — hazard analysis of feed ingredients. 4. **HACCP application** at feed mills where feasible. 5. **Labelling** — clear declaration of ingredients, additives, medications. 6. **Prohibition of unsafe substances** — banned antibiotics, hormones, contaminants. 7. **Inspection & enforcement** by competent authority. **Mycotoxin Pathway (4 marks distributed):** Asynchronous production of aflatoxin by *Aspergillus flavus* and *A. parasiticus* on maize, groundnut, and copra under hot-humid storage conditions leads to: - Aflatoxin B1 in feed → ingested by dairy cow → hepatic bioactivation → secreted as Aflatoxin M1 in milk (carry-over 1–3%). - Carry-over is calculated as: $$TC_{milk} = \frac{C_{milk}}{C_{feed}} \approx 0.015$$ **Regulatory Limits:** | Matrix | Aflatoxin | Limit | Body | |---|---|---|---| | Dairy cattle feed | B1 | **20 µg/kg** | FSSAI / EU has 5 µg/kg | | Milk | M1 | **0.5 µg/kg** | Codex / FSSAI | | Groundnut meal | B1 | **30 µg/kg** | FSSAI | **Mitigation:** - Use of **HSCAS (Hydrated Sodium Calcium Aluminosilicate)** binder in feed. - Moisture control in silos (< 12% moisture). - Sorting and rejection of mouldy grains. --- **(b) Animal Welfare & Food Safety (7 marks):** **Definition:** Animal welfare in GAHP is the **physical and mental state** of an animal in relation to the conditions in which it lives and dies, ensuring humane treatment throughout its life cycle. **Five Freedoms (Brambell Committee, 1965 — adopted by WOAH):** 1. Freedom from hunger and thirst — ready access to fresh water and a diet to maintain full health. 2. Freedom from discomfort — appropriate environment including shelter and resting area. 3. Freedom from pain, injury, disease — prevention or rapid treatment. 4. Freedom to express normal behaviour — adequate space and facilities. 5. Freedom from fear and distress — conditions and treatment that avoid mental suffering. **Stress → Food Safety Linkage:** When animals are subjected to **pre-slaughter stress** (rough handling, overcrowding, transport, dehydration): - **Catecholamine surge** (adrenaline, noradrenaline) → glycogen depletion in muscle. - Post-mortem, the muscle pH fails to fall adequately → **DFD (Dark, Firm, Dry) meat** in beef and **PSE (Pale, Soft, Exudative)** in pork — both indicating poor meat quality, reduced shelf-life, and higher bacterial spoilage. - Immunosuppression from chronic stress → increased **faecal shedding of Salmonella and E. coli O157:H7** → carcass contamination at slaughter. - Stress also increases **bruising**, downgrading carcass value. **Quantitative link:** $$pH_{24h} < 5.8 \Rightarrow \text{Normal meat}$$ $$pH_{24h} > 6.0 \Rightarrow \text{DFD meat (high microbial risk)}$$ **Conclusion:** Welfare is **not just ethical** — it is a **measurable food safety and quality variable**. KTU expects integration of welfare, food safety, and product quality in the answer. *[Examiner note: CO4, CO5; Bloom Level: Apply/Analyse/Evaluate; full marks require connecting physiological stress to measurable quality/safety parameters.]* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing GAP with GAHP** — GAP covers crops; GAHP covers animals. Many students interchange these; examiner docks **1 mark**. > 2. **Forgetting B-C-P classification** — Always classify hazards as Biological / Chemical / Physical. Generic statements like "contaminants" score zero. > 3. **Missing numerical values** — Questions on MRLs, withdrawal periods, and microbiological standards **require numbers**. Vague qualitative answers get partial marks only. > 4. **Omitting traceability** — Traceability is a **non-negotiable** domain in GAHP. Skipping it loses 1 mark even if other domains are perfect. > 5. **Ignoring the welfare–safety link** — Welfare is **NOT** separate from food safety. Examiner expects an integrated answer, not two disjointed paragraphs. > 6. **Not citing regulatory bodies** — Always mention **Codex / FSSAI / WOAH / FAO** to anchor authority and score higher. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist (Save for Last-Minute Review):** - ⭐ **GAHP = Primary Production Food Safety** — first link in farm-to-fork continuum. - ⭐ **Four Pillars:** Health, Feed, Shelter, Records. - ⭐ **Seven Domains:** ID, Health, Feed/Water, Drugs, Housing, Welfare, Environment. - ⭐ **Five Freedoms** (Brambell 1965) are the bedrock of animal welfare in GAHP. - ⭐ **Drug Withdrawal Period (DWP)** must be strictly observed; failure = residue violation. - ⭐ **MRLs are mandatory** — Codex sets global benchmarks; FSSAI adopts them. - ⭐ **Aflatoxin B1 (feed) = 20 µg/kg** (India) / **5 µg/kg** (EU); **M1 (milk) = 0.5 µg/kg**. - ⭐ **Carry-over ratio** for aflatoxin from feed to milk ≈ 1–3%; transfer coefficient ≈ 0.015. - ⭐ **B-C-P Hazard Classification** — Biological / Chemical / Physical — always use this terminology. - ⭐ **Salmonella, Brucella, Campylobacter, E. coli O157** — the "Big Four" zoonoses controlled by GAHP. - ⭐ **All-in/All-out** poultry systems dramatically reduce cross-batch contamination. - ⭐ **Closed herd, quarantine 21–28 days**, vaccination calendars are non-negotiable. - ⭐ **Potable water** for animals: **0 MPN coliforms/100 mL**; periodic testing mandatory. - ⭐ **DFD and PSE meat** are direct consequences of pre-slaughter stress — welfare IS food safety. - ⭐ **GAHP precedes HACCP** — you cannot implement HACCP on a poorly managed farm. - ⭐ **Regulatory anchors:** Codex CAC/RCP, FAO, WOAH (OIE), FSSAI, BIS, EU Hygiene Package (853/2004). - ⭐ **AMR connection:** Indiscriminate antibiotic use at farm → resistant bacteria → human treatment failure; hence FSSAI's 2019 colistin ban. - ⭐ **Records** must be retained for **3–5 years** for full traceability compliance. - ⭐ **Carcass disposal** options: incineration, burial, rendering — never open dumping. - ⭐ **Manure management** via composting, biogas — prevents zoonotic and environmental contamination. ---

topics.title: Good Aquaculture Practices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCFTT503 - FOOD SAFETY AND QUALITY REGULATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Good Aquaculture Practices ## 1.
