# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define ISO 9001:2000. List any three of its Quality Management Principles.
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
ISO 9001:2000 is an international standard published by the International Organization for Standardization (ISO) in December 2000 that specifies the requirements for a Quality Management System (QMS). It is applicable to all organizations regardless of type, size, or product, and provides a framework for demonstrating the ability to consistently provide products/services that meet customer and regulatory requirements.

**Three Quality Management Principles (out of 8):**

1. **Customer Focus:** Organizations depend on their customers and should understand current and future customer needs, meet customer requirements, and strive to exceed customer expectations.

2. **Leadership:** Leaders establish unity of purpose and direction of the organization. They should create and maintain the internal environment in which people can become fully involved in achieving the organization's objectives.

3. **Process Approach:** A desired result is achieved more efficiently when activities and related resources are managed as a process.

*(Other principles: Involvement of People, System Approach to Management, Continual Improvement, Factual Approach to Decision Making, Mutually Beneficial Supplier Relationships)*

---

### Question 2: What is meant by the "Process Approach" in ISO 9001:2000? Why is it important?
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition of Process Approach:**
The Process Approach is a management strategy in which an organization manages its activities as a set of interrelated processes. Each process uses resources to transform inputs into outputs, and the outputs of one process become the inputs of the next.

**Importance:**
1. **Systematic Understanding:** It helps the organization clearly define and understand its activities, making the QMS more transparent.
2. **Customer Focus:** It ensures customer requirements are met at every stage of product realization.
3. **Efficiency:** Resources are used efficiently through clear process ownership and management.
4. **Continual Improvement:** Processes can be measured and improved continuously.
5. **Effective Decision Making:** Data from process monitoring enables fact-based decisions.
6. **PDCA Integration:** It forms the basis for applying the Plan-Do-Check-Act cycle.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks):**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the eight Quality Management Principles of ISO 9001:2000 in detail. (7 marks)

**(b)** Describe the Process Approach Model of ISO 9001:2000 with a suitable diagram. Explain how the PDCA cycle is applied to the clauses of the standard. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution:

### (a) The Eight Quality Management Principles (7 marks)

| # | Principle | Detailed Explanation |
|---|-----------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Customer Focus** | The primary focus is to meet customer requirements and exceed their expectations. This requires understanding present and future customer needs, communicating customer requirements throughout the organization, measuring customer satisfaction, and managing customer relationships. |
| 2 | **Leadership** | Leaders must establish a clear vision, create unity of purpose, set challenging goals, and provide the environment in which people can be fully involved. Leadership ensures the QMS is implemented effectively. |
| 3 | **Involvement of People** | Competent, empowered, and engaged people at all levels enhance the organization's capability to create value. Benefits include motivated employees, innovation, and accountability. |
| 4 | **Process Approach** | Activities and resources are managed as processes. Each process has inputs, outputs, controls, and measurement criteria. The result is improved efficiency, predictability, and continual improvement capability. |
| 5 | **System Approach to Management** | Interrelated processes are managed as a system. This improves effectiveness and efficiency in achieving the organization's quality objectives. |
| 6 | **Continual Improvement** | Continual improvement of the QMS is a permanent objective. It uses audit results, corrective/preventive actions, and management review to drive ongoing enhancement. |
| 7 | **Factual Approach to Decision Making** | Effective decisions are based on the analysis of accurate data and information. This reduces guesswork and ensures objectivity. |
| 8 | **Mutually Beneficial Supplier Relationships** | An organization and its suppliers are interdependent. A mutually beneficial relationship enhances the ability of both to create value. |

### (b) Process Approach Model and PDCA Application (7 marks)

**Process Approach Model Explanation:**

The Process Approach Model shows how a QMS delivers the desired outcomes through a network of processes. The model has the following key features:

1. **Customer-Centric:** The customer is at the center. Customer requirements drive all processes.
2. **Interested Parties:** Regulatory bodies, shareholders, employees, and suppliers also influence the QMS.
3. **Process Network:** Processes are interconnected and feed into each other.
4. **Feedback Loop:** Customer satisfaction feeds back into the QMS for improvement.

**PDCA Cycle Application to ISO 9001:2000 Clauses:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{PLAN:} \quad & \text{Clause 4: QMS Requirements} \\
& \text{Clause 5: Management Responsibility (Policy, Objectives, Review)} \\
& \text{Clause 6: Resource Management} \\
\\
\textbf{DO:} \quad & \text{Clause 7.1: Realization Planning} \\
& \text{Clause 7.2: Customer-Related Processes} \\
& \text{Clause 7.3: Design and Development} \\
& \text{Clause 7.4: Purchasing} \\
& \text{Clause 7.5: Production and Service Provision} \\
\\
\textbf{CHECK:} \quad & \text{Clause 8.2: Monitoring and Measurement} \\
& \text{Clause 8.3: Control of Nonconforming Product} \\
& \text{Clause 8.4: Analysis of Data} \\
\\
\textbf{ACT:} \quad & \text{Clause 8.5.1: Continual Improvement} \\
& \text{Clause 8.5.2: Corrective Action} \\
& \text{Clause 8.5.3: Preventive Action}
\end{aligned}
$$

> The PDCA cycle is applied continuously: the outputs of ACT feed back into PLAN, making the QMS a self-improving system.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the requirements of Clause 5 (Management Responsibility) and Clause 7 (Product Realization) under ISO 9001:2000. (7 marks)

**(b)** Discuss the mandatory documented procedures required by ISO 9001:2000. What is the role of the Quality Manual in the QMS? (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution:

### (a) Clause 5: Management Responsibility (7 marks)

**5.1 Management Commitment:** Top management must provide evidence of its commitment by communicating the importance of meeting customer, statutory, and regulatory requirements; establishing the quality policy; ensuring quality objectives are set; conducting management reviews; and providing adequate resources.

**5.2 Customer Focus:** Top management must ensure customer needs and expectations are identified, converted into requirements, and fulfilled with the aim of enhancing customer satisfaction.

**5.3 Quality Policy:** Must be appropriate to the organization, include commitments to meet requirements and continual improvement, provide a framework for objectives, be communicated and understood, and be reviewed for suitability.

**5.4 Planning:**
- **5.4.1 Quality Objectives:** Established at relevant functions and levels, measurable, consistent with the policy.
- **5.4.2 Quality Management System Planning:** Top management shall plan the QMS to meet requirements and quality objectives, and maintain QMS integrity during changes.

**5.5 Responsibility, Authority, and Communication:**
- Responsibilities and authorities are defined and communicated.
- Management representative is appointed.
- Internal communication processes are established.

**5.6 Management Review:** Conducted at planned intervals. Inputs include audit results, customer feedback, process performance, status of preventive/corrective actions, follow-up actions, changes, and recommendations for improvement. Outputs include resource needs and improvement actions.

**Clause 7: Product Realization (7 marks)**

**7.1 Planning of Product Realization:** Define quality objectives and requirements; define processes, documents, and resources; define verification, validation, monitoring, and inspection activities; and define records.

**7.2 Customer-Related Processes:**
- Identification of requirements (product, delivery, post-delivery, statutory, regulatory).
- Review of requirements before acceptance.
- Effective customer communication.

**7.3 Design and Development:** Includes planning, inputs, outputs, review, verification, validation, and control of changes.

**7.4 Purchasing:** Includes purchasing information, evaluation and selection of suppliers, and verification of purchased product.

**7.5 Production and Service Provision:** Includes production control, process validation, identification and traceability, customer property, preservation of product, and control of monitoring/measuring devices.

### (b) Mandatory Documented Procedures and Quality Manual (7 marks)

**Six Mandatory Documented Procedures under ISO 9001:2000:**

| # | Procedure | Clause | Purpose |
|---|-----------|--------|---------|
| 1 | Control of Documents | 4.2.3 | Ensure documents are approved, reviewed, updated, identified, and available |
| 2 | Control of Records | 4.2.4 | Maintain evidence of QMS effectiveness and product conformity |
| 3 | Internal Audit | 8.2.2 | Verify QMS conformity and effectiveness |
| 4 | Control of Nonconforming Product | 8.3 | Prevent unintended use or delivery of nonconforming product |
| 5 | Corrective Action | 8.5.2 | Eliminate causes of nonconformities to prevent recurrence |
| 6 | Preventive Action | 8.5.3 | Eliminate causes of potential nonconformities to prevent occurrence |

**Role of the Quality Manual (Clause 4.2.1):**

The Quality Manual is a **mandatory top-level document** that:
1. States the **scope of the QMS** (including any exclusions and justifications).
2. Describes the **interaction between the processes** of the QMS (process map).
3. Provides a **summary of documented procedures** or reference to them.
4. Defines the **structure of documentation** used in the QMS.

The Quality Manual serves as the **central reference document** for internal and external auditors, customers, and employees to understand how the organization's QMS is structured and implemented.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Conflating ISO 9001:2000 with ISO 9001:2015:** Do NOT mention "risk-based thinking" or "context of the organization" (Clause 4) as part of ISO 9001:2000 — these are 2015 concepts.
> 2. **Forgetting Preventive Action:** Preventive Action (Clause 8.5.3) was REMOVED in ISO 9001:2015 but is a **mandatory requirement in ISO 9001:2000**. Always include it.
> 3. **Wrong PDCA Mapping:** Many students incorrectly place Clause 8.5 (Improvement) in DO instead of ACT. Remember: ACT is where improvements are *decided and implemented*, but PDCA is about applying the cycle.
> 4. **Skipping the 8 Principles:** For 7-mark questions on principles, students list only 4–5 principles. Examiners **require all 8** for full marks.
> 5. **Documentation Confusion:** The Quality Manual is a *document*; the 6 procedures are *documented procedures*; the standard requires both — these are different concepts.

---

## TOPIC RECAP — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **ISO 9001:2000** was published in **December 2000** and withdrawn in **November 2008**.
- It introduced the **Process Approach** and the **PDCA cycle** as central concepts.
- The standard has **8 introductory clauses (0–3)** and **5 auditable clauses (4–8)**.
- The **8 Quality Management Principles** are: Customer Focus, Leadership, Involvement of People, Process Approach, System Approach, Continual Improvement, Factual Decision Making, Mutually Beneficial Supplier Relationships.
- **Clause 4** = QMS requirements, **Clause 5** = Management responsibility, **Clause 6** = Resource management, **Clause 7** = Product realization, **Clause 8** = Measurement, analysis, and improvement.
- **6 mandatory documented procedures**: Control of Documents (4.2.3), Control of Records (4.2.4), Internal Audit (8.2.2), Control of Nonconforming Product (8.3), Corrective Action (8.5.2), Preventive Action (8.5.3).
- **Quality Manual** is required under Clause 4.2.1 and must state scope, process interactions, and documentation structure.
- **PDCA Mapping**: PLAN = Clauses 4, 5, 6; DO = Clause 7; CHECK = Clauses 8.1–8.4; ACT = Clause 8.5.
- **Continual Improvement** is a **permanent objective** of the organization.
- ISO 9001:2000 is a **generic standard** applicable to all organizations — its relevance to food industry is the **foundation** for later food safety management systems (ISO 22000, FSSC 22000, BRC, SQF).
- **Customer satisfaction** is treated as a **measurable metric** under Clause 8.2.1.
- **Management Review** (Clause 5.6) must be conducted at **planned intervals** with specific inputs and outputs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ISO 22000:2005
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT503 - FOOD SAFETY AND QUALITY REGULATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** ISO 22000:2005

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ISO 22000:2005 — Food Safety Management Systems — Requirements for any organization in the food chain**
> 
> It is an **international standard published by the International Organization for Standardization (ISO)** in **September 2005**, specifying the requirements for a **Food Safety Management System (FSMS)**. It combines the **Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Point (HACCP) system** with **Prerequisite Programs (PRPs)**, following the management system approach of **ISO 9001** and the **Plan–Do–Check–Act (PDCA) cycle**.

The standard was developed by **ISO Technical Committee 34 (TC 34)** — *Food products*, in collaboration with the **Codex Alimentarius Commission** and the **Confédération des Industries Agro-Alimentaires (CIAA)**. It is applicable to all organizations directly or indirectly involved in the food chain, including:

- Primary producers (farmers, fishermen)
- Feed producers
- Food manufacturers
- Transport and storage operators
- Retailers and food service outlets
- Catering and cleaning/sanitation service providers
- Equipment manufacturers and packaging material suppliers

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant Kitchen Passport" 🍳

Imagine you are a tourist entering a foreign country. To prove you are safe, you need a **passport** verified by an authority. Similarly, ISO 22000:2005 acts as a **"Food Safety Passport"** issued to a company after independent auditors verify that the company can:

1. **Identify** all the *hazards* (biological, chemical, physical) that could make food unsafe — like checking for risks before a journey.
2. **Plan** controls to manage those hazards — like planning the route and stops on a trip.
3. **Verify** continuously that controls are working — like checking your GPS during the journey.
4. **Act** upon deviations to prevent recurrence — like changing route when there's a roadblock.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Core Philosophy:** ISO 22000:2005 is built on the principle that the **most effective food safety systems are preventive, documented, risk-based, and continuously improved** rather than reactive.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Definition | Standard Reference |
|------|------------|-------------------|
| **FSMS** | Food Safety Management System | ISO 22000:2005 |
| **HACCP** | Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Point | Codex Alimentarius |
| **PRP** | Prerequisite Program | ISO 22000:2005 |
| **OPRP** | Operational Prerequisite Program | ISO 22000:2005 |
| **CCP** | Critical Control Point | Codex/ISO |
| **PDCA** | Plan-Do-Check-Act Cycle | ISO 9001/22000 |
| **ALOP** | Appropriate Level of Protection | WTO SPS Agreement |
| **FBO** | Food Business Organization | Generic term |

## 1.4 Position in the Food Safety Standards Family

ISO 22000:2005 belongs to a **family of standards** related to FSMS:

- **ISO 22000** — Core requirements (the topic of this module)
- **ISO 22001** — Guidance on the application of ISO 22000
- **ISO/TS 22002-1** — Prerequisite programs for food manufacturing
- **ISO 22003** — Requirements for bodies providing audit and certification
- **ISO 22004** — Guidance on the use of ISO 22000
- **ISO 22005** — Traceability in the feed and food chain
- **ISO 22006** — Application of ISO 9001 to crop production

---

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Note:** ISO 22000:2005 was **superseded by ISO 22000:2018** in June 2018. However, KTU 2024 syllabus still references the 2005 version because (a) the structural logic of 2005 forms the foundation for understanding 2018, and (b) many Indian food businesses and certification audits in Kerala/Karnataka still operate on 2005-aligned documentation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Content

## 2.1 The Architecture of ISO 22000:2005

The standard is structured into **8 main clauses** (Clauses 0–8) following the **ISO management system harmonized structure**:

### Clause-Wise Breakdown

| Clause | Title | Focus | CO Mapping |
|--------|-------|-------|------------|
| **0** | Introduction | PDCA, food safety hazard identification | CO4 |
| **1** | Scope | Defines applicability | CO4 |
| **2** | Normative References | ISO standards referenced | CO4 |
| **3** | Terms & Definitions | Vocabulary (hazard, risk, PRP, CCP) | CO4 |
| **4** | Food Safety Management System | Documentation, records | CO4 |
| **5** | Management Responsibility | Top management commitment | CO4 |
| **6** | Resource Management | Human resources, infrastructure, work environment | CO4 |
| **7** | Planning & Realization of Safe Products | **HACCP plan, PRPs, OPRPs, traceability** | CO4 |
| **8** | Validation, Verification & Improvement | Internal audit, corrective action | CO4 |

## 2.2 The PDCA Cycle in ISO 22000:2005

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PDCA = Plan–Do–Check–Act** (also called the **Deming Cycle**). It is the **governing logic** of the entire FSMS.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PLAN} &\rightarrow \text{Establish FSMS, HACCP plan, PRPs, objectives} \\
\text{DO} &\rightarrow \text{Implement and operate the FSMS} \\
\text{CHECK} &\rightarrow \text{Monitor, measure, validate, verify, audit} \\
\text{ACT} &\rightarrow \text{Update, improve, take corrective action}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Why PDCA?
- **Logical, iterative, and self-correcting**.
- Allows organizations to **continuously improve** the food safety system.
- Provides a **structured framework** for managing risk rather than reacting to crises.

## 2.3 Clause 7: The Heart of ISO 22000:2005 — Planning & Realization of Safe Products

This is the **most heavily examined clause** in KTU. It contains the **HACCP system** integrated with the management system framework.

### Sub-clauses of Clause 7:
1. **7.1** — General
2. **7.2** — Prerequisite Programs (PRPs)
3. **7.3** — Preliminary steps to enable hazard analysis
4. **7.4** — Hazard analysis
5. **7.5** — Establishing the operational prerequisite programs (OPRPs)
6. **7.6** — Establishing the HACCP plan (including identification of CCPs and critical limits)
7. **7.7** — Updating the information for the products/processes
8. **7.8** — Verification
9. **7.9** — Control of monitoring and measuring
10. **7.10** — Product withdrawal/recall

## 2.4 The 5 Preliminary Steps + 7 HACCP Principles (KTU High-Yield)

### Preliminary Steps (Pre-HACCP):
1. Assemble the **HACCP team**.
2. Describe the **product** and its intended use.
3. Construct the **process flow diagram** (from raw material to consumer).
4. Verify the flow diagram **on-site**.
5. Conduct **hazard analysis** (Principle 1) and determine control measures.

### 7 HACCP Principles (Codex Alimentarius / ISO 22000:2005):
1. **Principle 1** — Conduct a **hazard analysis** and determine control measures.
2. **Principle 2** — Determine the **Critical Control Points (CCPs)**.
3. **Principle 3** — Establish **critical limits** (numerical values separating acceptability from unacceptability).
4. **Principle 4** — Establish a system to **monitor control of CCPs**.
5. **Principle 5** — Establish the **corrective action** to take when monitoring indicates that a particular CCP is not under control.
6. **Principle 6** — Establish procedures for **verification** to confirm that the FSMS is working effectively.
7. **Principle 7** — Establish **documentation and record-keeping** concerning all procedures and records appropriate to these principles and their application.

## 2.5 The Decision Tree for CCPs vs OPRPs

This is **the single most exam-relevant flowchart** in ISO 22000:2005:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The CCP Decision Tree (Codex/ISO 22000)**

```
Q1: Do preventive control measure(s) exist for the identified hazard?
    ├── NO  → Modify process/product to introduce control → Re-evaluate
    └── YES ↓

Q2: Is this step specifically designed to eliminate or reduce 
    the likely occurrence of the hazard to an acceptable level?
    ├── NO  → Not a CCP (could be an OPRP)
    └── YES ↓
    → CCP

OR

Q2 (alt): Could contamination with the identified hazard(s) occur 
           in excess of acceptable level(s), or could these increase 
           to unacceptable level(s)?
    ├── NO  → Not a CCP
    └── YES ↓
    → CCP
```

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Definition | Key Boundary |
|---------|------------|--------------|
| **Hazard** | Biological, chemical, or physical agent in food with potential to cause adverse health effect | **Risk = Severity × Probability** |
| **Risk** | Function of probability of an adverse effect and severity | **Qualitative or Quantitative** |
| **PRP** | Basic environmental and operational practices | Mandatory, **food-handling-wide** |
| **OPRP** | Control measure applied to control a significant hazard, but **not** a CCP | Documented, validated, controlled |
| **CCP** | Step at which control can be applied to **prevent, eliminate, or reduce** a food safety hazard to an acceptable level | Has **critical limit** |
| **Critical Limit** | Numerical value/parameter separating acceptability from unacceptability | **Measurable** (e.g., temp ≤ 4 °C, pH ≤ 4.5) |
| **Tolerance** | Margin around critical limit | For calibration, not safety |
| **CL** | Codex Limit / Critical Limit | **= Critical Limit in HACCP** |
| **Acceptable Level** | Level below which hazard is considered tolerable | Set by FBO based on ALOP |
| **Two-way communication** | Interactive communication along the food chain | Clause 5.6 ISO 22000:2005 |
| **Withdrawal** | Removal from market when reason exists for suspecting non-conformity | Clause 7.10 |
| **Recall** | Removal when non-conforming product has reached consumer | Clause 7.10 |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

ISO 22000:2005 is used in:

- **Food processing plants** in Kerala (spices, coconut, seafood) — e.g., Marine Products Export Development Authority (MPEDA) certified plants.
- **Dairy industry** — Mother Dairy, Amul plants for export.
- **Pharmaceuticals** — where GMP cross-references FSMS for excipient manufacture.
- **Hotels, airlines, and institutional catering** — for tender prequalification.
- **Cold-chain logistics** — required documentation for transport of perishable goods.

> [!NOTE]
> **Connection to Other Modules:** ISO 22000:2005 is an **umbrella standard** that incorporates **HACCP (Module 3)** and integrates with **ISO 9001:2008 (Module 5)** and **Total Quality Management (Module 2)**.

---

## 2.7 Difference Between ISO 22000:2005 and ISO 22000:2018 (Board Frequently Asked)

| Parameter | ISO 22000:2005 | ISO 22000:2018 |
|-----------|----------------|----------------|
| **Structure** | 8 clauses | 10 clauses (Annex SL) |
| **PDCA Application** | Single PDCA cycle | Two PDCA cycles (Management + FSMS) |
| **Approach** | Plan-based | **Risk-based thinking** throughout |
| **PDCA mapping** | Clause 4 = Plan, 5–6 = Do, 7–8 = Check-Act | Clauses 6, 7, 8, 9, 10 mapped |
| **Number of PRPs** | Single category | PRPs + OPRPs (refined) |
| **Context of Organization** | Not explicit | **Clause 4.1–4.4** — new |
| **Leadership** | Under "Management Responsibility" | **Clause 5** — dedicated Leadership |
| **Documented information** | "Documents" and "Records" | "Documented information" |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse **HACCP** (a Codex system from 1993) with **ISO 22000:2005** (an ISO management system standard from 2005). They are related but **NOT the same**. ISO 22000:2005 *contains* HACCP but is a *broader management system*.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Frameworks

## 3.1 The Exhaustive ISO 22000:2005 Implementation Roadmap

Below is the **full sequential workflow** required to implement ISO 22000:2005 in any food business. Each step is logically derived from the standard's clauses and is **examination-relevant**.

### Step 1: Top Management Commitment (Clause 5.1)

The Food Business Operator (FBO) must demonstrate top management commitment by:

- Communicating the **importance of meeting customer, statutory, and regulatory food safety requirements**.
- Establishing the **food safety policy** (Clause 5.2).
- Conducting **management review** (Clause 5.8).
- Ensuring **resources are available** (Clause 6.1).

> **Logic:** Without top management support, no FSMS can survive an audit. This is the *Plan* phase for management.

### Step 2: Food Safety Policy (Clause 5.2)

A documented policy must:

- Be **appropriate to the organization's role in the food chain**.
- Include **compliance with statutory and regulatory requirements**.
- Include a **commitment to continual improvement**.
- Be **communicated, understood, and reviewed** (Clause 5.2.d).

### Step 3: Food Safety Management System Planning (Clause 4.1)

The organization shall:

- Identify **processes needed** for FSMS.
- Determine **sequence and interaction** of these processes.
- Determine **criteria and methods** for monitoring.
- Ensure **availability of resources and information**.

### Step 4: Documentation (Clause 4.2)

The FSMS documentation shall include:

- **Documented statements** of policy, objectives, scope.
- **Procedures** required by ISO 22000:2005.
- **Documents** needed to ensure effective planning and control.
- **Records** (objective evidence).

> **Logic:** "If it is not documented, it is not done." This is the *Do* phase documentation logic.

### Step 5: Define Scope of FSMS (Clause 1.2)

The organization must clearly state:

- **Product types** covered.
- **Processes** covered.
- **Sites** covered.
- **Exclusions and justifications**.

### Step 6: Prerequisite Programs (PRPs) Implementation (Clause 7.2)

PRPs form the **foundation** of the HACCP plan. Typical PRPs include:

- **Cleaning and sanitation** of premises and equipment.
- **Pest control**.
- **Personal hygiene** of food handlers.
- **Supplier control** and incoming material inspection.
- **Maintenance** of equipment.
- **Waste management**.
- **Water and air quality** control.
- **Storage and distribution** conditions.
- **Allergen management**.

> **Logic:** PRPs control the *general environment*. Without solid PRPs, HACCP cannot function.

### Step 7: Preliminary Hazard Analysis Steps (Clause 7.3)

Conducted in 5 sub-steps as per Codex/ISO:

1. **7.3.1** — Assemble HACCP team with multi-disciplinary expertise.
2. **7.3.2** — Describe product and intended use.
3. **7.3.3** — Construct process flow diagram.
4. **7.3.4** — Verify flow diagram on-site.
5. **7.3.5** — List all potential biological, chemical, physical hazards.

### Step 8: Hazard Analysis & Determination of Acceptable Levels (Clause 7.4)

For each identified hazard, the team must:

- Conduct a **hazard analysis** to identify which hazards are of significance.
- Determine **acceptable levels** of the hazard in the finished product.
- Identify **control measures** that prevent, eliminate, or reduce the hazard to acceptable level.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Acceptable Level (AL)} &= f(\text{Risk Assessment}) \\
\text{Risk} &= \text{Probability} \times \text{Severity} \\
\text{Significant Hazard} &\Leftrightarrow \text{Risk} \geq \text{Threshold}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 9: Classification into OPRPs and CCPs (Clauses 7.5 & 7.6)

This is the **decision logic** — examine each significant hazard's step:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Hazard at step} \rightarrow \text{Control Measure?} \rightarrow 
\begin{cases}
\text{Control eliminates hazard} &\Rightarrow \textbf{CCP} \\
\text{Control reduces but not eliminates} &\Rightarrow \textbf{OPRP or CCP} \\
\text{No specific control exists} &\Rightarrow \text{Modify process} \\
\text{Hazard not significant} &\Rightarrow \text{PRP level}
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 10: Establish Critical Limits and Monitoring (Clause 7.6.3 & 7.6.4)

For each CCP:
- **Critical limits** are set: measurable, validated, regulatory-based.
- **Monitoring system** established: who, what, when, how.
- **Monitoring equipment** calibrated.

**Example:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CCP}_1 &: \text{Pasteurization} \\
\text{Critical Limit} &: T \geq 72^\circ C \text{ for } t \geq 15 \text{ seconds} \\
\text{Monitoring} &: \text{Continuous chart recorder, every 30 min} \\
\text{Corrective Action} &: \text{Re-pasteurize or destroy batch if } T < 70^\circ C
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 11: Establish Corrective Actions (Clause 7.6.5)

When monitoring shows a critical limit is exceeded:

- Identify and control the **non-conforming product**.
- Determine the **cause** of deviation.
- Ensure CCP returns under control.
- Verify the **effectiveness of corrective action**.

### Step 12: Verification Activities (Clause 7.8 & Clause 8)

Verification confirms the FSMS is effective:

- **Internal audits** (Clause 8.2.2).
- **Calibration** of monitoring equipment (Clause 7.9).
- **Product testing** (microbial, chemical).
- **Trend analysis** of monitoring records.
- **Review of complaints, recalls, withdrawals**.

### Step 13: Management Review (Clause 5.8)

Top management reviews the FSMS at planned intervals, considering:

- Results of audits.
- Customer/regulatory feedback.
- Status of preventive and corrective actions.
- Follow-up from previous reviews.
- Changes affecting the FSMS.
- Recommendations for improvement.

### Step 14: Continual Improvement (Clause 8.5.2)

The organization shall:

- Analyze data from monitoring, audits, complaints.
- Implement corrective actions.
- Update PRPs, OPRPs, HACCP plan.
- Communicate improvements to the food chain.

> **Logic:** This is the *Act* phase — the system never rests.

---

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Example — Risk Classification Matrix

Let us analyze a step in a **dairy milk processing plant** for hazard classification:

| Process Step | Hazard Identified | Severity | Probability | Risk Score | Control Measure | Classification |
|--------------|-------------------|----------|-------------|-----------|-----------------|----------------|
| Raw milk reception | *Salmonella* | High (3) | High (3) | 9 | Pasteurization step | **OPRP** (raw milk control) |
| Pasteurization | *Salmonella, Listeria* | High (3) | High (3) | 9 | Time-temperature control | **CCP** |
| Packaging | Foreign matter (metal) | Medium (2) | Low (1) | 2 | Metal detector | **CCP** (if allergen) / OPRP |
| Storage (cold) | *Listeria* growth | High (3) | Medium (2) | 6 | Temperature ≤ 4 °C | **OPRP** |

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Risk Score} &= \text{Severity} \times \text{Probability} \\
\text{Threshold for Significant Hazard} &= \text{Risk Score} \geq 4 \\
\text{Decision} &: \text{Risk} \geq 4 \rightarrow \text{Apply OPRP or CCP}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Traceability Logic (Clause 7.9)

ISO 22000:2005 requires **two-way traceability**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Backward Traceability} &: \text{Finished Product} \rightarrow \text{Raw Material} \\
\text{Forward Traceability} &: \text{Raw Material} \rightarrow \text{Finished Product} \\
\text{Recall Time Target} &: \leq 4 \text{ hours} \text{ (industry best practice)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Section is Critical:** The KTU Part-B 14-mark question on ISO 22000 almost always requires students to *apply* the framework to a real or hypothetical food business case. Memorizing the clauses is insufficient — you must demonstrate **logical sequence** and **clause-to-action mapping**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ISO 22000:2005 — PDCA Cycle Mapping (Functional Architecture Flow)

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **PDCA mapping onto the clauses** of ISO 22000:2005, showing how each clause aligns with the management system logic.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef plan fill:#FFB347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef do fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef check fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef act fill:#FF7F7F,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef core fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    A[Top Management Commitment]:::plan
    B[Clause 5.2 Food Safety Policy]:::plan
    C[Clause 4.1 FSMS Planning]:::plan
    D[Clause 7.1 Realization Planning]:::plan

    E[Clause 4.2 Documentation]:::do
    F[Clause 6 Resource Management]:::do
    G[Clause 7.2 PRPs]:::do
    H[Clause 7.3-7.6 HACCP Plan]:::do
    I[Clause 7.7 Process Control]:::do

    J[Clause 7.8 Verification]:::check
    K[Clause 7.9 Control of Monitoring]:::check
    L[Clause 8.2 Internal Audit]:::check
    M[Clause 5.8 Management Review]:::check

    N[Clause 8.3 Nonconformity]:::act
    O[Clause 8.5 Continual Improvement]:::act
    P[Clause 7.10 Withdrawal/Recall]:::act
    Q[Updated FSMS]:::act

    A --> B --> C --> D
    D --> E --> F --> G --> H --> I
    I --> J --> K --> L --> M
    M --> N --> O
    N --> P
    O --> Q
    Q -.-> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The four colored bands represent PDCA phases — Orange (Plan), Blue (Do), Green (Check), Red (Act). The dashed feedback loop from Q back to A represents the **continual improvement cycle**.

---

## 4.2 HACCP Decision Tree — CCP vs OPRP Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef q fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ccp fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef oprp fill:#4682B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef prp fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    Q1{Hazard Identified?}:::q
    Q2{Preventive control exists?}:::q
    Q3{Step specifically designed to eliminate hazard?}:::q
    Q4{Contamination possible to unacceptable level?}:::q
    Q5{Acceptable level achievable?}:::q

    C1[CCP - Critical Control Point]:::ccp
    O1[OPRP - Operational PRP]:::oprp
    P1[PRP - Basic Control]:::prp
    R1[Modify process / reject hazard]:::prp

    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2
    Q1 -->|No| P1
    Q2 -->|No| R1
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3
    Q3 -->|Yes| C1
    Q3 -->|No| Q4
    Q4 -->|Yes| C1
    Q4 -->|No| Q5
    Q5 -->|Yes| O1
    Q5 -->|No| P1
```

---

## 4.3 Clause 7 — HACCP Plan Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef step fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    S1[7.3 Preliminary Steps]:::step
    S2[7.4 Hazard Analysis]:::step

    D1{Significant Hazard?}:::decision
    D2{CCP or OPRP?}:::decision

    O1[HACCP Plan Documented]:::output
    O2[Critical Limits Set]:::output
    O3[Monitoring Established]:::output
    O4[Corrective Actions Defined]:::output

    S1 --> S2 --> D1
    D1 -->|No| S5
    D1 -->|Yes| D2
    D2 -->|OPRP| S3 --> O1
    D2 -->|CCP| S4 --> O2 --> O3 --> O4 --> S6
    S6 --> S5
```

## 4.4 Food Chain Coverage by ISO 22000:2005

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef stage fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef org fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    A1[Primary Production]:::stage
    A2[Feed Production]:::stage
    A3[Food Manufacturing]:::stage
    A4[Transport & Storage]:::stage
    A5[Retail & Catering]:::stage

    B1[ISO 22000 Applicable]:::org
    B2[Equipment & Packaging]:::stage
    B3[Service Providers]:::stage
    B4[Cleaning & Sanitation]:::stage

    A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 --> A5
    A3 -.- B1
    A4 -.- B1
    A2 -.- B2
    A5 -.- B3
    A3 -.- B4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** ISO 22000:2005 covers *every* node of the food chain from farm to fork, including ancillary industries (equipment, packaging, sanitation chemicals). This is its **uniqueness** compared to HACCP which typically applies only to food manufacturers.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

### **Q1.** Define ISO 22000:2005. List its main clauses. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, S7 B.Tech FPE]`

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 mark):** ISO 22000:2005 is an international standard published by the International Organization for Standardization (ISO) that specifies the requirements for a Food Safety Management System (FSMS), combining HACCP principles with Prerequisite Programs (PRPs) and the Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA) cycle.

**Main clauses (2 marks):**
1. Clause 0 — Introduction
2. Clause 1 — Scope
3. Clause 2 — Normative references
4. Clause 3 — Terms and definitions
5. Clause 4 — Food Safety Management System
6. Clause 5 — Management responsibility
7. Clause 6 — Resource management
8. Clause 7 — Planning and realization of safe products
9. Clause 8 — Validation, verification and improvement

---

### **Q2.** Distinguish between Prerequisite Programs (PRPs) and Operational Prerequisite Programs (OPRPs). `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, S7 B.Tech FPE]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | PRP | OPRP |
|-----------|-----|------|
| **Definition** | Basic environmental and operational practices to maintain hygienic conditions | Control measure applied to control a significant hazard, but not a CCP |
| **Control Type** | Generic, broad | Specific, targeted |
| **Hazard** | May or may not address specific hazards | Addresses a *significant* hazard |
| **Critical Limit** | Not measurable as critical limit | Has defined control criteria |
| **Failure Consequence** | Increases likelihood of product contamination | Could lead to unacceptable contamination |
| **Examples** | Sanitation, pest control, hygiene | Cold storage temp ≤ 4 °C, allergen control |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often interchange PRPs and OPRPs. **Memorize the key distinction**: PRPs are *necessary conditions* for hygienic production; OPRPs are *additional control measures* for *significant* hazards that don't qualify as CCPs.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part-B question in a module carries **14 marks** with sub-parts **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students must answer ONE of two choices.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Comprehensive Case Study

> **`[KTU University Exam - July 2024, S7 B.Tech FPE]`**

**A.** A new dairy processing plant in Kerala producing pasteurized milk, paneer, and ghee wishes to implement ISO 22000:2005. As a quality consultant:

**(a)** Outline the **steps to be taken for the preliminary hazard analysis** (Clause 7.3). Discuss the 5 preliminary steps in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Identify the **PRPs, OPRPs, and CCPs** for the **pasteurization step** in this dairy plant, with a clear justification using the **CCP decision tree**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer — (a) Preliminary Hazard Analysis (7 marks)**

The preliminary steps for hazard analysis under **Clause 7.3** of ISO 22000:2005 are as follows:

**1. Assemble the HACCP team (1 mark)**
- Multi-disciplinary team with expertise in:
  - Dairy science and technology
  - Microbiology
  - Engineering (process, electrical, instrumentation)
  - Quality assurance
  - Cleaning and sanitation
  - Regulatory affairs (FSSAI compliance)
  - A team leader to coordinate.

**2. Describe the product and its intended use (1.5 marks)**
- Product: Pasteurized cow milk, paneer, and ghee.
- Composition: ~3.5% fat, 8.5% SNF, 0.7% ash, 4.8% lactose, 87% water (for milk).
- Packaging: Food-grade pouches / tetra pak.
- Storage: 4 °C for milk; refrigerated for paneer; ambient for ghee.
- Intended use: Direct consumption by general public (including infants, elderly — vulnerable groups).
- Shelf life: 24 h (raw), 5–7 days (pasteurized), 15–30 days (ghee).

**3. Construct process flow diagram (1.5 marks)**

```
Raw milk reception → Filtration / clarification → Standardization 
   → Pasteurization (72 °C × 15 s) → Cooling (≤ 4 °C) → Packing 
   → Cold storage → Distribution
```

For paneer: additional coagulation, whey separation, pressing, packaging.
For ghee: butter → clarification → moisture removal → settling → packaging.

**4. Verify the flow diagram on-site (1 mark)**
- The HACCP team walks through the actual plant and confirms each step.
- Discrepancies (e.g., unintended transfer lines, sampling points) are documented and added to the diagram.

**5. List all potential hazards (2 marks)**

| Hazard Type | Examples in Dairy |
|-------------|-------------------|
| **Biological** | *Salmonella spp., Listeria monocytogenes, E. coli O157:H7, Staphylococcus aureus, Bacillus cereus, Mycobacterium bovis, Brucella abortus* |
| **Chemical** | Antibiotic residues, pesticide residues, heavy metals (Pb, Cd), aflatoxin M1, cleaning chemical residues, allergens |
| **Physical** | Glass fragments, metal pieces, dust, plastic, hair, insects |

The analysis then continues into **Clause 7.4** for hazard significance determination, followed by **Clause 7.5/7.6** for OPRP/CCP classification.

---

#### **Model Answer — (b) PRPs, OPRPs, CCPs for Pasteurization (7 marks)**

**PRPs (Foundation level — 1.5 marks):**
- Good Manufacturing Practices (GMP) — clean premises, hygienic equipment.
- Sanitation program — CIP (Clean-in-Place) for the pasteurizer.
- Personal hygiene — gloves, hairnets, hand washing.
- Pest control.
- Water quality (potable, tested daily).
- Supplier control (raw milk from approved dairy cooperatives).
- Equipment calibration.
- Waste management.

**OPRPs (Targeted control for significant hazards — 2 marks):**
- **OPRP 1: Raw milk reception temperature (≤ 4 °C on arrival).**
  - Hazard: *Salmonella* and *Listeria* growth during transportation.
  - Control: Truck thermograph, intake temperature check at every delivery.
  - Justification: Critical but not a CCP because the **pasteurization step** (the next step) is specifically designed to eliminate this hazard.

- **OPRP 2: Cold chain post-pasteurization (≤ 4 °C during storage and distribution).**
  - Hazard: *Listeria monocytogenes* and *B. cereus* growth.
  - Control: Continuous temperature monitoring in cold room and refrigerated trucks.

**CCPs (Critical control — 3.5 marks):**
- **CCP 1: Pasteurization**
  - **Critical Limit:** T ≥ 72 °C for t ≥ 15 seconds (HTST).
  - **Monitoring:** Continuous chart recorder + automatic flow diversion valve (FDV) for sub-lethal temperatures.
  - **Corrective Action:** Flow diversion; reprocess sub-standard product; investigate cause; calibrate thermometer.
  - **Justification via CCP Decision Tree:**
    - Q1: Hazard *Salmonella, Listeria, Mycobacterium*? → YES
    - Q2: Preventive control exists (heat)? → YES
    - Q3: Step specifically designed to eliminate the hazard? → YES
    - → **CCP**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Critical Limit} &: T \geq 72^\circ C, \quad t \geq 15 \text{ s} \\
\text{Tolerance} &: T_{\text{operating}} - T_{\text{critical}} = 2^\circ C \\
\text{Destruction Equivalent} &: \log_{10}(\text{reduction}) \geq 5 \text{ for } \text{pathogens}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently write *"the pasteurization step is a CCP"* without applying the decision tree. KTU 2024 ESE requires **logical justification** using the decision tree. Always include the **Q1, Q2, Q3** sequence.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice (Conceptual / Structural)

> **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, S7 B.Tech FPE]`**

**B.** *(a) Explain the **PDCA cycle** and discuss how it is applied to the **8 clauses of ISO 22000:2005** with a suitable schematic representation.* **(7 marks)**

*(b) Discuss the **7 HACCP principles** as integrated within ISO 22000:2005. List **5 typical PRPs** and explain the **concept of critical limits** with two examples.* **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer — (a) PDCA Cycle in ISO 22000:2005 (7 marks)**

**PDCA Cycle Definition (1 mark):**
PDCA stands for **Plan–Do–Check–Act**. It is a four-stage management methodology for continuous improvement. In ISO 22000:2005, it is the fundamental structure for implementing and maintaining the Food Safety Management System.

**Plan (Clause 4, 5, 7) — 2 marks:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Plan} &= \text{Clause 4 (FSMS) + Clause 5 (Management Responsibility) + Clause 7 (Planning)} \\
&= \text{Establish policy, objectives, FSMS scope, hazard analysis, HACCP plan, PRPs, OPRPs}
\end{aligned}
$$

- The organization plans food safety policies, objectives, FSMS structure, and the HACCP plan.

**Do (Clause 6, 7) — 1.5 marks:**

$$
\text{Do} = \text{Clause 6 (Resource Management) + Clause 7.2–7.7 (Implementation)}
$$

- The organization implements PRPs, OPRPs, HACCP plan, and operational controls.

**Check (Clause 7.8, 7.9, 8.2) — 1.5 marks:**

$$
\text{Check} = \text{Clause 7.8 (Verification) + Clause 7.9 (Monitoring Control) + Clause 8.2 (Internal Audit)}
$$

- The organization monitors, measures, calibrates equipment, and audits the system.

**Act (Clause 8.3, 8.5) — 1 mark:**

$$
\text{Act} = \text{Clause 8.3 (Nonconformity + Corrective Action) + Clause 8.5 (Continual Improvement)}
$$

- Non-conformities are addressed, corrective actions implemented, and the system updated.

**Schematic (additional clarity — describe, since 7 marks):**

```
       PLAN  →  DO  →  CHECK  →  ACT
       Clause 4   Clause 6   Clause 7.8-8.2   Clause 8.3, 8.5
       Clause 5   Clause 7   Clause 7.9
       Clause 7
```

The output of ACT feeds back into PLAN, creating a continuous cycle of improvement.

---

#### **Model Answer — (b) 7 HACCP Principles + PRPs + Critical Limits (7 marks)**

**7 HACCP Principles (3 marks):**

1. **Conduct a hazard analysis** — Identify biological, chemical, physical hazards; determine significance and control measures.
2. **Determine Critical Control Points (CCPs)** — Apply the decision tree.
3. **Establish critical limits** — Numerical values separating acceptability from unacceptability.
4. **Establish monitoring procedures** — Who, what, when, how.
5. **Establish corrective actions** — What to do when monitoring shows deviation.
6. **Establish verification procedures** — Confirm FSMS effectiveness.
7. **Establish documentation and record-keeping** — All procedures, records, and evidence.

**5 Typical PRPs (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PRP}_1 &: \text{Personal hygiene} \quad (\text{hand washing, gloves, hairnets}) \\
\text{PRP}_2 &: \text{Cleaning and sanitation} \quad (\text{CIP procedures, sanitizer rotation}) \\
\text{PRP}_3 &: \text{Pest control} \quad (\text{rodent bait stations, insect light traps}) \\
\text{PRP}_4 &: \text{Supplier control} \quad (\text{approved vendor list, CoA verification}) \\
\text{PRP}_5 &: \text{Equipment maintenance and calibration} \quad (\text{preventive maintenance, gauge calibration})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Critical Limits with 2 Examples (2 marks):**

> A critical limit is a **maximum or minimum value** to which a biological, chemical, or physical parameter must be controlled at a CCP to prevent, eliminate, or reduce a food safety hazard to an acceptable level.

**Example 1: Pasteurization of milk (CCP 1)**
$$
\text{Critical Limit} = T \geq 72^\circ C \quad \text{for} \quad t \geq 15 \text{ seconds (HTST)}
$$
- If $T < 70^\circ C$ for any measurable time → flow diversion valve activates → product re-pasteurized.

**Example 2: Cooking of meat patties (CCP 2)**
$$
\text{Critical Limit} = T_{\text{internal}} \geq 74^\circ C \quad \text{for} \quad t \geq 15 \text{ seconds}
$$
- Verified using calibrated probe thermometer in the geometric center of 9 patties per batch.
- If not achieved → product is held, re-cooked, or destroyed.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks Lost on Part-B Questions:**
> 1. **Not answering the entire question** — Many students answer sub-part (a) well but skip sub-part (b) parts.
> 2. **No clause references** — Always cite the **Clause number** (e.g., Clause 7.6) for marks.
> 3. **Mere listing without explanation** — Bullet points alone do not earn full marks; **justify** each point.
> 4. **Confusing "withdrawal" vs "recall"** — Withdrawal = suspect product *not yet* at consumer; Recall = product *has reached* consumer.
> 5. **Skipping the decision tree** in CCP questions — This is worth at least 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Save This!):**

### Core Facts
- ISO 22000:2005 was published in **September 2005** by ISO TC 34.
- It **combines HACCP + PRPs + ISO 9001 PDCA approach**.
- It applies to **all organizations in the food chain** (farm to fork, including ancillary).
- It is **voluntary** unless mandated by customer or regulator.

### Clause Knowledge
- **Clause 4** = FSMS documentation
- **Clause 5** = Management responsibility (policy, communication, review)
- **Clause 6** = Resources (people, infrastructure, work environment)
- **Clause 7** = Realization of safe products (PRPs, HACCP plan, traceability, withdrawal/recall)
- **Clause 8** = Verification, validation, internal audit, non-conformity, continual improvement

### HACCP / Decision Tree
- 5 preliminary steps → 7 HACCP principles
- CCP decision tree: Q1 (hazard) → Q2 (control) → Q3 (elimination) → CCP
- OPRP = control measure for significant hazard, not a CCP
- Critical limit = numerical value (e.g., T ≥ 72 °C, pH ≤ 4.5, Aw ≤ 0.85)

### Key Distinctions
- **PRP ≠ OPRP ≠ CCP** — *Memorize* the table.
- **Withdrawal ≠ Recall** — Withdrawal = from market; Recall = from consumer.
- **Validation ≠ Verification** — Validation = "Are we doing the right thing?"; Verification = "Are we doing it right?"
- **ISO 22000:2005 ≠ HACCP** — 22000 is the management system; HACCP is the technical tool.

### Benefits of ISO 22000:2005
- Harmonizes food safety globally.
- Reduces foodborne illness.
- Facilitates international trade.
- Provides a **communication platform** along the food chain (Clause 5.6).
- Integrates with **ISO 9001**, **ISO 14001**, **ISO 45001**.

### KTU-Specific Memory Anchors
- "**7**" appears a lot — **7** HACCP principles, **7** sub-clauses in Clause 7.
- Always mention **PDCA** — examiners love it.
- For KTU, always cite the **Clause number** alongside any concept.
- Real-world application in Kerala: **spice processing, marine export, dairy co-operatives**.

### Common Exam Traps
- ❌ Saying "ISO 22000 is HACCP" → Marks deducted. (It's a system that *embeds* HACCP.)
- ❌ Skipping the **decision tree** in CCP questions.
- ❌ Confusing **acceptable level** with **critical limit**.
- ❌ Forgetting **traceability** is two-way.
- ❌ Not mentioning **calibration** of monitoring devices.
- ❌ Confusing **withdrawal** and **recall**.

---

> ✅ **End of Module 4 — Topic: ISO 22000:2005 | PCFTT503**
> *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech — Outcome-Based Education (OBE) alignment.*

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ISO 9000** is a series of internationally recognized standards developed by the **International Organization for Standardization (ISO)** that define the requirements for a **Quality Management System (QMS)**. The ISO 9000 family provides a framework for organizations to ensure they consistently meet customer and regulatory requirements and continuously improve the effectiveness of their quality management processes.

The **ISO 9000 family** of standards includes:

- **ISO 9000:2015** – Fundamentals and vocabulary (provides the foundational concepts, principles, and terminology).
- **ISO 9001:2015** – Requirements (specifies the criteria for a QMS; the only standard in the family that can be certified).
- **ISO 9004:2018** – Quality of an organization (provides guidance on achieving sustained success).
- **ISO 19011:2018** – Guidelines for auditing management systems.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Simple Analogy: The Restaurant Kitchen Standard**
> Imagine every restaurant must follow the same universal recipe for "excellent kitchen management." The recipe doesn't tell the chef *what* food to cook — it tells the chef *how to manage the kitchen* so the food is always good, safe, and consistent.
>
> - ISO 9001 is like a **kitchen rulebook**: wash hands (hygiene), keep ingredients fresh (raw material control), check the oven temperature (process control), train the cooks (competence), listen to customer complaints (feedback), and keep getting better.
> - ISO 9000:2015 is the **dictionary** that explains what every word in the rulebook means.
> - ISO 9004 is the **advanced cookbook** for kitchens that want to become Michelin-star restaurants.
>
> In the food industry, ISO 9000 ensures that whether you buy a packaged snack in Kerala or in Canada, the company has a **documented, audited, and continuously improving** system to deliver consistent quality.

### Key Terminology (as per ISO 9000:2015 vocabulary)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Quality** | Degree to which a set of inherent characteristics of an object fulfils requirements. |
| **Quality Management System (QMS)** | Set of interrelated or interacting elements to establish policies and objectives for quality. |
| **Customer Satisfaction** | Customer's perception of the degree to which the customer's expectations have been fulfilled. |
| **Process** | Set of interrelated or interacting activities that use inputs to deliver an intended result. |
| **Conformity** | Fulfilling a requirement. |
| **Defect** | Nonconformity related to an intended or specified use. |
| **Traceability** | Ability to identify and trace the history, distribution, location, and application of an item. |
| **Audit** | Systematic, independent, and documented process for obtaining audit evidence. |

### Why ISO 9000 Matters in Food Safety

> [!IMPORTANT]
> ISO 9000 is the **universal backbone** of modern food quality assurance. While **HACCP** focuses on food *safety* hazards, **ISO 9000** focuses on the *management system* that ensures the entire organization works consistently. It is often the *gateway* standard that a food company achieves before moving to more specialized food safety standards like **FSSC 22000, BRC, or SQF**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Evolution of ISO 9000

| Year | Version | Key Shift |
|---|---|---|
| 1987 | First Edition | Introduced the QMS concept; 20 elements model. |
| 1994 | Revision | Clarified existing requirements; preventive action emphasis. |
| 2000 | Major Revision | Process approach introduced; customer focus. |
| 2008 | Minor Revision | Clarifications only; structure unchanged. |
| 2015 | Major Revision | Risk-based thinking, leadership, context of organization. |
| 2025 | Latest update (ISO 9001:2025) | Climate change considerations, life-cycle perspective. |

### The Seven Quality Management Principles (ISO 9000:2015)

> [!NOTE]
> The 7 principles form the **philosophical foundation** of the entire ISO 9000 family. Every clause of ISO 9001 traces back to one of these principles.

1. **Customer Focus** — The primary focus is to meet customer requirements and exceed expectations.
2. **Leadership** — Leaders establish unity of purpose and direction of the organization.
3. **Engagement of People** — Competent, empowered, and engaged people enhance the organization's capability.
4. **Process Approach** — Activities are managed as interrelated processes functioning as a coherent system.
5. **Improvement** — Successful organizations have an ongoing focus on improvement.
6. **Evidence-Based Decision Making** — Decisions based on analysis and evaluation of data and information.
7. **Relationship Management** — Managing relationships with interested parties (suppliers, partners, regulators).

### The Process Approach & PDCA Cycle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Process Approach** is the heart of ISO 9001. Every activity in a food company is treated as a **process** with inputs, controls, outputs, and feedback.

**PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) Cycle** — The engine of continual improvement:

$$\boxed{\text{PDCA} = \text{Plan} \rightarrow \text{Do} \rightarrow \text{Check} \rightarrow \text{Act} \rightarrow (\text{repeat})}$$

| Stage | Meaning | Food Industry Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Plan** | Establish objectives & processes | Define HACCP plan, set critical limits, design CCPs |
| **Do** | Execute the planned processes | Implement CCP monitoring, train staff, run production |
| **Check** | Monitor & measure performance | Lab testing, audit findings, customer complaint log |
| **Act** | Take corrective actions & improve | Update SOPs, retrain, eliminate root cause |

### Clause Structure of ISO 9001:2015 (High-Yield for Exams)

> [!NOTE]
> The **10-clause structure** known as **Annex SL (High-Level Structure)** is shared across all modern ISO management system standards (ISO 14001, ISO 45001, ISO 22000). This enables **integrated management systems**.

| Clause | Title | Key Requirement |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Scope | Defines applicability. |
| 2 | Normative References | ISO 9000:2015. |
| 3 | Terms and Definitions | Reference to ISO 9000 vocabulary. |
| 4 | Context of the Organization | Internal/external issues, interested parties, QMS scope. |
| 5 | Leadership | Top management commitment, quality policy, roles. |
| 6 | Planning | Risks, opportunities, quality objectives. |
| 7 | Support | Resources, competence, awareness, communication, documented information. |
| 8 | Operation | Operational planning, product/service requirements, design, control of externally provided processes. |
| 9 | Performance Evaluation | Monitoring, measurement, analysis, internal audit, management review. |
| 10 | Improvement | Nonconformity, corrective action, continual improvement. |

### Risk-Based Thinking (Major 2015 Innovation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> ISO 9001:2015 made **risk-based thinking** mandatory, replacing the older "preventive action" clause. The organization must identify risks and opportunities that could affect QMS performance.

**Risk Equation (used in quality planning):**

$$\text{Risk} = \text{Likelihood (L)} \times \text{Severity (S)} \times \text{Detection Difficulty (D)}$$

Or in standard ISO 31000 terminology:

$$R = f(L, S)$$

A common KTU scoring matrix is:

| Likelihood \ Severity | Low (1) | Medium (2) | High (3) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **High (3)** | 3 | 6 | 9 |
| **Medium (2)** | 2 | 4 | 6 |
| **Low (1)** | 1 | 2 | 3 |

Where Risk Priority Number (RPN) ≥ 6 requires action.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **PDCA Cycle** | Plan → Do → Check → Act | Continual improvement of any process |
| **Risk Priority Number** | $RPN = L \times S \times D$ | Prioritize food safety/quality risks |
| **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** | $COPQ = C_{prevention} + C_{appraisal} + C_{internal\,failure} + C_{external\,failure}$ | Quantify waste in food production |
| **First Pass Yield (FPY)** | $FPY = \dfrac{\text{Good Units}}{\text{Total Units Produced}} \times 100\%$ | Process efficiency |
| **Customer Satisfaction Index** | $CSI = \dfrac{\sum (\text{Performance Ratings})}{\sum (\text{Importance Ratings})}$ | Quality of service |
| **Process Capability Index** | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | Measure process spread vs tolerance |
| **Conformance Rate** | $CR = \dfrac{\text{Conforming Products}}{\text{Total Inspected}} \times 100\%$ | Quality performance metric |
| **Audit Nonconformity Rate** | $NCR = \dfrac{\text{Number of NCs}}{\text{Total Audit Findings}} \times 100\%$ | Audit effectiveness |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Risk Priority Number (RPN) Application in Food Quality

**Problem Statement:** A food processing unit has identified a potential hazard in cold storage temperature monitoring. The team assigns:
- **Severity (S) = 4** (high — could cause foodborne illness),
- **Likelihood (L) = 3** (medium — temperature drifts occur weekly),
- **Detectability (D) = 5** (very difficult — sensor is old).

Compute the **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** and recommend a control action.

**Step 1 — Recall the RPN formula:**

$$RPN = L \times S \times D$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the assigned values:**

$$RPN = (3) \times (4) \times (5)$$

**Step 3 — Perform the multiplication in order:**

$$RPN = 3 \times 4 = 12$$

$$RPN = 12 \times 5 = 60$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the result using an industry threshold:**

| RPN Range | Risk Category | Action |
|---|---|---|
| 1 – 15 | Low | Accept / monitor |
| 16 – 40 | Moderate | Reduce via control measures |
| 41 – 100 | High | Immediate action required |

Since $60 > 40$, the risk is classified as **HIGH**.

**Step 5 — Recommendation:**
- Replace the temperature sensor.
- Install a continuous **data logger** with SMS alerts.
- Add a **second independent sensor** for redundancy.
- Train staff on **corrective action protocol** when temperature exceeds the critical limit (e.g., > 5 °C for chilled foods).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ) Calculation

**Problem Statement:** A packaged-food company has the following annual quality costs:

- Prevention cost (training, SOP development): **₹2,00,000**
- Appraisal cost (lab tests, inspections): **₹1,50,000**
- Internal failure cost (rework, scrap): **₹1,00,000**
- External failure cost (recalls, returns, complaints): **₹50,000**

Compute the **Total COPQ** and the percentage spent on **failure costs** (internal + external).

**Step 1 — Use the COPQ formula:**

$$COPQ_{total} = C_{prevention} + C_{appraisal} + C_{internal\,failure} + C_{external\,failure}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**

$$COPQ_{total} = 200{,}000 + 150{,}000 + 100{,}000 + 50{,}000$$

**Step 3 — Sum sequentially:**

$$COPQ_{total} = 350{,}000 + 100{,}000 + 50{,}000$$

$$COPQ_{total} = 450{,}000 + 50{,}000 = 5{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 4 — Compute failure cost percentage:**

$$C_{failure} = C_{internal\,failure} + C_{external\,failure} = 1{,}00{,}000 + 50{,}000 = 1{,}50{,}000$$

$$\% \text{Failure} = \dfrac{C_{failure}}{COPQ_{total}} \times 100\%$$

$$\% \text{Failure} = \dfrac{1{,}50{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} \times 100\%$$

$$\% \text{Failure} = 0.30 \times 100\% = 30\%$$

**Step 5 — Managerial Insight (KTU favourite):**
A well-managed food QMS typically has **prevention + appraisal ≥ 70%** of COPQ. A 30% failure cost indicates a reactive (not proactive) system, signalling the need to **invest more in prevention** (training, preventive maintenance, supplier qualification).

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: ISO 9001:2015 vs HACCP

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This comparison is a **guaranteed KTU question**. Understand the *differences* and *how they complement each other*.

| Parameter | ISO 9001:2015 | HACCP (Codex Alimentarius) |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | Generic management standard (all industries) | Food safety-specific (NASA + Pillsbury, 1960s) |
| **Scope** | Quality management system | Food safety hazard control |
| **Focus** | Customer satisfaction + process control | Biological, chemical, physical hazards |
| **Mandatory?** | Voluntary (market-driven) | Often mandatory under FSSAI/FDA |
| **Approach** | Process approach + risk-based thinking | 7 principles + 12 steps |
| **Documentation** | Documented information (flexible) | Mandatory HACCP plan |
| **CCP** | Does not specifically identify CCPs | Core feature: identifies CCPs and critical limits |
| **Certification** | Yes (third-party) | No universal certification; verified by regulators/audits |
| **Relationship** | Umbrella QMS | Sits *inside* the QMS as a food safety module |

**Integrated Food Safety & Quality System:**

$$\text{ISO 9001} \supset \text{HACCP} \rightarrow \text{ISO 22000 / FSSC 22000}$$

Where:
- ISO 22000 = ISO 9001 + HACCP (a food-safety-specific management system standard).
- FSSC 22000 = ISO 22000 + ISO/TS 22002-1 (PRPs) + additional requirements.

---

### 3.4 Step-by-Step: PDCA Applied to a Food Recall

**Scenario:** A batch of ready-to-eat idli batter was found to have *Listeria monocytogenes* contamination.

**PLAN:**
- Establish a **food safety policy** that requires microbiological testing before release.
- Set the **critical limit**: absence of *L. monocytogenes* in 25 g sample.

**DO:**
- Implement CCP monitoring: lab analysis of every batch.
- Train staff on sample collection and hygiene.

**CHECK:**
- Lab report comes back positive for *L. monocytogenes* in Batch 47B.
- Customer complaint log shows 3 reports of illness.

**ACT:**
- Issue a **product recall** for Batch 47B.
- Conduct a **root cause analysis** (5-Why analysis).
- Update SOP: introduce an additional pasteurization step.
- Verify effectiveness by re-testing post-pasteurization.

**Cycle Repeats** → Process is improved for the next batch.

> [!NOTE]
> The PDCA cycle is **iterative and never-ending** — it is the engine that makes ISO 9001 a **living system**, not a static certificate on the wall.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

```mermaid
    D --> C
    C --> A
    A --> P
    A -.->|Continual\nImprovement| P
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    C -->|Moderate 16-40| E[Implement Controls]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define ISO 9000. List the seven quality management principles of ISO 9000:2015.**

**Model Answer:**

**ISO 9000** is a family of international standards published by the International Organization for Standardization (ISO) that provides the **fundamentals, vocabulary, and principles** of quality management systems.

The **Seven Quality Management Principles** are:
1. Customer Focus
2. Leadership
3. Engagement of People
4. Process Approach
5. Improvement
6. Evidence-Based Decision Making
7. Relationship Management

*(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 2 marks for listing all 7 principles)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is the PDCA cycle? Explain its four stages briefly.**

**Model Answer:**

The **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) cycle** is a four-stage iterative management method used in ISO 9001:2015 for continual improvement of processes.

| Stage | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Plan** | Establish objectives, processes, and resources needed to deliver results. |
| **Do** | Execute the planned processes. |
| **Check** | Monitor and measure performance against policies, objectives, and requirements. |
| **Act** | Take actions to improve performance based on check results. |

*(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 2 marks for 4 stages)*

---

### PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

**a) Explain the evolution of the ISO 9000 family of standards. (7 Marks)**
**b) Discuss the process approach and risk-based thinking in ISO 9001:2015 with food industry examples. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Evolution of ISO 9000 (7 Marks)**

The ISO 9000 family has evolved significantly since its inception:

1. **ISO 9000:1987** — First edition; introduced 20 quality system elements. Heavily documentation-focused; often called the "tick-box" approach. *(1 mark)*

2. **ISO 9000:1994** — Clarified 1987 version; introduced concept of **preventive action** to address nonconformities proactively. *(1 mark)*

3. **ISO 9000:2000** — Major revision; introduced the **process approach** and the **8 quality management principles**. Customer focus became central. *(1.5 marks)*

4. **ISO 9000:2008** — Minor editorial revision to align with other management standards; no new requirements. *(1 mark)*

5. **ISO 9000:2015** — Major revision; reduced to **7 principles**; introduced **risk-based thinking**, **context of the organization** (Clause 4), **leadership** (Clause 5), and the **High-Level Structure (Annex SL)**. Documentation term changed from "documents and records" to **"documented information."** *(2 marks)*

6. **ISO 9001:2025 (Latest)** — Incorporates **climate change**, **life-cycle perspective**, and **emerging technologies (AI, Industry 4.0)**. *(0.5 mark)*

**Part (b) — Process Approach & Risk-Based Thinking (7 Marks)**

**Process Approach (3.5 marks):**
A **process** is a set of interrelated activities that use inputs to deliver intended outputs. The process approach treats the entire organization as a network of processes.

**Food Industry Example:** In a dairy plant,
- **Input:** Raw milk received from farm.
- **Process:** Pasteurization at 72 °C for 15 seconds.
- **Output:** Pasteurized milk, ready for packaging.
- **Control:** Temperature recorder, time clock, flow meter.
- **Feedback:** Lab test for phosphatase enzyme.

This maps directly to the **PDCA cycle** and is the foundation of food safety management.

**Risk-Based Thinking (3.5 marks):**
ISO 9001:2015 replaced "preventive action" with **risk-based thinking**. The organization must identify risks at the **QMS level** (Clause 6.1) and at the **operational level** (Clause 8).

**Food Industry Example:** A bakery identifies the risk of *Salmonella* in eggs.
- **Likelihood (L) = 4**, **Severity (S) = 5**, **Detectability (D) = 3**.
- **RPN = 4 × 5 × 3 = 60 (HIGH).**
- **Action:** Use pasteurized liquid eggs; bake at ≥ 160 °C core temperature; verify with lab testing.

This directly integrates with **HACCP** and **FSSC 22000** frameworks.

---

**Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper]**

**a) Compare and contrast ISO 9001:2015 with HACCP. How can they be integrated in a food company? (7 Marks)**
**b) A spice export company has the following quality costs for the year: Prevention = ₹3,00,000, Appraisal = ₹2,00,000, Internal Failure = ₹1,50,000, External Failure = ₹1,50,000. Calculate the total COPQ and the percentage of failure costs. Comment on the findings. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — ISO 9001 vs HACCP & Integration (7 Marks)**

| Parameter | ISO 9001:2015 | HACCP |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | Generic ISO standard, 1987 | Codex Alimentarius, 1993 |
| **Focus** | Quality management | Food safety hazards |
| **Approach** | Process + Risk-based thinking | 7 Principles + 12 Steps |
| **Mandatory** | Voluntary | Often mandatory (FSSAI) |
| **Certification** | Yes (third-party) | Verified by audit/regulator |
| **Scope** | All industries | Food only |
| **Documentation** | Documented information | Mandatory HACCP plan |

*(3.5 marks for the comparison table)*

**Integration in a Food Company (3.5 marks):**

A modern food company builds an **integrated management system**:

1. **ISO 9001** provides the **QMS umbrella** — clauses for leadership, planning, support, performance evaluation, improvement. *(1 mark)*
2. **HACCP** is implemented *within* ISO 9001's Operation clause (Clause 8) to address food safety hazards. *(1 mark)*
3. **PRPs (Prerequisite Programmes)** like sanitation, pest control, personal hygiene form Clause 7 (Support). *(0.5 mark)*
4. Together they form **ISO 22000** and can be extended to **FSSC 22000** for global market acceptance (FSSAI, FDA, EU). *(1 mark)*

**Integration Diagram:**

$$\text{ISO 9001 (QMS Umbrella)} \supset \text{PRPs} \cup \text{HACCP} = \text{ISO 22000}$$

**Part (b) — COPQ Calculation (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Total COPQ:**

$$COPQ_{total} = C_{prevention} + C_{appraisal} + C_{internal\,failure} + C_{external\,failure}$$

$$COPQ_{total} = 3{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000$$

$$COPQ_{total} = 8{,}00{,}000$$

*(2 marks for formula + 1 mark for substitution + 1 mark for answer)*

**Step 2 — Failure Costs:**

$$C_{failure} = 1{,}50{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 = 3{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 3 — Failure Cost Percentage:**

$$\% \text{Failure} = \dfrac{3{,}00{,}000}{8{,}00{,}000} \times 100\% = 37.5\%$$

*(2 marks)*

**Step 4 — Managerial Comment (1 mark):**

A 37.5% failure cost is **high**. The company is **reactive** rather than proactive. **Recommendation:** Increase investment in **prevention** (better raw material testing, supplier qualification, employee training) and **appraisal** (online inspection systems) to reduce internal and external failure costs. A benchmark of a mature QMS has failure cost < 20% of COPQ.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse ISO 9000 with ISO 9001.** ISO 9000 is the *vocabulary* standard; ISO 9001 is the *requirement* (certifiable) standard. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this confusion.
> 2. **PDCA must be written in full** — Plan, Do, Check, Act — not just summarized as "continuous improvement." Always explain the food-industry application.
> 3. **The 7 principles must be remembered in order** — Student-friendly trick: "**C**ustomers **L**ove **E**ngineering **P**rocesses **I**mproving **E**vidence **R**elationships" (C-L-E-P-I-E-R).
> 4. **In COPQ problems**, show **all** summation steps — partial marks are awarded for each step.
> 5. **For comparison questions (ISO vs HACCP)**, a **table format** scores better than paragraph format.
> 6. **Cite the year (2015)** whenever mentioning ISO 9001 — older versions (1994, 2000, 2008) follow different clause structures and will lose marks.

---

### 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — ISO 9000**

- ✅ **ISO 9000:2015** = *Fundamentals and Vocabulary* (cannot be certified).
- ✅ **ISO 9001:2015** = *QMS Requirements* (the certifiable standard).
- ✅ **ISO 9004:2018** = *Sustained success* guidance.
- ✅ **ISO 19011:2018** = *Audit guidelines*.
- ✅ **Seven Principles**: Customer Focus, Leadership, Engagement of People, Process Approach, Improvement, Evidence-Based Decision Making, Relationship Management.
- ✅ **PDCA** = Plan → Do → Check → Act (iterative improvement cycle).
- ✅ **Annex SL** = High-Level Structure shared across all ISO management standards (9001, 14001, 22000, 45001).
- ✅ **10 Clauses of ISO 9001:2015**: Scope → Normative Refs → Definitions → Context → Leadership → Planning → Support → Operation → Performance Evaluation → Improvement.
- ✅ **Risk-Based Thinking** replaced "Preventive Action" in 2015 revision.
- ✅ **RPN = L × S × D** — Risk Priority Number formula.
- ✅ **COPQ** = Prevention + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure.
- ✅ **ISO 9001 is generic**; **HACCP is food-specific**; **ISO 22000 = ISO 9001 + HACCP**; **FSSC 22000 = ISO 22000 + PRPs**.
- ✅ **Documented Information** is the modern term (replaces "documents and records").
- ✅ **Top management commitment** is a critical 2015 addition — leadership cannot be delegated.
- ✅ **Continual improvement** is a *lifelong* requirement, not a one-time project.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
