## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define ISO 17025. What is its significance in food testing laboratories?**

**Model Answer:**
ISO/IEC 17025 is the international standard specifying the **general requirements for the competence of testing and calibration laboratories**. It is published jointly by **ISO (International Organization for Standardization)** and **IEC (International Electrotechnical Commission)**, with the current edition being **ISO/IEC 17025:2017**.

**Significance in food testing laboratories:**
- Establishes **technical competence** of labs performing microbiological, chemical, and nutritional analysis of food.
- Ensures **international acceptance** of test reports through ILAC and APAC MRAs.
- Required for **FSSAI notification** of food testing labs under Section 43 of the FSS Act, 2006.
- Provides **legal admissibility** of test reports in food safety disputes and court proceedings.
- Mandates **metrological traceability**, **measurement uncertainty estimation**, and **proficiency testing** — three pillars of technical validity.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] List the two general requirements of ISO 17025:2017 and explain why "impartiality" is critical in food testing.**

**Model Answer:**
The two **General Requirements** under **Clause 4** of ISO 17025:2017 are:

1. **Impartiality (Clause 4.1)**
2. **Confidentiality (Clause 4.2)**

**Why Impartiality is critical in food testing:**
- Food test results have **direct public health and economic consequences** — a falsely low pesticide residue report can lead to consumer poisoning; a falsely high result can destroy a brand.
- Impartiality ensures results are **not influenced** by commercial, financial, or personal pressures from the client, parent company, or competing brands.
- The lab must **continuously identify risks to impartiality** (e.g., when a lab is owned by a food manufacturer whose products it tests) and **mitigate or eliminate** them.
- Without demonstrated impartiality, the test report has **no legal or regulatory standing**.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**[A] Explain in detail the structure of ISO/IEC 17025:2017. Discuss its resource requirements and process requirements with specific reference to a food testing laboratory.** **(14 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Structure of ISO 17025:2017** **(7 Marks)**

ISO/IEC 17025:2017, titled *"General requirements for the competence of testing and calibration laboratories,"* is the current international standard published jointly by ISO and IEC. The standard is structured into **8 clauses (0 through 7)** plus informative annexes, following the **High-Level Structure (HLS)** of ISO 9001:2015:

- **Clause 0 – Introduction** – Sets the context and emphasizes the lab's responsibility for risk-based planning.
- **Clause 1 – Scope** – Specifies that the standard applies to all organizations performing laboratory activities, regardless of sector.
- **Clause 2 – Normative References** – Lists referenced standards (e.g., ISO/IEC Guide 99, ISO 9001).
- **Clause 3 – Terms and Definitions** – Defines key terminology.
- **Clause 4 – General Requirements** – Covers Impartiality (4.1) and Confidentiality (4.2).
- **Clause 5 – Structural Requirements** – Legal identity, organizational responsibilities, role allocation, and risk ownership.
- **Clause 6 – Resource Requirements** – The "inputs" of testing: personnel, facilities, equipment, metrological traceability, and external supplies.
- **Clause 7 – Process Requirements** – The "execution" of testing: method selection, sampling, sample handling, technical records, MU, validity checks, reporting, complaints, non-conforming work, and data control.
- **Clause 8 – Management Requirements** – Documented information, records control, risk and opportunity, internal audits, management reviews, corrective actions.

The 2017 version introduced **two major philosophical shifts** from the 2005 version: (i) **risk-based thinking** and (ii) the **process approach (PDCA cycle)**, aligning it with ISO 9001:2015.

**Part (b) — Resource and Process Requirements for a Food Lab** **(7 Marks)**

**Resource Requirements (Clause 6):**

A food testing laboratory requires:

1. **Competent Personnel (6.2):** Microbiologists, chemists, and analysts must be trained on specific methods (e.g., ISO 21528 for *Enterobacteriaceae*, AOAC methods for pesticide residues). Training records, competency assessments, and ongoing professional development must be documented.

2. **Facilities & Environmental Conditions (6.3):** Separate areas for microbiological and chemical analysis; controlled temperature (20 ± 3°C) for instrument rooms; BSL-2 cabinets for pathogen work; segregated zones to prevent cross-contamination (e.g., gluten-free testing area isolated from gluten-handling zones).

3. **Equipment (6.4):** HPLC, GC-MS, AAS, PCR machines, balances, pH meters, incubators, autoclaves — each must be calibrated, uniquely identified, and have maintenance logs.

4. **Metrological Traceability (6.5):** Reference standards for aflatoxin quantification must be traceable to SI through certified reference materials from NIST, IRMM, or equivalent. Balances must be calibrated using traceable mass standards.

5. **External Supplies (6.6):** Culture media, reagents, and consumables must be sourced from qualified suppliers with documented purchase specifications and incoming inspection.

**Process Requirements (Clause 7):**

1. **Method Selection (7.2):** Use validated international methods (ISO, AOAC, FSSAI) or verify in-house methods with documented performance data.
2. **Sample Handling (7.4):** Maintain cold chain (4 ± 2°C) for perishable food samples; record sample condition on receipt.
3. **Measurement Uncertainty (7.6):** Compute MU for every test (e.g., lead in canned fish reported as 0.182 ± 0.015 mg/kg at 95% confidence).
4. **Validity Checks (7.7):** Run certified reference materials in each batch; participate in **FAPAS or FSSAI PT schemes**; monitor z-scores.
5. **Reporting (7.8):** Test reports must include client ID, sample ID, method reference, results with units, MU, statement of compliance (where applicable), and the authorized signatory.

These integrated requirements ensure that every test report issued by a food lab is **technically valid, traceable, and internationally defensible**.

---

#### **QUESTION B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**[B] (a) What is the difference between ISO 17025 and ISO 9001? Why is ISO 17025 specifically required for food testing laboratories?** **(7 Marks)**

**(b) Explain the role of NABL in accrediting food testing laboratories in India. How does NABL accreditation enable international acceptance of Indian food test reports?** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Difference between ISO 17025 and ISO 9001, and need for ISO 17025 in food testing** **(7 Marks)**

**Differences between ISO 17025:2017 and ISO 9001:2015:**

| Basis | ISO 9001:2015 | ISO 17025:2017 |
|---|---|---|
| Scope | Generic QMS for any organization | Specifically for testing/calibration labs |
| Technical Competence | Not required | Mandatory |
| Metrological Traceability | Not required | Required (Clause 6.5) |
| Measurement Uncertainty | Not mandated | Mandatory (Clause 7.6) |
| Proficiency Testing | Not required | Required (Clause 7.7) |
| Method Validation | Not addressed | Required (Clause 7.2) |
| Focus | Customer satisfaction + QMS | Validity + reliability of test results |

**Why ISO 17025 is specifically required for food testing:**

Food testing has **life-or-death consequences** — wrong aflatoxin results can cause liver cancer outbreaks; incorrect pesticide residue data can trigger mass poisoning. Therefore, beyond a generic QMS (ISO 9001), food labs must demonstrate:

- **Technical competence** to perform microbiological and chemical analysis.
- **Metrological traceability** of every measurement to international standards.
- **Measurement uncertainty** for every reported result.
- **Independent validation** of competence through Proficiency Testing.

Without ISO 17025 accreditation, an Indian food lab's reports would be **rejected at international borders** (e.g., by EU's RASFF system) and would not be **legally admissible** in Indian food safety courts.

**Part (b) — Role of NABL and international acceptance** **(7 Marks)**

**NABL (National Accreditation Board for Testing and Calibration Laboratories):**

NABL is a **constituent board of the Quality Council of India (QCI)**, which was established under the **Department of Industry and Internal Trade, Ministry of Commerce, Government of India**. NABL is the **sole national accreditation body** in India for testing and calibration laboratories, including food testing labs.

**Roles of NABL:**

1. **Accreditation:** NABL assesses and accredits food testing labs against ISO/IEC 17025:2017, granting accreditation in specific test methods (e.g., "Aflatoxin B1 in cereals by HPLC," "*Salmonella* in meat by ISO 6579").
2. **Surveillance:** Annual surveillance audits ensure continued compliance.
3. **Reassessment:** Full reassessment every 4 years.
4. **Proficiency Testing Coordination:** NABL organizes inter-laboratory comparisons.
5. **Reference to Standards:** NABL bridges labs with **BIS (Bureau of Indian Standards)** and **FSSAI** for regulatory linkage.

**International Acceptance Mechanism:**

NABL is a signatory to:
- **ILAC MRA (International Laboratory Accreditation Cooperation – Mutual Recognition Arrangement)**
- **APAC MRA (Asia Pacific Accreditation Cooperation – MRA)**

These MRAs establish that **NABL-accredited lab reports are equivalent to reports from labs accredited by any other ILAC/APAC signatory body worldwide** (e.g., A2LA in USA, UKAS in UK, NATA in Australia, DAkkS in Germany). Therefore, an Indian food exporter's test report from a NABL-accredited lab is **accepted by importing countries** without the need for re-testing. This dramatically reduces trade friction, prevents rejection at foreign ports, and ensures **legal and commercial credibility** of Indian food exports in the global market.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in ISO 17025 Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing ISO 17025 with ISO 9000 / 9001.** Many students write generic QMS content. Examiners expect specific mention of **technical competence, metrological traceability, and measurement uncertainty** as the distinguishing pillars.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to cite the year (2017).** The standard is currently **ISO/IEC 17025:2017**. Writing only "ISO 17025" without the year costs marks.
>
> 3. **Mixing up NABL with BIS or FSSAI.** NABL is the **accreditation body** (not regulator). FSSAI is the **regulator**. BIS sets **product standards**. Each has a distinct role.
>
> 4. **Ignoring the "Risk-Based Thinking" concept.** A 2017-version answer that does not mention risk-based thinking or PDCA is marked outdated.
>
> 5. **In MU problems, students forget the coverage factor $k=2$.** Always report **expanded uncertainty**, not just combined standard uncertainty.
>
> 6. **PT z-score questions:** Students often misclassify $\vert z \vert = 2.5$ as "satisfactory." It is **"questionable"** (i.e., $2.0 < \vert z \vert < 3.0$).
>
> 7. **Misnaming clauses.** Clause 6 is *Resource Requirements* (not "Personnel" alone). Clause 7 is *Process Requirements* (not "Methods" alone).

---

### 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> ✅ **ISO/IEC 17025:2017** = the current international standard for **competence** of testing and calibration labs.
>
> ✅ **Issuing body:** Jointly by **ISO and IEC** (note: it's ISO *and* IEC, not ISO alone).
>
> ✅ **Four mandatory requirement clauses:** 4 (General), 5 (Structural), 6 (Resource), 7 (Process).
>
> ✅ **Two General Requirements (Clause 4):** Impartiality + Confidentiality.
>
> ✅ **Three pillars of technical competence (Clause 6.5 + 7.6 + 7.7):** Metrological Traceability + Measurement Uncertainty + Proficiency Testing.
>
> ✅ **Measurement Uncertainty formula:** $U = k \cdot u_c$ with $k = 2$ for ~95% confidence.
>
> ✅ **PT z-score formula:** $z = (x_{lab} - x_{assigned}) / s_{pt}$; $\vert z \vert \le 2$ = satisfactory.
>
> ✅ **GUM RSS formula:** $u_c = \sqrt{\sum (c_i u(x_i))^2}$ where $c_i = \partial f / \partial x_i$.
>
> ✅ **PDCA cycle:** Plan (Cl 4–6) → Do (Cl 7) → Check (Cl 8.6–8.7) → Act (Cl 8.8–8.9).
>
> ✅ **Key difference from ISO 9001:** ISO 17025 demands **technical competence + MU + traceability + PT**; ISO 9001 demands only a QMS.
>
> ✅ **NABL** = India's sole accreditation body; under QCI.
>
> ✅ **NABL is signatory to ILAC MRA and APAC MRA** → enables global acceptance of Indian food test reports.
>
> ✅ **FSSAI mandate:** ISO 17025 (via NABL) is mandatory for food testing labs seeking FSSAI notification under Section 43 of the FSS Act, 2006.
>
> ✅ **2017 version innovations:** Risk-based thinking + Process approach + Aligned with ISO 9001:2015 HLS.
>
> ✅ **Mandatory report content (Clause 7.8):** Client ID, sample ID, method, results with units, MU, statement of compliance, authorized signatory.
>
> ✅ **Mnemonics:** *"I am Confident"* (Clause 4: Impartiality + Confidentiality); *"People Place Stuff"* (Clause 6: Personnel, Facilities, Equipment).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: FSSC 22000
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT503 - FOOD SAFETY AND QUALITY REGULATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** FSSC 22000

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FSSC 22000 (Food Safety System Certification 22000)** is a complete, internationally recognized **food safety management system (FSMS) certification scheme**, owned by the **Foundation for Food Safety Certification (FSSC)**. It is built upon three core components: **ISO 22000:2018** (the FSMS requirements), **ISO/TS 22002-1:2009** (prerequisite programmes on food manufacturing), and a set of **Additional Requirements (ARs)** defined by the FSSC organization. It provides a robust framework for managing food safety risks across the entire supply chain and is **GFSI (Global Food Safety Initiative) benchmarked**.

The current publicly established version is **FSSC 22000 Version 6**, which is aligned with the high-level structure (HLS) of ISO 22000:2018 and the updated GFSI Benchmarking Requirements v2020.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping:** This topic directly maps to **CO4** – *Apply international food safety standards and certification schemes in the food industry*. The learner must be able to differentiate FSSC 22000 from HACCP, ISO 22000, and BRC, and describe its structure, implementation, and certification process.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Airport Security" Metaphor for Food Safety:**
> Imagine an international airport. Multiple security layers exist: ticket verification (basic screening), body scanning (detailed inspection), baggage checks (specific threat detection), and behavioral observation (continuous monitoring). A single layer is not enough. **FSSC 22000 works exactly like this multi-layered security model for food.** ISO 22000 is the master "security protocol," ISO/TS 22002-1 provides the "standard operating procedures" for manufacturing zones, and the Additional Requirements are the "elite customs and intelligence checks" that make the system globally acceptable (GFSI-recognized). Together, they ensure that no contaminated, mislabeled, or hazardous food product leaves the "airport" (food facility) and boards the "flight" (reaches the consumer).

### 1.3 Core Components Snapshot

The scheme rests on **three pillars**, often visualized as an integrated stack:

1. **ISO 22000:2018** – Core FSMS requirements (Plan-Do-Check-Act, risk-based thinking, leadership commitment).
2. **ISO/TS 22002-1:2009** – Sector-specific prerequisite programmes (PRPs) for food and feed manufacturing.
3. **FSSC 22000 Additional Requirements (ARs)** – FSSC-specific clauses on:
   - Quality management
   - Food defense
   - Food fraud prevention
   - Allergen management
   - Environmental monitoring
   - Compliance with regulatory requirements

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Recognized Authorities

| Parameter | Detail |
|---|---|
| Scheme Owner | Foundation for Food Safety Certification (FSSC), Netherlands |
| GFSI Recognition | Yes (since 2010) |
| Accreditation Body | International Accreditation Forum (IAF) members |
| Current Version | Version 6 (V6) |
| Certificate Validity | **3 years** (with surveillance audits at 12 and 24 months) |
| Audit Duration | Minimum 1 to 5 days depending on organization size |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Architecture — The Three-Layer Model

FSSC 22000 is not a single standard but a **schematic integration** of multiple standards. Each layer has a specific role:

**Layer 1 — ISO 22000:2018 (Foundation Layer)**
- Provides the **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA) cycle** and **High-Level Structure (HLS)**.
- Includes **10 clauses**: Context of organization, Leadership, Planning, Support, Operation, Performance evaluation, Improvement.
- Implements **HACCP principles** combined with **prerequisite programmes (PRPs)** and **operational prerequisite programmes (OPRPs)**.
- Introduces **risk-based thinking** at both organizational and operational levels.

**Layer 2 — ISO/TS 22002-1:2009 (Technical Specification Layer)**
- Defines **PRPs specific to food and feed manufacturing**.
- Covers 17 categories of requirements: construction and layout of buildings, equipment, cleaning, sanitation, personal hygiene, training, supplier control, storage, etc.
- Acts as the **technical backbone** ensuring baseline hygienic conditions before HACCP is applied.

**Layer 3 — FSSC Additional Requirements (Augmentation Layer)**
- Closes the gaps identified by **GFSI Benchmarking Requirements**.
- Includes clauses on:
  - **Food Defense** (Clause 2.5.1) — Protection against intentional adulteration
  - **Food Fraud Prevention** (Clause 2.5.2) — VACCP (Vulnerability Assessment and Critical Control Points)
  - **Allergen Management** (Clause 2.5.3)
  - **Environmental Monitoring** (Clause 2.5.4) — for pathogens like *Listeria* and *Salmonella*
  - **Quality Management** (Clause 2.5.5) — to distinguish FSSC from pure safety
  - **Communication** with stakeholders
  - **Management of services and purchased products**

### 2.2 High-Yield Formula Sheet (Conceptual Models)

> [!NOTE]
> The following "formulae" are conceptual and structural, used by examiners to test student understanding of how FSSC 22000 is built. No physical constants apply, but the relationships are critical.

$$
\text{FSSC 22000} = \text{ISO 22000:2018} + \text{ISO/TS 22002-1} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Additional Requirements}_i
$$

$$
\text{Total Audit Man-Days} = f(\text{Number of Employees}, \text{Number of Sites}, \text{Complexity})
$$

$$
\text{Certificate Validity} = 3 \text{ years}
$$

$$
\text{Surveillance Frequency} = \frac{1}{\text{year}} \text{ (years 1 and 2)}
$$

| Conceptual Variable | Definition | Typical Value / Boundary |
|---|---|---|
| $n_{AR}$ | Number of Additional Requirements | $\geq 17$ clauses |
| $T_{audit}$ | Total audit time | $1 \text{ to } 5 \text{ days}$ |
| $V_{cert}$ | Certificate validity period | **36 months** |
| $f_{surv}$ | Surveillance audit frequency | Every 12 months |
| $N_{NC}$ | Number of Non-Conformities | Major (critical), Minor |
| $GFSI$ | GFSI Recognition status | **Benchmarked** |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering / Industry Utility

In the food industry, FSSC 22000 serves multiple **engineering and managerial utilities**:

1. **Market Access** — Many global retailers (Walmart, Tesco, Carrefour, Metro) require GFSI-benchmarked certification. FSSC 22000 grants automatic acceptance.
2. **Risk Reduction** — The HACCP + PRP + OPRP structure reduces foodborne illness incidents by **systematic hazard identification**.
3. **Brand Protection** — The food fraud and food defense clauses protect companies from economic and reputational damage.
4. **Regulatory Alignment** — Helps comply with **FSSAI (India), FDA FSMA (USA), EU Regulation 178/2002**, and **Codex Alimentarius**.
5. **Process Optimization** — The PDCA cycle and risk-based thinking drive **continuous improvement** (Kaizen-like benefits) in manufacturing operations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Insight:** KTU questions on FSSC 22000 often ask students to **list the three components** of the scheme and **briefly explain the role of GFSI**. Memorizing the layer structure yields **3 to 5 marks instantly** in Part A questions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Step-by-Step Derivation: Building an FSSC 22000 System

**Step 1 — Context Analysis (ISO 22000 Clause 4)**

$$
\text{Context} = \{\text{Internal Issues}\} \cup \{\text{External Issues}\} \cup \{\text{Needs of Interested Parties}\}
$$

Logic: The organization identifies all internal/external issues (e.g., regulatory environment, market trends) and interested parties (consumers, regulators, suppliers). This defines the **scope of the FSMS**.

**Step 2 — Leadership Commitment (Clause 5)**

$$
\text{Leadership Outputs} = \{\text{FSMS Policy},\ \text{Organizational Roles},\ \text{Resource Authorization}\}
$$

Logic: Top management must demonstrate commitment by establishing policy, assigning responsibilities, and authorizing resources.

**Step 3 — Risk-Based Planning (Clause 6)**

$$
\text{Risk} = f(\text{Likelihood},\ \text{Severity}) \Rightarrow \text{Risk Matrix}
$$

Logic: The organization identifies risks and opportunities. Risk levels are determined by combining likelihood (rare, unlikely, possible, likely, certain) and severity (insignificant, minor, moderate, major, catastrophic).

**Step 4 — Support Mechanisms (Clause 7)**

$$
\text{Support} = \{\text{Resources},\ \text{Competence},\ \text{Awareness},\ \text{Communication},\ \text{Documented Information}\}
$$

Logic: Resources (human, infrastructure, financial) and competent personnel are ensured. Internal and external communication processes are defined.

**Step 5 — Operational Planning and HACCP Implementation (Clause 8)**

$$
\text{HACCP Plan} = \sum_{i=1}^{7} \text{Principle}_i
$$

The **7 Codex HACCP Principles** are:
1. Conduct hazard analysis
2. Determine Critical Control Points (CCPs)
3. Establish critical limits
4. Establish monitoring procedures
5. Establish corrective actions
6. Establish verification procedures
7. Establish record-keeping and documentation procedures

Combined with **PRPs** and **OPRPs** from ISO/TS 22002-1.

**Step 6 — Performance Evaluation (Clause 9)**

$$
\text{Evaluation} = \{\text{Monitoring},\ \text{Internal Audits},\ \text{Management Review}\}
$$

Logic: The organization monitors FSMS performance, conducts internal audits, and performs management reviews at planned intervals.

**Step 7 — Improvement (Clause 10)**

$$
\text{Improvement Loop} = \text{NC} \rightarrow \text{CA} \rightarrow \text{Verification} \rightarrow \text{PDCA Cycle Closure}
$$

Logic: Non-conformities trigger corrective actions, which are verified for effectiveness, completing the PDCA cycle.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Food Safety Systems

| Parameter | HACCP | ISO 22000 | FSSC 22000 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Year of Origin | 1960s (Pillsbury) | 2005 (revised 2018) | 2009 (current v6) |
| Scope | Food safety only | FSMS | Complete GFSI-benchmarked scheme |
| Structure | 7 principles | 10 clauses (HLS) | ISO 22000 + TS 22002-1 + ARs |
| Prerequisite Programmes | Implicit | Explicit | Explicit + sector-specific |
| GFSI Recognition | No | No | **Yes** |
| Food Defense / Fraud | Not included | Limited | **Explicitly included** |
| Certificate Issuance | Self-declaration | Third-party | **Third-party accredited** |
| Validity | Continuous | 3 years | **3 years** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Note:** This comparison table is a **high-yield chart** and has appeared in multiple KTU model question papers. A student should be able to draw this table in 2 minutes during a 14-mark question.

### 3.3 Certification Process Flow — Derivation of Stages

The certification process can be expressed as a sequential function:

$$
\text{Certification} = \text{Stage}_1 \rightarrow \text{Stage}_2 \rightarrow \text{Surveillance}_1 \rightarrow \text{Surveillance}_2 \rightarrow \text{Re-cert}
$$

Where:
- $\text{Stage}_1$ = Documentation review and readiness audit
- $\text{Stage}_2$ = On-site certification audit
- $\text{Surveillance}_1$ = Year 1 follow-up audit
- \text{Surveillance}_2$ = Year 2 follow-up audit
- \text{Re-cert} = Re-certification audit (Year 3)

If **major non-conformities** are found, certification is denied until closure is verified. Minor non-conformities require corrective action within **90 days**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of FSSC 22000

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **integrated architecture** of FSSC 22000, showing how the three layers interact with operational functions.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L1["LAYER 1: ISO 22000 2018 FOUNDATION"]
        direction LR
        A1[Clause 4 Context of Organization]
        A2[Clause 5 Leadership]
        A3[Clause 6 Planning Risk Based Thinking]
        A4[Clause 7 Support Resources Competence]
        A5[Clause 8 Operation HACCP PRPs OPRPs]
        A6[Clause 9 Performance Evaluation]
        A7[Clause 10 Improvement]
    end

    subgraph L2["LAYER 2: ISO TS 22002-1 PRPs"]
        direction LR
        B1[Building Construction and Layout]
        B2[Equipment and Utensils]
        B3[Cleaning and Sanitation]
        B4[Personal Hygiene]
        B5[Supplier Control]
        B6[Storage and Distribution]
    end

    subgraph L3["LAYER 3: FSSC ADDITIONAL REQUIREMENTS"]
        direction LR
        C1[Food Defense]
        C2[Food Fraud Prevention VACCP]
        C3[Allergen Management]
        C4[Environmental Monitoring]
        C5[Quality Management]
        C6[Regulatory Compliance]
    end

    subgraph OUT["OUTPUT: GFSI RECOGNIZED CERTIFICATION"]
        D1[Stage 1 Documentation Audit]
        D2[Stage 2 On Site Audit]
        D3[Surveillance Year 1 and 2]
        D4[Re Certification Year 3]
    end

    A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 --> A5 --> A6 --> A7
    B1 --> A5
    B2 --> A5
    B3 --> A5
    B4 --> A5
    B5 --> A5
    B6 --> A5
    C1 --> A5
    C2 --> A5
    C3 --> A5
    C4 --> A5
    C5 --> A5
    C6 --> A5
    A7 --> D1 --> D2 --> D3 --> D4
```

### 4.2 Process Flow Diagram — FSSC 22000 Implementation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Step 1 Gap Analysis] --> P2[Step 2 Define FSMS Scope]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3 Establish PRPs from ISO TS 22002-1]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4 Conduct Hazard Analysis]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5 Identify CCPs and OPRPs]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6 Establish Critical Limits and Monitoring]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7 Implement Food Defense and Fraud Plans]
    P7 --> P8[Step 8 Internal Audit and Management Review]
    P8 --> P9[Step 9 Stage 1 Certification Audit]
    P9 --> P10[Step 10 Stage 2 Certification Audit]
    P10 --> P11[Step 11 Issue Certificate Valid 3 Years]
    P11 --> P12[Step 12 Surveillance Audits Annual]
    P12 --> P13[Step 13 Re Certification Audit]
```

### 4.3 Schematic of Hazard Analysis Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    H1[All Identified Hazards] --> H2{Is it Significant}
    H2 -->|No| H3[Controlled by PRPs]
    H2 -->|Yes| H4{Does CCP Apply}
    H4 -->|No| H5[Control via OPRP]
    H4 -->|Yes| H6[Establish CCP with Critical Limits]
    H3 --> H7[Documented in FSMS]
    H5 --> H7
    H6 --> H7
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation Note for Students:** The above diagrams are not literal engineering schematics but **functional architecture block diagrams** that map how FSSC 22000's components interact. In KTU examinations, drawing such block diagrams (with proper labels) in Part B 14-mark answers typically earns **2 to 3 extra marks** under the "presentation and clarity" assessment criteria.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define FSSC 22000. Name its three core components.**

**Model Answer:**
FSSC 22000 is a complete, GFSI-recognized food safety management system certification scheme owned by the Foundation for Food Safety Certification. Its three core components are:
1. **ISO 22000:2018** — Core FSMS requirements.
2. **ISO/TS 22002-1:2009** — Sector-specific prerequisite programmes (PRPs) for food manufacturing.
3. **FSSC 22000 Additional Requirements** — Including food defense, food fraud prevention, allergen management, and quality management.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] What is the role of GFSI in food safety certification?**

**Model Answer:**
The **Global Food Safety Initiative (GFSI)**, managed by The Consumer Goods Forum, is a non-profit organization that **benchmarks food safety certification schemes** against its Benchmarking Requirements. Certification schemes that meet GFSI standards (such as FSSC 22000, BRC, SQF) gain **worldwide recognition**, allowing manufacturers to access global markets without the need for multiple certifications.

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 4 Internal Choice Pattern:** In KTU 2024 ESE, Module 4 Part B questions offer an internal choice between two questions. We provide both alternatives below.

---

**QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain in detail the three components of FSSC 22000 with the role of each component.**

**Model Answer:**

**Component 1: ISO 22000:2018 (Foundation Standard)**
ISO 22000 specifies the requirements for a Food Safety Management System (FSMS). It is built on the High-Level Structure (HLS) shared by all ISO management system standards and follows the PDCA cycle. It integrates **HACCP principles** with **prerequisite programmes (PRPs)** and **operational prerequisite programmes (OPRPs)**. It also introduces **risk-based thinking** at both organizational and operational levels. The 10 clauses cover context, leadership, planning, support, operation, performance evaluation, and improvement.

**Component 2: ISO/TS 22002-1:2009 (Sector-Specific PRPs)**
This Technical Specification provides detailed prerequisite programmes for organizations in the food and feed manufacturing chain. It covers 17 categories including:
- Construction and layout of buildings
- Design and layout of equipment
- Cleaning and sanitation programmes
- Pest control
- Personal hygiene
- Water, air, and energy supply
- Waste management
- Storage and transport
- Supplier control and incoming materials
- Documentation and records

It ensures that **baseline hygienic conditions** are established before HACCP is applied.

**Component 3: FSSC 22000 Additional Requirements (ARs)**
The Additional Requirements close gaps to meet GFSI Benchmarking Requirements. They include:
- **Food Defense** (protection against intentional contamination)
- **Food Fraud Prevention** using VACCP
- **Allergen Management**
- **Environmental Monitoring** for pathogens
- **Quality Management** to distinguish FSSC from pure safety
- **Communication** with authorities and stakeholders
- **Management of services and purchased products**

**(b) [7 Marks] Describe the certification process for FSSC 22000. What is the validity of the certificate?**

**Model Answer:**

The FSSC 22000 certification process involves the following stages:

**Step 1: Application and Scope Definition**
The organization defines the scope of its FSMS and applies to an accredited certification body (CB).

**Step 2: Stage 1 Audit (Documentation Review)**
The CB reviews the FSMS documentation for compliance with all three components. The audit is performed **on-site or remotely** and identifies readiness for Stage 2.

**Step 3: Stage 2 Audit (On-Site Certification Audit)**
Auditors verify the implementation and effectiveness of the FSMS at the organization's premises. They check HACCP plans, PRPs, OPRPs, food defense, food fraud prevention, allergen management, and additional requirements.

**Step 4: Non-Conformity Management**
- **Major NCs** must be closed within 60 days.
- **Minor NCs** must be addressed within 90 days.
- If the organization fails, certification is denied.

**Step 5: Certificate Issuance**
Upon successful closure of all NCs, the CB issues the FSSC 22000 certificate, valid for **3 years (36 months)**.

**Step 6: Surveillance Audits**
Conducted **annually** (at 12 and 24 months) to verify continued compliance.

**Step 7: Re-Certification**
At the end of 3 years, a full re-certification audit is conducted for renewal.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often write only 3–4 stages and miss the surveillance and re-certification stages. **Loss of 2 marks.** Also, students forget to mention the **60-day (Major NC) and 90-day (Minor NC) closure timelines** — a frequent 1-mark loss in Part B answers.

---

**QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks] Compare FSSC 22000 with HACCP and ISO 22000. What are the advantages of FSSC 22000 over these systems?**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | HACCP | ISO 22000 | FSSC 22000 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Year of Origin | 1960s | 2005 (rev 2018) | 2009 (v6) |
| Scope | Food safety only | FSMS | Full GFSI scheme |
| PRPs | Implicit | Explicit | Detailed + sector-specific |
| Food Defense / Fraud | Not included | Limited | **Explicitly included** |
| GFSI Recognition | No | No | **Yes** |
| Certification | Self-declaration | Third-party | **Accredited third-party** |

**Advantages of FSSC 22000:**
1. **Global Acceptance** — GFSI recognition means a single certificate is accepted by major global retailers.
2. **Comprehensive Coverage** — Combines food safety, food defense, food fraud, and quality.
3. **Structured PRPs** — Detailed ISO/TS 22002-1 ensures robust baseline conditions.
4. **Risk-Based Approach** — Two-level risk analysis (organizational and operational) is built in.
5. **Continuous Improvement** — PDCA cycle drives ongoing enhancement.
6. **Credibility** — Backed by accredited certification bodies under the IAF framework.

**(b) [7 Marks] Explain the concepts of Food Defense and Food Fraud Prevention under FSSC 22000. Why are they important?**

**Model Answer:**

**Food Defense (Clause 2.5.1 of FSSC 22000 ARs):**
Food Defense refers to the **protection of food products from intentional adulteration or contamination** by malicious actors (e.g., disgruntled employees, terrorist groups, saboteurs). Under FSSC 22000, organizations must:
- Conduct a **food defense threat assessment**.
- Identify vulnerable points in the supply chain.
- Implement **security measures** (access control, surveillance, employee screening).
- Develop a **food defense plan** and a **food defense team**.

**Food Fraud Prevention (Clause 2.5.2 of FSSC 22000 ARs):**
Food Fraud refers to the **deliberate substitution, dilution, mislabeling, or misrepresentation of food products for economic gain**. FSSC 22000 requires organizations to use the **VACCP (Vulnerability Assessment and Critical Control Points)** methodology:
- Identify vulnerable commodities and suppliers.
- Assess the **likelihood** and **severity** of fraud.
- Implement **mitigation measures** (e.g., supplier audits, authenticity testing).
- Maintain a **food fraud mitigation plan**.

**Importance:**
1. **Consumer Protection** — Prevents harm from malicious contamination.
2. **Brand Reputation** — Protects companies from public scandals.
3. **Regulatory Compliance** — Aligns with FSMA (USA) and EU food law.
4. **Economic Loss Prevention** — The food fraud industry is estimated to cost **$10–15 billion annually** worldwide.
5. **Supply Chain Integrity** — Ensures traceability from raw material to finished product.

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes to Avoid in FSSC 22000 Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing FSSC with ISO 22000** — FSSC is a **certification scheme** that **includes** ISO 22000. They are not the same.
> 2. **Forgetting the third component** — Students often write only ISO 22000 and PRPs, missing the **Additional Requirements**.
> 3. **Ignoring GFSI** — Always mention **GFSI benchmarking** in any FSSC-related question; it is the scheme's key advantage.
> 4. **Mixing up VACCP and TACCP** — VACCP = Vulnerability (Food Fraud); TACCP = Threat (Food Defense). Examiners test this distinction.
> 5. **Skipping surveillance audits** — Certificate is **3 years valid**, with **annual surveillance** audits. Missing this costs 1–2 marks.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for FSSC 22000:**
> - FSSC = ISO 22000 + ISO/TS 22002-1 + Additional Requirements.
> - Owned by **Foundation for Food Safety Certification (FSSC), Netherlands**.
> - Current version: **V6**; **GFSI benchmarked**.
> - **Certificate validity = 3 years**; **surveillance audits annually**.
> - **7 HACCP principles** + PRPs + OPRPs are the operational backbone.
> - **Food Defense** = TACCP (Threat Assessment); **Food Fraud** = VACCP (Vulnerability Assessment).
> - **Allergen management** and **environmental monitoring** are ARs unique to FSSC.
> - **Stage 1** = documentation review; **Stage 2** = on-site audit.
> - **Major NC** closure = 60 days; **Minor NC** closure = 90 days.
> - Helps comply with **FSSAI, FDA FSMA, EU 178/2002, Codex Alimentarius**.
> - **HLS (High-Level Structure)** of ISO 22000:2018 has 10 clauses with PDCA.
> - **17 categories** of PRPs are defined in ISO/TS 22002-1:2009.
> - Recognized globally by retailers like **Walmart, Tesco, Carrefour, Metro**.
> - **Risk-based thinking** is applied at both organizational and operational levels.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: BRC IOP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT503 - FOOD SAFETY AND QUALITY REGULATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** BRC IOP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# BRC IOP — Brand Reputation Compliance International Open Standard for Packaging Materials

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **BRC IOP (Brand Reputation Compliance — International Open Standards for Packaging Materials)** is a globally recognized, GFSI (Global Food Safety Initiative) **benchmarked technical standard** published by **BRCGS (Brand Reputation Compliance Global Standards)** for manufacturers of packaging materials that come into contact with food, consumer products, or where packaging integrity and safety are critical. The acronym "IOP" formally denotes "International Open Standard for Packaging."

The standard provides a structured framework to manage **product safety, quality, legality, and fitness for purpose** throughout the entire packaging manufacturing supply chain — from raw material sourcing to final dispatch.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you buy a juice from a shop. The juice is manufactured under strict food safety rules (FSSC 22000, BRC Food, etc.), but the **bottle, the cap, the printed label, and the shrink-wrap** were all made by a different company. If that packaging supplier uses contaminated recycled paper, toxic inks, or unsanitary machinery, the perfectly safe juice becomes unsafe the moment it touches the packaging.

> **BRC IOP is the "safety passport"** required from the packaging maker — proving that the *box around your food* is as safe and clean as the *food inside it*.

### Key Terminology & Salient Features

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **BRCGS** | The publishing body (formerly Brand Reputation through Compliance) |
| **IOP** | International Open Standard (open to all, irrespective of trade membership) |
| **GFSI** | Global Food Safety Initiative — BRC IOP is **benchmarked** against GFSI v2020.1 |
| **Issue 6** | Current version (published **2024**); Issue 5 released 2020, Issue 4 in 2016 |
| **Scope** | All packaging types: flexible, rigid, paper, board, glass, metal, plastic, wood |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Open"?** Unlike older sector-specific schemes that required paid association membership, BRC IOP is commercially open — any packaging manufacturer worldwide can apply, be audited by accredited third-party CBs (Certification Bodies), and be listed on the **BRCGS Directory**.

### Scope of Application

BRC IOP applies to manufacturers producing:

1. **Primary packaging** (in direct contact with food) — e.g., PET bottles, tin cans, milk cartons.
2. **Secondary packaging** (outer cartons, shrink-wrap, corrugated boxes).
3. **Tertiary packaging** (pallets, stretch films for logistics).
4. **Packaging materials** — inks, coatings, lacquers, adhesives, and printing substrates.
5. **Other consumer goods** — cosmetics, toiletries, household care items.
6. **Construction products** (in Issue 6 onward).
7. **Articles in contact with food** under **EU Regulation 1935/2004** and **US FDA 21 CFR**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Architecture of the BRC IOP Standard

The standard is organized into **seven (7) main sections** plus introductory clauses. Each section contains **fundamental statements (mandatory)** and **clauses (good-practice recommendations)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Every clause begins with a three-tier compliance keyword**:
> - **"SHALL"** → Mandatory, non-negotiable requirement.
> - **"SHOULD"** → Strongly recommended; deviation must be justified.
> - **"MAY"** → Permitted but optional.

### Section-Wise Breakdown (KTU High-Yield)

| Section | Title | Core Focus | Key Clauses |
|---------|-------|-----------|-------------|
| **1** | Senior Management Commitment | Leadership, culture, review | 1.1–1.3 |
| **2** | Hazard and Risk Analysis | **HACCP / HACCP-light** based system | 2.1–2.15 |
| **3** | Quality Management System | Documentation, traceability, complaints | 3.1–3.11 |
| **4** | Site Standards | Building fabric, layout, housekeeping | 4.1–4.15 |
| **5** | Product Control | Specifications, inspection, testing | 5.1–5.6 |
| **6** | Process Control | Operations, equipment, CCPs | 6.1–6.7 |
| **7** | Personnel | Training, hygiene, medical screening | 7.1–7.5 |

## 2.2 The Hazard and Risk Management System (Section 2)

Unlike full **Codex Alimentarius HACCP** (which has 7 principles + 12 steps), BRC IOP adopts a **streamlined 5-step prerequisite-integrated HACCP** approach because packaging manufacturing involves **physical, chemical, and migration hazards** rather than microbiological pathogens.

### The 5 Steps

$$
\text{Step 1} \rightarrow \text{Assemble the HACCP Team}
$$

$$
\text{Step 2} \rightarrow \text{Describe the Product \& End-Use}
$$

$$
\text{Step 3} \rightarrow \text{Construct the Process Flow Diagram}
$$

$$
\text{Step 4} \rightarrow \text{Conduct Hazard Analysis (R-A-P model)}
$$

$$
\text{Step 5} \rightarrow \text{Determine CCPs, Critical Limits \& Monitoring}
$$

> **The R-A-P Model** stands for **Risk Assessment of Products** — three hazard dimensions:
> - **R — Risk** (likelihood × severity)
> - **A — Applicability** (does it apply to this product?)
> - **P — Probability** (occurrence at this process step)

## 2.3 Audit Protocol — Grades and Frequencies

BRC IOP audit outcomes are graded by the third-party auditor based on the number and severity of non-conformities:

| Grade | No. of Major NCs | No. of Minor NCs | Re-audit Frequency |
|-------|------------------|------------------|--------------------|
| **AA+** | 0 | 0 | **Annual + unannounced** |
| **AA** | 0 | ≤ 5 | Annual |
| **A** | 0 | 6–10 | Annual |
| **B** | 0 | 11–20 | 6 months |
| **C** | 1 | ≤ 20 | 6 months |
| **D** | ≥ 2 OR Critical NC | Any | **Suspension** |

> [!WARNING]
> A **Critical non-conformity** (immediate risk to product safety, legality, or quality) automatically results in **Grade D** regardless of other findings, and certification is **not granted**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Definition / Equation | Purpose |
|---------|---------------------|---------|
| **CCPs (Critical Control Points)** | Process steps where control is *essential* to prevent an unacceptable hazard | Mandatory identification |
| **Critical Limit (CL)** | Maximum and/or minimum value to which a CCP must be controlled | Real-time boundary |
| **OPRP** | Operational Prerequisite Programme (ISO 22000 term) — control measure for significant hazards | Lower-level control |
| **Migration Limit (ML)** | Maximum permitted transfer of substances from packaging into food | $\text{ML} \le \text{SML} \, (\text{Specific Migration Limit})$ |
| **Overall Migration Limit (OML)** | EU-wide total migration ceiling for all non-volatile substances | $\text{OML} = 10 \, \text{mg/dm}^2$ |
| **Audit Cycle** | $T_{\text{audit}} = 12 \, \text{months}$ (or 6 for B/C grades) | Frequency of recertification |
| **HACCP Decision Tree** | Q1 → Q2 → Q3 → Q4 → CCP? (Codex) | CCP identification logic |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

In the **Kerala packaging industry** (especially the rubber, coir, cashew, and spice sectors), BRC IOP certification is now the **de facto passport** to export to **EU, UK, US, and Australia**, because global retailers (Tesco, Walmart, Carrefour, Aldi) **mandate GFSI-benchmarked audits** for all packaging suppliers under their **vendor qualification protocols**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — Implementing BRC IOP in a Packaging Unit

## 3.1 Conceptual Derivation of the Audit-Grade Decision Tree

The grading algorithm of BRC IOP can be expressed as a sequential conditional logic block. Let us derive it:

$$
G = f(N_C, N_m)
$$

Where:
- $G$ = Final Audit Grade
- $N_C$ = Number of **Critical** non-conformities
- $N_m$ = Number of **Major** non-conformities

### Step 1 — Critical NC Override Check

$$
\text{IF} \; N_C \geq 1 \; \Rightarrow \; G = \text{D}
$$

This is the **highest priority condition**. Even one critical non-conformity (e.g., using a banned ink containing lead chromate on food-contact surfaces) voids the audit.

### Step 2 — Major NC Count Evaluation

$$
\text{IF} \; N_C = 0 \;\; \text{AND} \; N_m = 0 \;\;\text{THEN}\;\; G \in \{AA+, AA, A\}
$$

### Step 3 — Granular Grade Selection (Based on Minor NCs only)

$$
\begin{aligned}
G &= AA+ \quad \text{if} \;\; N_{\text{minor}} = 0 \;\; \text{AND unannounced audit opted} \\
G &= AA \quad \;\text{if} \;\; N_{\text{minor}} \le 5 \\
G &= A \quad \;\text{if} \;\; 6 \le N_{\text{minor}} \le 10 \\
G &= B \quad \;\text{if} \;\; 11 \le N_{\text{minor}} \le 20 \\
G &= C \quad \;\text{if} \;\; N_m = 1 \;\; \text{AND} \; N_{\text{minor}} \le 20 \\
G &= D \quad \;\text{if} \;\; N_m \ge 2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Recertification Window

$$
T_{\text{next-audit}} = \begin{cases} 12 \;\text{months}, & G \in \{AA+, AA, A\} \\ 6 \;\text{months}, & G \in \{B, C\} \\ \text{Suspension}, & G = D \end{cases}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Audit Outcome Simulation

**Problem Statement:**
A packaging unit (PET bottle manufacturer) was audited by a BRCGS-accredited CB. Findings:
- Critical NCs = 0
- Major NCs = 1 (failure to validate allergen-cleaning between colour-changeover)
- Minor NCs = 12 (housekeeping, record-keeping, calibration)

**Solution (Step-by-Step):**

$$
\text{Step 1: } N_C = 0 \Rightarrow \text{Critical override NOT triggered.}
$$

$$
\text{Step 2: } N_m = 1 \Rightarrow G \in \{B, C\} \text{ based on minor count.}
$$

$$
\text{Step 3: } N_{\text{minor}} = 12 \Rightarrow \text{Since } N_m = 1, \text{ check } N_{\text{minor}} \le 20.
$$

$$
12 \le 20 \Rightarrow \text{True.}
$$

$$
\therefore G = C
$$

$$
T_{\text{next-audit}} = 6 \;\text{months}.
$$

> **Interpretation:** The unit must close out all NCs and re-audit within 6 months; failure to do so results in certificate withdrawal.

## 3.3 Migration Limit Calculation — Worked Example

A printed paperboard (450 cm² = 4.5 dm²) contacts a fatty food. Measured migration of a photoinitiator = **6 mg/kg food simulant**.

$$
\text{Specific Migration Limit (SML) of photoinitiator} = 0.05 \; \text{mg/kg}
$$

$$
\text{Observed migration} = 6 \; \text{mg/kg} \gg \text{SML} = 0.05 \; \text{mg/kg}
$$

$$
\therefore \text{Non-compliant} \Rightarrow \text{Product recall under EU 1935/2004.}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For packaging compliance under EU regulations, BRC IOP Section 5.3 mandates **supplier declarations of compliance (DoC)** and **annual migration testing** by ISO 17025-accredited labs.

## 3.4 HACCP Logic for a Lamination Process

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Q1: Do preventive control measures exist?} &\rightarrow \text{YES (temperature \& viscosity control)} \\
\text{Q2: Is the step specifically designed to eliminate/reduce the hazard?} &\rightarrow \text{YES (heat-set lamination removes solvent residues)} \\
\therefore \text{CCP IDENTIFIED} &\rightarrow \text{Critical Limit: } T = 130 \pm 5^\circ C
\end{aligned}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 BRC IOP Framework — Modular Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[BRC IOP Standard Architecture] --> B[Section 1 Senior Management Commitment]
    A --> C[Section 2 Hazard and Risk Management]
    A --> D[Section 3 Quality Management System]
    A --> E[Section 4 Site Standards]
    A --> F[Section 5 Product Control]
    A --> G[Section 6 Process Control]
    A --> H[Section 7 Personnel]

    B --> B1[Leadership Policy]
    B --> B2[Management Review]
    B --> B3[Food Safety Culture]

    C --> C1[Assemble HACCP Team]
    C --> C2[Product Description]
    C --> C3[Process Flow Diagram]
    C --> C4[Hazard Analysis R-A-P]
    C --> C5[Identify CCPs]

    D --> D1[Building Fabric and Layout]
    D --> D2[Housekeeping and Cleaning]
    D --> D3[Pest Control]
    D --> D4[Waste Management]

    F --> F1[Raw Material Specifications]
    F --> F2[Supplier Approval]
    F --> F3[Finished Product Inspection]
    F --> F4[Migration Testing]

    G --> G1[Equipment Calibration]
    G --> G2[Process Validation]
    G --> G3[CCPs Monitoring]
    G --> G4[Traceability]
```

## 4.2 BRC IOP Audit Cycle Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Stage 1 Document Review] --> P2[Stage 2 On-site Audit]
    P2 --> P3{NC Analysis}
    P3 -->|Zero NCs| P4[Grade AA Awarded]
    P3 -->|Minor NCs Only| P5[Grade AA A B C]
    P3 -->|Critical NCs| P6[Grade D Suspension]
    P4 --> P7[Annual Surveillance]
    P5 --> P8[Annual or 6 Month Re-audit]
    P6 --> P9[Re-audit after Closure]
    P7 --> P10[Recertification Every 3 Years]
```

## 4.3 CCP Decision Tree (Codex-based, adapted for BRC IOP)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Q1 Preventive Controls Exist?}
    Q1 -->|No| CCP1[NOT a CCP - Modify Process]
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Q2 Specifically Designed to Eliminate Hazard?}
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Q3 Contamination Risk with Subsequent Step Removal?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| CCP2[CCP Identified]
    Q3 -->|Yes| CCP3[NOT a CCP - Address Downstream]
    Q3 -->|No| CCP4[CCP Identified]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**
*Define BRC IOP. What does the term "Open Standard" signify in its nomenclature?*

**Model Answer:**
BRC IOP stands for **Brand Reputation Compliance — International Open Standard for Packaging Materials**. It is a globally recognized, GFSI-benchmarked technical standard developed by BRCGS for manufacturers of food-contact packaging. The term "Open Standard" signifies that the framework is **commercially accessible to all packaging manufacturers worldwide**, regardless of any trade body membership — unlike older proprietary schemes that required paid association enrolment. This openness promotes universal adoption across the supply chain.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**
*List the seven main sections of the BRC IOP standard (Issue 6).*

**Model Answer:**
The seven sections are:
1. Senior Management Commitment
2. Hazard and Risk Management
3. Quality Management System
4. Site Standards
5. Product Control
6. Process Control
7. Personnel

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / 2024 Scheme Model]**
*(a)* Explain in detail the **Hazard and Risk Management** framework (Section 2) of BRC IOP. How does it differ from classical Codex HACCP? *(7 Marks)*

*(b)* Describe the **audit grading system** of BRC IOP. Mention all six grades and the conditions under which each is awarded. *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Answer — Part (a) [7 Marks]

The Hazard and Risk Management section of BRC IOP is built on a **streamlined, 5-step prerequisite-integrated HACCP approach**. The five steps are:

**Step 1 — Assemble the HACCP Team:**
A multidisciplinary team is formed, including members from production, quality, engineering, and procurement. The team leader must have formal HACCP training.

**Step 2 — Product Description and End Use:**
Every product (e.g., PET bottle for dairy use) is described in detail — raw materials, dimensions, intended food contact type, end-user demographic (infants, elderly, general population), storage conditions, and shelf-life.

**Step 3 — Construct Process Flow Diagram:**
A block diagram maps every stage from raw material receipt to dispatch.

**Step 4 — Conduct Hazard Analysis (R-A-P Model):**
For each process step, three hazard categories are assessed — **Risk** (likelihood × severity), **Applicability**, and **Probability**. Hazards include physical (glass fragments), chemical (ink migration, solvent residues), microbiological (low priority for dry packaging but critical for wet-contact), and allergen (cross-contact during changeovers).

**Step 5 — Identify CCPs, Establish Critical Limits & Monitoring:**
Using the Codex decision tree, CCPs are determined. Critical limits and monitoring procedures are documented.

**Difference from Classical Codex HACCP:**

| Feature | Codex HACCP | BRC IOP Section 2 |
|---------|------------|-------------------|
| Number of Principles | 7 principles + 12 steps | Streamlined 5-step approach |
| Hazard Focus | Microbiological (food) | **Physical, chemical, migration** (packaging) |
| Regulatory Frame | Codex Alimentarius | EU 1935/2004, FDA 21 CFR |
| Prerequisite Programs | Separate (PRPs) | Integrated within Section 2 |

#### Model Answer — Part (b) [7 Marks]

The BRC IOP audit grading system consists of **six grades**: **AA+, AA, A, B, C, and D**. The grading is determined by the **number and severity of non-conformities (NCs)** identified during the audit.

- **Grade AA+**: Zero non-conformities, awarded only when the site **opts for an unannounced audit**. This is the highest achievable grade.
- **Grade AA**: Zero non-conformities, or up to 5 minor NCs.
- **Grade A**: 6 to 10 minor NCs; no major or critical.
- **Grade B**: 11 to 20 minor NCs; no major or critical.
- **Grade C**: Exactly **1 major NC**, with up to 20 minor NCs.
- **Grade D**: Awarded automatically if **2 or more major NCs** exist, OR if **any single critical NC** is found. This results in **certificate suspension**.

Additionally:
- Grades AA+, AA, A → **annual re-audit** (every 12 months).
- Grades B, C → **6-monthly re-audit** to demonstrate closure.
- Recertification is required every **3 years**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – 2024 Scheme Model]**
*(a)* Discuss the importance of **Site Standards (Section 4)** and **Personnel requirements (Section 7)** under BRC IOP. *(7 Marks)*

*(b)* What are the **types of audits** recognized under BRC IOP? Explain the role of the Certification Body (CB) and the BRCGS Directory. *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Answer — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Site Standards (Section 4):**
Section 4 establishes the **physical infrastructure prerequisites** necessary for safe packaging manufacturing. Key clauses include:

- **Building Fabric and Layout**: Walls, floors, and ceilings must be smooth, impervious, and easy to clean. The factory must have a **logical material flow** (forward, non-crossing) to prevent raw material contamination of finished goods.
- **Housekeeping and Cleaning**: Master cleaning schedules (MCS) with validated cleaning procedures for every zone. SSOPs (Sanitation Standard Operating Procedures) must be documented.
- **Pest Control**: Annual contract with a licensed pest control operator (PCO). Bait maps, inspection logs, and trend analysis are mandatory.
- **Waste Management**: Segregated waste bins, daily removal, and documented disposal contracts.
- **Lighting and Ventilation**: Minimum **500 lux** in inspection areas; filtered air in critical zones.

**Personnel Requirements (Section 7):**
- All employees must undergo **documented induction training** before deployment.
- Annual **refresher training** on food safety, hygiene, allergens, and foreign-body awareness.
- **Medical screening** for workers handling food-contact materials.
- Defined **personal hygiene rules** — no jewellery, no nail polish, designated uniforms, hand-wash stations at every entry point.
- **Behavioural compliance audits** for hygiene and GMP adherence.

#### Model Answer — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Types of Audits under BRC IOP:**

1. **Announced Audit**: Pre-scheduled, conventional third-party audit.
2. **Unannounced Audit**: Conducted without prior notice; only **Grade AA+** can be awarded if successful.
3. **Short Notice Audit**: 5-day prior notification window.
4. **Re-audit / Follow-up Audit**: Triggered after Grade B/C findings to verify closure of NCs.
5. **Suspension / Withdrawal Audits**: When critical risks are identified post-certification.

**Role of the Certification Body (CB):**
The CB must be **accredited by a national accreditation body** that is a signatory to the **ILAC/IAF Multilateral Recognition Arrangement (MRA)**. Examples include UKAS (UK), NABL (India), A2LA (US). The CB deploys **qualified BRCGS-registered auditors** who conduct the audit using standardized protocols and submit findings to the **BRCGS Compliance Team** for grade verification and certificate issuance.

**Role of the BRCGS Directory:**
The BRCGS Directory (www.brcdirectory.com) is the **publicly accessible global register** of all certified sites. Buyers, retailers, and brand owners use it to **verify supplier credentials** in real time. The directory lists the grade achieved, audit date, scope, and certificate validity — making it a critical procurement tool for global supply chains.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> Students frequently confuse **BRC Food** (food manufacturers) with **BRC IOP** (packaging manufacturers). Always clarify the scope. Also, do **not** mix up BRC IOP with **BRCGS Standards** such as BRC Storage & Distribution, BRC Agents & Brokers, or BRC Consumer Products — these are separate standards. Marks are commonly lost for using incorrect terminology like "BRC Packaging Standard" instead of the precise "BRC IOP — International Open Standard for Packaging Materials."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **BRC IOP** = Brand Reputation Compliance — International Open Standard for **Packaging Materials**, published by **BRCGS**.
- It is **GFSI-benchmarked**, ensuring global acceptance by major retailers.
- The standard applies to **all packaging types** (flexible, rigid, paper, plastic, glass, metal, wood) used in food, cosmetics, and consumer goods.
- **Issue 6** (2024) is the latest version; current key sections are **1 to 7**.
- The **Hazard and Risk Management** section follows a **streamlined 5-step HACCP** approach (not full Codex 7 principles).
- The **R-A-P Model** (Risk, Applicability, Probability) is used for hazard identification.
- **Six audit grades** exist: **AA+, AA, A, B, C, D**.
- **AA+ grade** requires an **unannounced audit**.
- Any **Critical NC** = automatic **Grade D** and certificate suspension.
- Grades AA, AA+, A → annual audits; B, C → 6-monthly audits.
- Recertification is required every **3 years**.
- The **BRCGS Directory** is the official public register of certified sites.
- Migration testing under **EU Regulation 1935/2004** mandates **SML/OML compliance**.
- All audits must be conducted by **ILAC/IAF-accredited Certification Bodies**.
- BRC IOP integrates **PRPs and OPRPs** into its framework, distinguishing it from raw HACCP.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SQF 1000
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT503 - FOOD SAFETY AND QUALITY REGULATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** SQF 1000

# 🍃 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is SQF 1000?

**SQF (Safe Quality Food) 1000** is a globally recognized food safety management and quality certification program specifically designed for **primary producers** and **manufacturers of finished food products** that are ready for consumer use or wholesale distribution. It is administered by the **SQF Institute**, a division of **FMI – The Food Industry Association**, headquartered in Arlington, Virginia, USA.

SQF 1000 forms one part of the broader SQF Program family, which includes:
- **SQF 1000** → For primary producers and food manufacturers (this topic)
- **SQF 2000** → For manufacturers of food packaging materials
- **SQF 4000** → For non-food consumer products

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
> *SQF 1000 is a HACCP-based food safety and quality management standard recognized by the Global Food Safety Initiative (GFSI), providing a rigorous, science-based system for managing food safety risks across the primary production and manufacturing stages of the food supply chain.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant Kitchen Inspection" Intuition

Imagine you run a large restaurant chain. Before opening each kitchen, you need **two types of checks**:

1. **Basic Hygiene Inspection** — Are the floors clean? Do cooks wash hands? Is the fridge at the right temperature?
2. **Master Chef Audit** — Does every recipe follow a Hazard Analysis plan? Are critical cooking temperatures recorded? Is there traceability from farm-to-fork?

**SQF 1000 is exactly this — but for industrial food producers.** The **"basic hygiene"** part is covered by **GMP (Good Manufacturing Practices)**, and the **"master chef audit"** part is covered by **HACCP (Hazard Analysis Critical Control Point)**. Together, they form the SQF 1000 certification standard.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Highlight:** SQF 1000 is the *only* SQF code applicable to primary production (e.g., farms, dairies) and end-product manufacturing. It is **GFSI-benchmarked**, which means it satisfies the world's toughest retailers like Walmart, Costco, and Tesco.

## 1.3 Governing Body and Recognition

| Attribute | Detail |
|-----------|--------|
| Standard Owner | SQF Institute (FMI) |
| Recognition | GFSI (Global Food Safety Initiative) |
| Foundation | Codex Alimentarius Commission |
| Latest Edition | Edition 9 (released 2021) |
| Audit Frequency | Surveillance audits every 6 months |
| Levels | Level 2 (Certified), Level 3 (Comprehensive) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory KTU Note:** SQF 1000 aligns with international regulatory frameworks including **FDA Food Safety Modernization Act (FSMA)**, **Codex Alimentarius**, and **ISO 22000** principles. Examiners often ask about this alignment.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🔬 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of the SQF 1000 Standard

The SQF 1000 Code is built on **three foundational pillars**, supported by a robust **System Elements** framework. Let us analyze each.

### Pillar 1: SQF Food Safety Code — GMP (Good Manufacturing Practices)
This covers the **prerequisite programs (PRPs)** — the basic operational hygiene and sanitation conditions necessary before HACCP can be applied. For primary producers, GMP includes:
- Site location and surrounding land use
- Building construction and layout
- Equipment design and maintenance
- Cleaning and sanitization procedures
- Pest control
- Personal hygiene of workers
- Water quality
- Waste management

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Point for Examiners:** Without effective GMPs, HACCP systems cannot function reliably. GMPs are the **foundation**; HACCP is the **roof**.

### Pillar 2: SQF Food Safety Code — HACCP
A systematic, science-based approach to food safety that:
1. **Identifies** biological, chemical, and physical hazards
2. **Determines** critical control points (CCPs)
3. **Establishes** critical limits
4. **Monitors** CCPs continuously
5. **Establishes** corrective actions
6. **Verifies** the system periodically
7. **Documents** all procedures and records

### Pillar 3: SQF Food Safety Code — Food Sector Categories (FSCs)
SQF 1000 classifies food production into **26 Food Sector Categories** (FSC 1–26). Some examples:

| FSC Code | Category |
|----------|----------|
| FSC 1 | Production, Capture, and Harvesting of Fresh Fish and Seafood |
| FSC 2 | Aquaculture |
| FSC 3 | Growing and Production of Fresh Produce and Nuts |
| FSC 4 | Fresh Produce and Nuts Pack-House Operations |
| FSC 5 | Extensive Broad Acre Farming (Cereals, Grains, Pulses) |
| FSC 7 | Slaughtering, Boning, and Preparation of Meat |
| FSC 9 | Processing of Dairy Products |
| FSC 10 | Honey Processing |
| FSC 11 | Aquaculture Feed Manufacturing |
| FSC 12 | Egg Processing |
| FSC 13 | Bakery and Snack Food Manufacturing |
| FSC 14 | Fruit, Vegetable, and Nut Processing |
| FSC 15 | Canning, UHT, and Retort Processing |
| FSC 17 | Confectionery Manufacturing |
| FSC 20 | Recipe Meals Manufacturing |
| FSC 25 | Repacking of Food Products |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Trap:** Students often confuse **SQF 1000** (applies to primary producers & manufacturers — FSC 1–18 generally) with **SQF 2000** (applies to packaging material manufacturers). Remember: **1000 = food itself**, **2000 = food packaging**.

## 2.2 The Two Certification Levels

### Level 2 — Certified SQF Code
- Comply with all **system elements** (management commitment, document control, records, internal audits, etc.)
- Comply with **applicable GMPs**
- Comply with **HACCP requirements**
- **Not mandatory:** Quality management system

### Level 3 — Comprehensive SQF Code
- All Level 2 requirements, **PLUS**
- Full **Quality Management System** requirements (similar to ISO 9001 principles)
- Includes **product quality specifications**, **customer satisfaction**, and **continuous improvement**

## 2.3 The 11 System Elements

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU Content:** Examiners frequently ask to "list the system elements of SQF 1000". Memorize these:

1. **Management Responsibility** — Top management must demonstrate commitment and provide resources
2. **Document Control** — All documents must be identified, approved, and controlled
3. **Records** — Maintained for minimum 3 years (or product shelf-life + 1 year)
4. **Internal Audits** — Conducted at least annually
5. **Corrective and Preventive Actions** — Address non-conformities and prevent recurrence
6. **Product Identification, Trace, and Recall** — One-up, one-down traceability within 4 hours
7. **Site Identification and Control** — Map of site, surroundings, and zoning
8. **Training** — Competency-based training for all employees
9. **Supplier Approval and Management** — All suppliers must be approved and monitored
10. **Food Defense and Food Fraud** — Mitigate intentional adulteration (VACCP/TACCP)
11. **Allergen Management** — Identify, segregate, and label allergens

## 2.4 SQF 1000 vs. Other Standards — KTU Comparison Table

| Parameter | SQF 1000 | ISO 22000 | BRCGS | FSSC 22000 |
|-----------|----------|-----------|-------|-----------|
| GFSI Recognized | ✅ | ❌ (yes v5+) | ✅ | ✅ |
| HACCP Required | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| Quality Focus | Optional (L3) | Strong | Moderate | Moderate |
| Scope | Food producers | All organizations | Food manufacturers | Food manufacturers |
| Coding System | FSC 1–26 | Clause-based | Clause-based | ISO-based |
| Audit Cycle | 6 months | 3 years | 6–12 months | 3 years |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Since SQF 1000 is a **standards-based** (not calculation-based) topic, the "formula" below represents the **scoring and compliance framework** typically asked in KTU exams.

$$
\text{Audit Score} = \frac{\sum (\text{Met Requirements})}{\sum (\text{Total Requirements})} \times 100
$$

| Compliance Outcome | Score Range | Status |
|--------------------|-------------|--------|
| E (Excellent) | **95–100%** | Certified, no major non-conformities |
| G (Good) | **85–94%** | Certified with minor non-conformities |
| C (Compliance) | **70–84%** | Conditional certification, must close NCs in **30 days** |
| F (Failure) | < 70% or critical NC | Re-audit required |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Pearl:** A single **Critical Non-Conformity** (e.g., absence of HACCP plan, presence of pathogen in finished product) **automatically fails** the audit, regardless of percentage score.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

For KTU students in **Food Technology**, **Biotechnology**, and **Chemical Engineering**, SQF 1000 knowledge is essential for:
- Working as **Quality Assurance Managers** in food processing plants
- Becoming certified **SQF Auditors** (after completing SQF Institute training)
- Designing **food processing equipment** that complies with hygienic design (EHEDG) standards
- Implementing **smart manufacturing (Industry 4.0)** for traceability via blockchain

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

Since SQF 1000 is a **process-implementation standard** rather than a numerical problem, this section provides the **exhaustive step-by-step implementation pathway** required for SQF 1000 certification. Each step is the equivalent of a "derivation" in a mathematical context — the logical sequence a facility must follow to achieve certification.

## 3.1 The 10-Step SQF 1000 Implementation Roadmap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Note:** This 10-step framework is a **guaranteed 14-mark question** topic in KTU ESE Module 4. Memorize the sequence.

### Step 1: Management Commitment and Project Initiation
The **top management** must formally commit to SQF implementation by issuing a documented policy statement, allocating resources (budget, personnel, infrastructure), and appointing an **SQF Practitioner** (the in-house certified expert).

$$
\text{Commitment Document} = \{\text{Policy}, \text{Budget}, \text{Team}, \text{Timeline}\}
$$

**Logic:** Without leadership commitment, implementation fails due to lack of resources.

### Step 2: Gap Analysis
The facility compares its current practices against SQF 1000 requirements to identify **gaps**.

$$
\text{Gap Score} = \text{SQF Requirements} - \text{Current Practices}
$$

**Logic:** Gap analysis produces a prioritized list of corrective actions before formal audit.

### Step 3: Establish the Food Safety Team and SQF Practitioner
Appoint a qualified **SQF Practitioner** (must complete HACCP training and pass SQF exam). The team conducts the hazard analysis.

### Step 4: Document the Food Safety System
Prepare the following mandatory documents:

| Document | Purpose |
|----------|---------|
| Food Safety Manual | Top-level system description |
| GMP Program | Prerequisite programs |
| HACCP Plan | Hazard control |
| SOPs (Standard Operating Procedures) | Work instructions |
| Records | Objective evidence |

### Step 5: Develop and Implement Good Manufacturing Practices (GMPs)
Implement cleaning, sanitation, pest control, personal hygiene, water quality, waste management, equipment maintenance programs.

### Step 6: Develop the HACCP Plan (Following Codex 12 Steps)

The Codex Alimentarius Commission defines **12 logical steps** to develop a HACCP plan:

$$
\text{Codex 12 Steps} = \text{Assemble Team} \rightarrow \text{Describe Product} \rightarrow \text{Identify Intended Use}
$$

$$
\rightarrow \text{Construct Flow Diagram} \rightarrow \text{On-site Verification of Flow Diagram}
$$

$$
\rightarrow \text{List Hazards (Principle 1)} \rightarrow \text{Determine CCPs (Principle 2)}
$$

$$
\rightarrow \text{Establish Critical Limits (Principle 3)} \rightarrow \text{Establish Monitoring (Principle 4)}
$$

$$
\rightarrow \text{Establish Corrective Actions (Principle 5)} \rightarrow \text{Establish Verification (Principle 6)}
$$

$$
\rightarrow \text{Establish Record-Keeping (Principle 7)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often forget the **5 preliminary steps** and start directly with the 7 HACCP principles. The full process is **5 + 7 = 12 steps**.

### Step 7: Validate and Verify the System
- **Validation:** Is the HACCP plan scientifically sound?
- **Verification:** Is the system working as designed?

$$
\text{Verification Frequency} = \text{At Least Annually}
$$

### Step 8: Internal Audit
A **trained internal auditor** (independent of the audited area) conducts a full audit before the certification audit.

### Step 9: Pre-Assessment Audit (Optional but Recommended)
A mock audit by an external consultant to identify residual gaps.

### Step 10: Certification Audit by SQF-Accredited CB
An accredited **Certification Body** (CB) like DNV, SGS, Bureau Veritas, or Intertek conducts the audit:
- **Stage 1 Audit:** Documentation review
- **Stage 2 Audit:** On-site audit

If successful, **SQF Certificate** is issued, valid for **1 year**, with **surveillance audits every 6 months**.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparison with HACCP Principles Mapping

| Codex Principle | SQF 1000 Implementation Element | Reference Document |
|-----------------|----------------------------------|-------------------|
| Principle 1: Hazard Analysis | Mandatory in SQF Element 4.3 | HACCP Plan |
| Principle 2: Determine CCPs | Decision tree (CCP tree) used | HACCP Worksheet |
| Principle 3: Critical Limits | Quantitative metrics (°C, %, ppm) | CCP Monitoring Sheet |
| Principle 4: Monitoring | Frequency, person, method defined | Monitoring Records |
| Principle 5: Corrective Actions | Pre-defined for each CCP | Corrective Action Log |
| Principle 6: Verification | Calibration, sampling, review | Verification Records |
| Principle 7: Record-Keeping | All CCP records, retained ≥ 3 years | Document Register |

## 3.3 Worked Example: Establishing a Critical Limit for Pasteurization of Milk

> [!NOTE]
> This is a typical **KTU 14-mark application question**.

**Scenario:** A dairy plant pasteurizes milk at **72°C for 15 seconds** (HTST method). Establish the critical limit, monitoring procedure, and corrective action.

**Step 1: Identify the Hazard**
- **Biological Hazard:** *Mycobacterium tuberculosis*, *Salmonella*, *Listeria monocytogenes*

**Step 2: Determine the CCP**
- **CCP-1:** Pasteurization step

**Step 3: Establish Critical Limits (Regulatory Standard)**

$$
T_{\text{critical}} \geq 72\,°C \quad \text{and} \quad t_{\text{hold}} \geq 15\,s
$$

**Step 4: Monitoring Procedure**
- Continuous temperature recorder with chart
- Flow diversion valve activates if temperature drops below **72°C**
- Frequency: **Continuous**

**Step 5: Corrective Action**
- Divert substandard product to **re-pasteurization**
- Investigate root cause within **24 hours**
- Record in **Corrective Action Log**

**Step 6: Verification**
- Daily calibration of temperature sensors (using **certified reference thermometer**)
- Monthly microbiological testing (Aerobic Plate Count, Coliform count)

**Step 7: Records**
- Pasteurization charts retained for **2 years** (Codex recommendation)
- Lab test reports retained for **3 years**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validation Reference:** FDA Pasteurized Milk Ordinance (PMO) — Time-Temperature standards for HTST pasteurization.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — SQF 1000 Certification Lifecycle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** Diagram-based questions on SQF certification cycle frequently appear for **3–7 marks**. Memorize the cycle direction.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Management Commitment"] --> B["Gap Analysis"]
    B --> C["Appoint SQF Practitioner"]
    C --> D["Document Food Safety System"]
    D --> E["Implement GMP Programs"]
    E --> F["Develop HACCP Plan Codex 12 Steps"]
    F --> G["Validate and Verify System"]
    G --> H["Internal Audit"]
    H --> I["Pre Assessment Audit"]
    I --> J["Certification Audit Stage 1 Documentation"]
    J --> K["Certification Audit Stage 2 On-site"]
    K --> L{Audit Result}
    L -->|Pass| M["SQF Certificate Issued Validity 1 Year"]
    L -->|Fail| N["Corrective Actions Required"]
    N --> O["Re-audit within 90 days"]
    O --> K
    M --> P["Surveillance Audit every 6 months"]
    P --> Q{"Compliance Maintained"}
    Q -->|Yes| P
    Q -->|No| N
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Three Pillars of SQF 1000 Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    Root["SQF 1000 Code"] --> P1["Pillar 1: GMP Prerequisite Programs"]
    Root --> P2["Pillar 2: HACCP Codex 12 Steps"]
    Root --> P3["Pillar 3: Food Sector Categories FSC 1 to 26"]
    Root --> P4["11 System Elements"]
    P1 --> P1a["Site and Equipment"]
```
