```mermaid
    P1 --> P1c["Personal Hygiene"]
    P1 --> P1d["Pest Control"]
    P2 --> P2a["Hazard Analysis"]
    P2 --> P2b["Critical Control Points"]
    P2 --> P2c["Critical Limits"]
    P2 --> P2d["Monitoring Procedures"]
    P2 --> P2e["Corrective Actions"]
    P2 --> P2f["Verification Activities"]
    P2 --> P2g["Documentation and Records"]
    P3 --> P3a["FSC 1 to 6 Primary Production"]
    P3 --> P3b["FSC 7 to 18 Manufacturing"]
    P4 --> P4a["Management Responsibility"]
    P4 --> P4b["Document Control"]
    P4 --> P4c["Records"]
    P4 --> P4d["Internal Audits"]
    P4 --> P4e["Corrective Actions"]
    P4 --> P4f["Trace and Recall"]
    P4 --> P4g["Site Identification"]
    P4 --> P4h["Training"]
    P4 --> P4i["Supplier Management"]
    P4 --> P4j["Food Defense and Fraud"]
    P4 --> P4k["Allergen Management"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Audit Scoring Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Audit Concludes"] --> B["Calculate Compliance Score"]
    B --> C{Score Range}
    C -->|"95 to 100 percent"| D["Grade E Excellent"]
    C -->|"85 to 94 percent"| E["Grade G Good"]
    C -->|"70 to 84 percent"| F["Grade C Compliance Conditional"]
    C -->|"Less than 70 percent"| G["Grade F Failure"]
    C -->|"Any Critical NC present"| G
    D --> H["SQF Certificate Awarded"]
    E --> H
    F --> I["Close NCs within 30 days"]
    I --> H
    G --> J["Re-audit Required after 30 days"]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Document Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Food Safety Manual Tier 1"] --> B["GMP Procedures Tier 2"]
    A --> C["HACCP Plan Tier 2"]
    A --> D["System Element SOPs Tier 2"]
    B --> B1["Cleaning SOP"]
    B --> B2["Pest Control SOP"]
    B --> B3["Personal Hygiene SOP"]
    C --> C1["Hazard Analysis Worksheet"]
    C --> C2["CCP Decision Tree"]
    C --> C3["Critical Limit Table"]
    D --> D1["Internal Audit SOP"]
    D --> D2["Recall SOP"]
    D --> D3["Supplier Approval SOP"]
    A --> E["Records Tier 3"]
    E --> E1["CCP Monitoring Records"]
    E --> E2["Training Records"]
    E --> E3["Calibration Records"]
    E --> E4["Audit Reports"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 🎯 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**"Define SQF 1000 and mention its governing body."**

**Model Answer:**

SQF 1000 is a HACCP-based food safety and quality management standard designed for **primary producers** and **manufacturers** of food products ready for consumer use. It is governed by the **SQF Institute**, a division of **FMI (Food Marketing Institute)**. SQF 1000 is **GFSI-recognized**, making it acceptable to global retailers. The standard includes GMP, HACCP, and 11 mandatory system elements, with two certification levels: **Level 2 (Certified)** and **Level 3 (Comprehensive)**.

*(Word count: ~70 — ideal for 3-mark answer)*

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**"List the three levels of SQF certification programs."**

**Model Answer:**

The SQF Program has three codes:
1. **SQF 1000** – For primary producers and food manufacturers (FSC 1–18)
2. **SQF 2000** – For manufacturers of food packaging materials
3. **SQF 4000** – For non-food consumer products

Additionally, SQF 1000 itself has two certification levels:
- **Level 2:** Certified SQF Code (food safety focus)
- **Level 3:** Comprehensive SQF Code (food safety + quality management)

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU ESE Module 4 Typical Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **architecture of SQF 1000** with its three foundational pillars and the 11 mandatory system elements. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between **SQF Level 2** and **SQF Level 3** certification with examples. **(7 marks)**

#### 📝 Model Solution:

**(a) Architecture of SQF 1000 (7 marks)**

SQF 1000 is built on three foundational pillars supported by system elements:

**Pillar 1 – GMP (Good Manufacturing Practices):** This includes prerequisite programs such as cleaning and sanitation, pest control, personal hygiene, water quality, waste management, equipment maintenance, and site control. GMPs are mandatory prerequisites.

**Pillar 2 – HACCP:** A systematic, science-based hazard control methodology following the **Codex Alimentarius 12-step approach** (5 preliminary steps + 7 principles). It identifies biological, chemical, and physical hazards, determines Critical Control Points (CCPs), establishes critical limits, monitors CCPs, defines corrective actions, verifies the system, and maintains records.

**Pillar 3 – Food Sector Categories (FSCs):** SQF 1000 covers **26 FSCs** ranging from FSC 1 (Fresh Fish Harvest) to FSC 26 (Storage and Distribution).

**11 System Elements:**
1. Management Responsibility
2. Document Control
3. Records (≥3 years retention)
4. Internal Audits (annual)
5. Corrective and Preventive Actions
6. Product Identification, Trace, and Recall (within 4 hours)
7. Site Identification
8. Training (competency-based)
9. Supplier Approval
10. Food Defense and Food Fraud
11. Allergen Management

---

**(b) SQF Level 2 vs. Level 3 (7 marks)**

| Parameter | Level 2 (Certified) | Level 3 (Comprehensive) |
|-----------|---------------------|--------------------------|
| Food Safety Focus | Yes | Yes |
| HACCP Required | Yes | Yes |
| GMP Required | Yes | Yes |
| Quality Management System (QMS) | Not Mandatory | **Mandatory** |
| Customer Satisfaction | Not Required | **Required** |
| Continuous Improvement | Optional | **Mandatory** |
| Typical Industry Use | Smaller processors, primary producers | Large manufacturers exporting globally |

**Example:** A small dairy processing unit (FSC 9) supplying only locally may opt for Level 2, while a multinational ice-cream manufacturer exporting to EU and USA requires Level 3 to satisfy retailer requirements.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice for Internal Option]**

**(a)** Describe the **Codex Alimentarius 12-step HACCP methodology** as adopted in SQF 1000. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fruit juice plant pasteurizes mango pulp at **95°C for 30 seconds**. As an SQF Practitioner, develop the **CCP monitoring procedure, critical limits, and corrective actions** for this process. **(7 marks)**

#### 📝 Model Solution:

**(a) Codex 12-Step HACCP Methodology (7 marks)**

**Preliminary Steps (Steps 0–4):**

1. **Step 0:** Assemble the HACCP team (multi-disciplinary: production, quality, engineering, sanitation)
2. **Step 1:** Describe the product (ingredients, packaging, shelf-life, storage)
3. **Step 2:** Identify intended use and target consumer
4. **Step 3:** Construct the process flow diagram (from raw material receipt to dispatch)
5. **Step 4:** On-site verification of the flow diagram

**Seven HACCP Principles (Steps 5–11):**

6. **Principle 1:** Conduct hazard analysis (Biological, Chemical, Physical)
7. **Principle 2:** Determine Critical Control Points using the CCP decision tree
8. **Principle 3:** Establish critical limits (e.g., temperature, pH, time)
9. **Principle 4:** Establish monitoring procedures (frequency, person, method)
10. **Principle 5:** Establish corrective actions
11. **Principle 6:** Establish verification procedures (calibration, testing, audits)
12. **Principle 7:** Establish documentation and record-keeping

---

**(b) CCP Development for Mango Pulp Pasteurization (7 marks)**

**Hazard Identification:**
- **Biological Hazard:** *Salmonella*, *E. coli*, spore-forming bacteria (e.g., *Bacillus* spp.)
- **Chemical Hazard:** Pesticide residues from mango skin
- **Physical Hazard:** None significant

**CCP Determination:**
- **CCP-2:** Pasteurization step (final pathogen kill before aseptic packaging)

**Critical Limits:**

$$
T \geq 95\,°C \quad \text{and} \quad t \geq 30\,s
$$

**Monitoring Procedure:**
- Continuous temperature recorder at the holding tube outlet
- Frequency: **Continuous (every second)**
- Person: **Trained Pasteurizer Operator**

**Corrective Actions:**
- If T < 95°C → divert to **re-pasteurization** using 3-way valve
- Hold product; investigate equipment fault within 24 hours
- Record deviation in **Corrective Action Register**

**Verification:**
- Daily calibration of temperature probe using certified reference thermometer
- Weekly microbial testing (Total Plate Count < 10 CFU/g)
- Annual review of HACCP plan

**Records:**
- Pasteurization charts, lab reports, calibration records — retained for **3 years**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 
> **Common Mistake 1:** Students often write **"7 HACCP Principles"** as the answer instead of **"12 Codex Steps"**. Always specify that SQF follows **Codex 12-step methodology** (5 preliminary + 7 principles).
> 
> **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing **SQF 1000** with **SQF 2000**. SQF 1000 = food manufacturers & primary producers; SQF 2000 = food **packaging** manufacturers. Markers deduct **2–3 marks** for this confusion.
> 
> **Common Mistake 3:** Failing to mention the **GFSI recognition** of SQF 1000. Examiners allocate marks specifically for global recognition context.
> 
> **Common Mistake 4:** Writing only the **7 principles** without the **5 preliminary steps**. SQF 1000 mandates the **complete Codex 12-step process**.
> 
> **Common Mistake 5:** Forgetting the **surveillance audit every 6 months** requirement. Certificate validity is **1 year**, but surveillance is **half-yearly**.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **SQF** = **Safe Quality Food**, administered by **SQF Institute (FMI)**
- **SQF 1000** applies to **primary producers & food manufacturers**, not packaging (that's SQF 2000)
- **Three Pillars:** GMP, HACCP, Food Sector Categories (FSC 1–26)
- **GFSI-recognized** — accepted by Walmart, Tesco, Costco globally
- **Two Levels:** Level 2 (Certified) + Level 3 (Comprehensive + QMS)
- **Codex Alimentarius 12 Steps** = 5 preliminary steps + 7 HACCP principles
- **11 System Elements** — Management responsibility through allergen management
- **Certificate validity:** 1 year, with **surveillance audits every 6 months**
- **Audit grades:** E (95–100%), G (85–94%), C (70–84%), F (<70% or Critical NC)
- **Critical NC** = automatic failure regardless of score
- **Records retention:** ≥ 3 years (or product shelf-life + 1 year, whichever is greater)
- **Traceability:** Must achieve **one-up, one-down** within **4 hours**
- **Foundation:** Codex Alimentarius, **aligns with FSMA, ISO 22000**
- **Latest edition:** Edition 9 (2021)

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SQF 2000
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT503 - FOOD SAFETY AND QUALITY REGULATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** SQF 2000

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SQF 2000 (Safe Quality Food – 2000)** is a **HACCP-based supplier assurance code** specifically designed for the **food manufacturing, processing, and packaging** sector. It is a globally recognized, third-party audited food safety and quality management standard originally developed in **1994** by the **Western Australian Department of Agriculture (AGWEST)** and currently owned and managed by the **SQF Institute (SQFI)**, a division of the **Food Marketing Institute (FMI), Washington, D.C., USA**.

The numerical suffix "**2000**" is **NOT a version number** — it denotes the **manufacturing tier** of the SQF family:
- **SQF 1000** → Primary Production (Farm to Gate)
- **SQF 2000** → Manufacturing, Processing, and Packaging
- **SQF 3000** → Retail, Food Service, and Catering

SQF 2000 is officially **benchmarked by the Global Food Safety Initiative (GFSI)**, which means it is accepted by major global retailers (Walmart, Costco, Tesco, etc.) as proof of compliance with international food safety norms.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Passport for Food Factories"**
> 
> Imagine you run a spice factory in Kerala that wants to export its products to a supermarket in Germany. The German buyer will NOT trust your word alone — they need a **universally recognized "passport"** that proves your factory operates under strict, audited safety and quality rules. **SQF 2000 is exactly that passport.**
> 
> Think of SQF 2000 as a **three-layer safety net**:
> - **Layer 1 (Foundation)**: Documented policies, hygiene, sanitation, training.
> - **Layer 2 (Middle)**: A working **HACCP plan** that identifies and controls biological, chemical, and physical hazards.
> - **Layer 3 (Top)**: A **Quality Management System (QMS)** that ensures consistent product specifications, customer satisfaction, and continuous improvement.
> 
> A factory WITHOUT SQF 2000 is like a traveler without a passport — trusted only locally. A factory WITH SQF 2000 is a **globally trusted citizen** of the food supply chain.

---

## 1.3 Key Standardized Metrics in SQF 2000

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Numerical Benchmarks in SQF 2000 (Edition 7/8/9 Aligned)**
> 
> - **Minimum Audit Score for Certification** → **70%** (Level 2) and **80%** (Level 3)
> - **Major Non-Conformity Threshold** → Any single clause scoring below **40%** or any critical violation
> - **Re-audit Window** → Within **30 days** for corrective actions
> - **Certification Validity** → **1 year** (annual re-certification mandatory)
> - **Audit Duration (typical)** → **6 to 18 hours** depending on facility size and risk class
> - **GFSI Benchmark Scope** → Codes BII, BIII, BIV (food processing categories)

---

## 1.4 Historical Evolution Timeline

| Year | Milestone |
|------|-----------|
| **1994** | SQF 1000, SQF 2000, SQF 3000 codes launched by **AGWEST**, Western Australia |
| **2003** | **Food Marketing Institute (FMI)** acquires SQF rights |
| **2004** | SQF 2000 Code attains **GFSI Benchmarking** |
| **2012** | SQF Edition 7 — major overhaul aligned with ISO 22000 and BRC |
| **2017** | SQF Edition 8 — strengthened allergen and food defense clauses |
| **2021** | SQF Edition 9 — consolidation into a single code across all tiers |
| **2024** | SQF Edition 9 remains the active benchmarked standard |

> **Current edition of SQF 2000 is now subsumed under SQF Edition 9**, but the term **"SQF 2000"** is still widely used in academic and industry parlance to refer to the **manufacturing-tier food safety code**.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Architectural Pillars of the SQF 2000 Code

The SQF 2000 Code is constructed on **two non-negotiable pillars**:

### Pillar I — **Food Safety Management System (FSMS)**
Built on **HACCP** (Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Points) principles codified by **Codex Alimentarius**. HACCP is the **scientific backbone** of SQF 2000.

### Pillar II — **Quality Management System (QMS)**
A structured set of processes that ensure products meet **defined specifications** consistently — drawn from **ISO 9001** philosophy.

---

## 2.2 The Three Certification Levels of SQF 2000

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept**: The 3-Level Certification Hierarchy

| Level | Name | Requirement | Score Needed | Equivalent |
|-------|------|-------------|--------------|------------|
| **Level 1** | Food Safety Fundamentals | Basic GAPs, hygiene, sanitation, regulatory compliance | **≥ 60%** | Pre-HACCP / Foundational |
| **Level 2** | Certified HACCP Food Safety Plan | Implemented HACCP plan + SQF System elements | **≥ 70%** | GFSI Benchmarked |
| **Level 3** | Comprehensive Food Safety & Quality Management | Full HACCP + SQF Quality Code elements | **≥ 80%** | GFSI + SQF Quality Endorsed |

> [!NOTE]
> **Most Indian food exporters target Level 2** because it gives GFSI acceptance — a minimum requirement to supply to global retailers. Level 3 is for organizations that want the **"SQF Quality Endorsement"** stamp.

---

## 2.3 SQF 2000 Code — Structural Breakdown (Edition 7/8 Logical Mapping)

The SQF 2000 Code is divided into **two major components**:

### **Module 2: SQF System Elements (The Foundation)**

These are **mandatory for all SQF 2000 sites**, regardless of certification level:

1. **Management Commitment** — Documented policy, objectives, resources, responsibility.
2. **Document Control and Records** — Master list of documents, retention policy (typically **minimum 3 years** for traceability).
3. **Specifications and Product Development** — Raw material, finished product, and process specifications.
4. **SQF System Verification & Auditing** — Internal audits (minimum **once per year**).
5. **Product Identification, Trace, Recall, and Withdrawal** — Full chain-of-custody system; mock recall must be performed **annually** with **100% traceability within 4 hours**.
6. **Site Standards**
   - **6.1 Location and Site** — Free from contamination sources.
   - **6.2 Construction and Design** — Walls, floors, drainage, lighting (≥ **540 lux** in inspection areas).
   - **6.3 Equipment and Utensils** — Hygienic design, non-toxic materials.
7. **Personnel Hygiene and Welfare** — Hand-washing stations, protective clothing, medical screening.
8. **Training** — Documented competency matrix; refresher training **at least annually**.
9. **Sanitation and Pest Control** — Cleaning schedules, chemical usage logs, IPM (Integrated Pest Management).
10. **Allergen Management** — Segregation, dedicated lines, validated cleaning.

### **Module 11: HACCP Requirements**

HACCP must be developed using **12 Codex steps + 7 Principles**:

#### **The 12 Codex Preliminary Steps**
1. Assemble HACCP team
2. Describe product (composition, packaging, shelf life)
3. Identify intended use
4. Construct flow diagram
5. On-site confirmation of flow diagram
6. List all potential hazards
7. Determine CCPs using **Decision Tree**
8. Establish critical limits
9. Establish monitoring procedures
10. Establish corrective actions
11. Establish verification procedures
12. Establish documentation and record-keeping

#### **The 7 Codex HACCP Principles**
1. **Principle 1** — Conduct Hazard Analysis (Biological, Chemical, Physical).
2. **Principle 2** — Determine Critical Control Points (CCPs).
3. **Principle 3** — Establish Critical Limits.
4. **Principle 4** — Establish Monitoring Procedures.
5. **Principle 5** — Establish Corrective Actions.
6. **Principle 6** — Establish Verification Procedures.
7. **Principle 7** — Establish Record-Keeping and Documentation.

---

## 2.4 The SQF 2000 Audit Scoring Formula

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Formula — SQF Audit Score Calculation**

The SQF auditor scores each clause. The overall facility score is computed as:

$$
S_{overall} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} S_i \cdot w_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i} \times 100
$$

Where:
- $S_{overall}$ = Final certified facility score (in %)
- $S_i$ = Score obtained for clause $i$ (out of 5)
- $w_i$ = Weight assigned to clause $i$
- $n$ = Total number of clauses

Each individual clause is scored on a **0 to 5 scale**:

$$
S_i = 
\begin{cases}
5 & \text{= Excellent / Full Compliance} \\
3 & \text{= Acceptable / Minor Deviation} \\
1 & \text{= Needs Improvement / Major Deviation} \\
0 & \text{= Critical Non-Conformity} \\
- & \text{= Not Applicable (N/A)}
\end{cases}
$$

> **Certification Rules**:
> - **Pass** if $S_{overall} \geq 70\%$ (Level 2) **AND** no critical non-conformities **AND** no single clause < 40%
> - **Fail** automatically if any **critical violation** is observed (e.g., active pest infestation, evidence of contaminated product release)

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Food Industry

| Application Domain | Use of SQF 2000 |
|-------------------|-----------------|
| **Food Processing Plant** | Mandatory compliance for export to EU/USA/Canada |
| **Packaging Industry** | Validates hygienic design of food contact materials |
| **Cold Chain Logistics** | SQF 2000 audit certifies temperature-controlled warehouses |
| **Pharmaceutical-Food Interfaces** | SQF integrates with FSMA (USA), SFCR (Canada) |
| **Indian Seafood Export** | SQF 2000 Level 2 is required by EU buyers (post-MPEDA mandate) |
| **Co-packing / Contract Manufacturing** | SQF certification is a market-entry tool |

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Hazard Severity × Likelihood (HACCP Risk Scoring)

In SQF 2000, the **HACCP team** must quantify each identified hazard. The standard risk matrix used during hazard analysis is:

| Severity \ Likelihood | **Low (1)** | **Medium (2)** | **High (3)** |
|------------------------|-------------|----------------|--------------|
| **Catastrophic (3)** | 3 | 6 | **9** |
| **Major (2)** | 2 | 4 | 6 |
| **Minor (1)** | 1 | 2 | 3 |

> A hazard scoring **≥ 6** is typically classified as a **Significant Hazard** → becomes a candidate for a **CCP**.

### **Step-by-Step Risk Score Derivation**

**Step 1**: Identify the hazard (e.g., *Salmonella* in raw chicken).

**Step 2**: Determine **Severity (S)** based on consequence:
- S = 3 if fatality / hospitalization possible
- S = 2 if significant illness
- S = 1 if mild discomfort

For *Salmonella*: S = **2** (Major).

**Step 3**: Determine **Likelihood (L)** based on historical/contamination data:
- L = 3 if frequent occurrence
- L = 2 if occasional
- L = 1 if rare

For *Salmonella* in raw poultry: L = **3** (High).

**Step 4**: Compute **Risk Score (R)**:
$$
R = S \times L = 2 \times 3 = 6
$$

**Step 5**: Classify:
$$
\text{Significant Hazard} \iff R \geq 6
$$

Since **R = 6**, *Salmonella* is a **Significant Hazard** → proceed to **CCP Determination** using the Codex Decision Tree.

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step CCP Determination Using the Codex Decision Tree

> [!NOTE]
> The Codex Decision Tree is a **logical flowchart** that converts a significant hazard into a CCP decision.

**Step 1: Does a control preventive measure exist for the identified hazard?**

If **NO** → The process must be modified to introduce a control, OR the product must be reformulated.

If **YES** → Proceed to Step 2.

**Step 2: Is the control step specifically designed to eliminate or reduce the likely occurrence of the hazard to an acceptable level?**

If **NO** → The hazard is **NOT a CCP** at this step. Look for another step. (The hazard may still require a Quality Point / OPRP control.)

If **YES** → Proceed to Step 3.

**Step 3: Could contamination with the identified hazard(s) occur in excess of acceptable level(s), or could it increase to unacceptable level(s)?**

If **NO** → **NOT a CCP**. Proceed to Step 4.

If **YES** → Proceed to Step 4.

**Step 4: Will a subsequent step eliminate or reduce the identified hazard to an acceptable level?**

If **NO** → **This step IS a CCP**. (Establish critical limits, monitoring, corrective action.)

If **YES** → **NOT a CCP** (the next step controls it).

---

## 3.3 SQF 2000 Audit Score — Worked Numerical Example

**Question**: A food processing facility has the following clause scores from an SQF 2000 Level 2 audit. Calculate the overall audit score.

| Clause | Description | Score (0–5) | Weight (w) |
|--------|-------------|-------------|------------|
| 2.1.1 | Management Commitment | 4 | 3 |
| 2.2.1 | Document Control | 5 | 2 |
| 2.6.1 | Site Standards | 3 | 4 |
| 11.1.1 | HACCP – Hazard Analysis | 5 | 5 |
| 11.2.1 | HACCP – CCP Determination | 4 | 5 |
| 11.7.1 | HACCP – Record-Keeping | 5 | 2 |

**Step 1**: Compute weighted score for each clause:
$$
S_i \cdot w_i
$$

$$
4 \times 3 = 12
$$
$$
5 \times 2 = 10
$$
$$
3 \times 4 = 12
$$
$$
5 \times 5 = 25
$$
$$
4 \times 5 = 20
$$
$$
5 \times 2 = 10
$$

**Step 2**: Sum of weighted scores:
$$
\sum S_i \cdot w_i = 12 + 10 + 12 + 25 + 20 + 10 = 89
$$

**Step 3**: Sum of weights:
$$
\sum w_i = 3 + 2 + 4 + 5 + 5 + 2 = 21
$$

**Step 4**: Apply the SQF score formula:
$$
S_{overall} = \frac{89}{21} \times 100 = 4.238 \times 100 = 423.8
$$

> **Note**: The ratio must be normalized to a percentage. The auditor's actual scale is normalized differently; the above is a simplified weighted-average percent. The correct **standardized** SQF scoring uses clause scores as percentages (0%–100%) directly. The above illustrates the **weighting logic** of SQF scoring.

**Step 5**: **Standardized SQF % Score Interpretation**:

If we use **percentage score per clause** where 5/5 = 100%, 4/5 = 80%, 3/5 = 60%:

$$
S_{overall} = \frac{(80 \times 3) + (100 \times 2) + (60 \times 4) + (100 \times 5) + (80 \times 5) + (100 \times 2)}{3 + 2 + 4 + 5 + 5 + 2}
$$

$$
S_{overall} = \frac{240 + 200 + 240 + 500 + 400 + 200}{21} = \frac{1780}{21}
$$

$$
S_{overall} = 84.76\%
$$

> **Result**: Facility scores **84.76%** → **PASSES SQF Level 2** (threshold = 70%) and approaches **Level 3** threshold (80%).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: SQF 2000 vs. Other Food Safety Systems

| Parameter | **SQF 2000** | **HACCP (Codex)** | **BRCGS v9** | **FSSC 22000 v6** | **ISO 22000:2018** |
|-----------|--------------|-------------------|--------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| **GFSI Benchmarked** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (raw standard) | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| **HACCP-Based** | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| **Quality Module** | ✅ Built-in | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | Optional |
| **Audit Score Threshold** | 70% / 80% | Not scored | N/A | N/A | N/A |
| **Public Recognition** | Walmart, Costco, Tesco | Regulatory | Tesco, Sainsbury's | Global | Global |
| **Levels Tiers** | 3 (L1, L2, L3) | None | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| **Cost (Approx.)** | **Moderate** | **Lowest** | **High** | **High** | **Moderate** |
| **Best For** | Mid-size exporters | Regulatory baseline | Large retailers | Global brands | ISO-style integration |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 SQF 2000 Implementation Lifecycle (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[StepA: Management Decision to Pursue SQF 2000 Certification] --> B[StepB: Appoint SQF Practitioner and HACCP Team]
    B --> C[StepC: Document Food Safety and Quality Policy]
    C --> D[StepD: Conduct Gap Analysis Against SQF 2000 Code]
    D --> E[StepE: Develop 12 Codex Preliminary Steps for HACCP]
    E --> F[StepF: Apply 7 HACCP Principles to Identify CCPs]
    F --> G[StepG: Establish Monitoring Corrective Action Verification]
    G --> H[StepH: Implement SQF System Elements Modules 2 to 10]
    H --> I[StepI: Conduct Internal Audit Minimum Once per Year]
    I --> J[StepJ: Perform Mock Recall to Verify 100 Percent Traceability in 4 Hours]
    J --> K[StepK: Engage SQFI Accredited Certification Body]
    K --> L[StepL: Stage 1 Document Review Audit]
    L --> M[StepM: Stage 2 On-Site Certification Audit]
    M --> N{Audit Outcome Decision}
    N -->|Score Greater Equal 70 Percent and No Critical NC| O[StepO: SQF 2000 Certificate Issued Valid 1 Year]
    N -->|Score 60 to 69 Percent| P[StepP: Conditional Pass Corrective Action 30 Days]
    N -->|Score Below 60 Percent or Critical NC| Q[StepQ: Certificate Denied Re-audit Required]
    O --> R[StepR: Surveillance Audit Every 12 Months]
    R --> S[StepS: Continuous Improvement and Recertification Cycle]
```

## 4.2 SQF Family Tier Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SQF_FAMILY[SQF Global Food Safety Program Family]
        A[SQF 1000: Primary Production Tier]
        B[SQF 2000: Manufacturing Processing and Packaging Tier]
        C[SQF 3000: Retail Food Service and Catering Tier]
    end
    A --> D[SQF 2000 Higher Level Processing Uses Raw Materials from SQF 1000]
    B --> E[SQF 3000 Distributes Products from SQF 2000 Certified Facilities]
    F[Codex Alimentarius HACCP Foundation] --> A
    F --> B
    F --> C
    G[ISO 9001 Quality Management Backbone] --> B
    H[GFSI Benchmark Recognition] --> B
    H --> C
    I[Regulatory Compliance FSMA EU 178 of 2002] --> A
    I --> B
    I --> C
```

## 4.3 SQF 2000 Level Hierarchy and Audit Threshold (Mermaid Block)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[SQF 2000 Certification Pyramid] --> B[Level 1: Food Safety Fundamentals Score 60 Percent]
    A --> C[Level 2: Certified HACCP Food Safety Score 70 Percent GFSI Benchmarked]
    A --> D[Level 3: Comprehensive Food Safety plus Quality Score 80 Percent]
    B --> E[Module 2 SQF System Elements Only]
    C --> F[Module 2 SQF System Elements plus Module 11 HACCP]
    D --> G[Module 2 plus Module 11 plus SQF Quality Code]
    H[Critical Non Conformity Examples] --> I[Active Pest Infestation]
    H --> J[Release of Contaminated Product]
    H --> K[Falsified Records]
    H --> L[Inadequate Potable Water Supply]
    M[Major Non Conformity Examples] --> N[Missing Documentation]
    M --> O[Single Clause Score Below 40 Percent]
    M --> P[Failure of Trend Analysis]
```

## 4.4 CCP Decision Tree Logic (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Q1: Does a Control Preventive Measure Exist for the Identified Hazard] -->|No| B[Modify Process or Reformulate Product]
    A -->|Yes| C[Q2: Is the Step Specifically Designed to Reduce the Hazard to Acceptable Level]
    C -->|No| D[NOT a CCP at this Step Reassess Other Steps]
    C -->|Yes| E[Q3: Could Contamination Occur Above Acceptable Levels]
    E -->|No| F[NOT a CCP Proceed to Q4]
    E -->|Yes| F
    F[Q4: Will a Subsequent Step Eliminate the Hazard] -->|No| G[This Step IS a CCP Establish Critical Limits]
    F -->|Yes| H[NOT a CCP Downstream Step Controls It]
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Question]**
**Define SQF 2000. Mention its parent organization and the year it was originally developed.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:

> **SQF 2000 (Safe Quality Food – 2000)** is a **HACCP-based, third-party certified food safety and quality management code** designed for the **food manufacturing, processing, and packaging sector**.
> 
> - **Parent Organization**: Originally developed by the **Western Australian Department of Agriculture (AGWEST)** in **1994**. Since **2003**, it is owned and managed by the **SQF Institute (SQFI)**, a division of the **Food Marketing Institute (FMI)**, Washington D.C., USA.
> - **GFSI Status**: SQF 2000 is **benchmarked by the Global Food Safety Initiative (GFSI)**, making it globally accepted by major retailers.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Question]**
**List the three certification levels of SQF 2000 and the minimum score required for each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:

> | Level | Name | Minimum Score |
> |-------|------|---------------|
> | **Level 1** | Food Safety Fundamentals | **60%** |
> | **Level 2** | Certified HACCP Food Safety Plan | **70%** (GFSI Benchmarked) |
> | **Level 3** | Comprehensive Food Safety & Quality Management | **80%** (SQF Quality Endorsed) |

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice Provided)

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **HACCP-based architecture** of the SQF 2000 Code. Outline the **12 preliminary steps** and the **7 principles** of HACCP as required under SQF 2000 Module 11.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **three certification levels** of SQF 2000. Explain the **scoring methodology** used by SQF auditors, including the **threshold scores** and the meaning of **critical non-conformity**.

---

#### **MODEL ANSWER — Question A**

### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**HACCP-Based Architecture of SQF 2000 (Module 11)**:

SQF 2000 makes it **mandatory** for any facility seeking **Level 2 or Level 3 certification** to develop and implement a fully operational HACCP plan based on **Codex Alimentarius standards**.

**The 12 Preliminary Steps (Pre-HACCP Foundation):**

1. **Assemble the HACCP Team** — A multidisciplinary team with expertise in food science, engineering, sanitation, and quality. The team must be led by a qualified **SQF Practitioner**.
2. **Describe the Product** — Composition, formulation, packaging, shelf-life, storage conditions.
3. **Identify Intended Use** — Who will consume the product? Is it for general public, infants, immunocompromised?
4. **Construct the Process Flow Diagram** — From raw material receipt to finished product dispatch.
5. **On-Site Confirmation of Flow Diagram** — The HACCP team must walk the production floor to verify accuracy.
6. **List All Potential Hazards** — Biological (*Salmonella*, *Listeria*), Chemical (pesticides, allergens), Physical (glass, metal).
7. **Determine Severity and Likelihood** — Apply a **risk matrix** to prioritize hazards.
8. **Identify CCPs Using the Codex Decision Tree** — A 4-question logic flowchart.
9. **Establish Critical Limits** — Measurable thresholds (e.g., cooking temperature **≥ 75°C** for 2 minutes).
10. **Establish Monitoring Procedures** — Who, what, when, how?
11. **Establish Corrective Actions** — Pre-planned actions when critical limits are breached.
12. **Establish Documentation and Record-Keeping** — All HACCP activities must be recorded.

**The 7 HACCP Principles:**

| Principle | Activity |
|-----------|----------|
| **Principle 1** | Conduct Hazard Analysis (Biological, Chemical, Physical) |
| **Principle 2** | Determine Critical Control Points (CCPs) |
| **Principle 3** | Establish Critical Limits (CLs) |
| **Principle 4** | Establish Monitoring Procedures |
| **Principle 5** | Establish Corrective Actions |
| **Principle 6** | Establish Verification Procedures |
| **Principle 7** | Establish Record-Keeping and Documentation |

(1 mark for preliminary steps overview, 2 marks for listing 12 steps, 2 marks for 7 principles, 2 marks for HACCP Codex link — total 7 marks)

### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Three Certification Levels of SQF 2000:**

**Level 1 – Food Safety Fundamentals (Threshold: 60%)**
- Entry-level certification requiring basic **Good Agricultural/Manufacturing Practices (GAP/GMP)**.
- Suitable for **low-risk food businesses** and small processors.
- Does **not require a fully implemented HACCP plan**.
- Provides a **foundation** for progression to higher levels.

**Level 2 – Certified HACCP Food Safety Plan (Threshold: 70%)**
- **GFSI-benchmarked** certification.
- Requires a **fully implemented HACCP plan** plus all SQF System Elements (Modules 2–10).
- The **most pursued level** in the global food industry because it provides international acceptance.
- Audit must demonstrate a **working, validated, verified HACCP system**.

**Level 3 – Comprehensive Food Safety & Quality Management (Threshold: 80%)**
- The **highest level** of SQF 2000 certification.
- Requires HACCP + SQF System Elements + **SQF Quality Code (additional clauses)**.
- Includes metrics like **customer satisfaction, product specifications, continuous improvement KPIs**.
- The certificate carries the **"SQF Quality Endorsed"** logo.

**SQF Audit Scoring Methodology:**

Each clause of the SQF 2000 Code is **scored on a 0–5 scale**:

- **5** → Excellent / Full Compliance
- **3** → Acceptable / Minor Deviation
- **1** → Needs Improvement / Major Deviation
- **0** → Critical Non-Conformity

The **overall score** is a **weighted average** of all clauses:
$$
S_{overall} = \frac{\sum S_i \cdot w_i}{\sum w_i} \times 100
$$

**Critical Non-Conformity** = An immediate, serious failure (e.g., active pest infestation, release of contaminated product, falsified records, failure to comply with regulatory requirements). **Any single critical non-conformity results in automatic audit failure**, regardless of overall score.

(2 marks for level description, 2 marks for thresholds, 2 marks for scoring scale, 1 mark for critical non-conformity definition — total 7 marks)

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast **SQF 2000** with **BRCGS, FSSC 22000, and ISO 22000** in terms of GFSI recognition, HACCP basis, quality integration, and certification levels.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **implementation lifecycle** of SQF 2000 in a food processing plant. Mention at least **6 key steps** in sequence.

---

#### **MODEL ANSWER — Question B**

### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Comparative Analysis of SQF 2000 with Other Food Safety Systems:**

| Feature | **SQF 2000** | **BRCGS v9** | **FSSC 22000 v6** | **ISO 22000:2018** |
|---------|--------------|--------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| **GFSI Recognition** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| **HACCP-Based** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **Quality Module Integrated** | ✅ Yes (Level 3) | ❌ No | ❌ No | Partial |
| **Certification Levels** | 3 Levels | 1 Level | 1 Level | 1 Level |
| **Audit Score System** | Yes (0–5 scale) | Yes (Foundation/Intermediate/High) | Yes | Yes |
| **Industry Acceptance** | High in USA, Australia, Asia | High in UK, EU | Global | Global |
| **Parent Organization** | FMI / SQFI | BRCGS (UK) | FSSC Foundation (NL) | ISO (Switzerland) |

**Key Differentiators of SQF 2000**:
1. **Triple-Level Certification** — unique to SQF among GFSI standards.
2. **Built-in Quality Module** (Level 3) — combines FSMS + QMS.
3. **Acceptance by Walmart, Costco, IKEA, Target, 7-Eleven** — major US retailers.

(2 marks for table, 2 marks for differentiating features, 2 marks for GFSI/HACCP/QMS discussion, 1 mark for conclusion — total 7 marks)

### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**SQF 2000 Implementation Lifecycle (Sequential Steps):**

**Step 1: Management Commitment** *(1 Mark)*
Senior management must declare commitment to SQF 2000 in a written policy, allocate budget, and appoint an **SQF Practitioner** (mandatory role under SQF code).

**Step 2: Gap Analysis** *(1 Mark)*
Conduct a baseline audit against the SQF 2000 Code to identify current gaps in HACCP, sanitation, documentation, training, and quality systems.

**Step 3: HACCP Plan Development** *(1 Mark)*
Implement the 12 Codex preliminary steps + 7 HACCP principles. Validate critical limits and establish monitoring frequencies.

**Step 4: SQF System Elements Implementation** *(1 Mark)*
Build documentation, training programs, sanitation schedules, allergen control plans, supplier approval programs, and internal audit calendars.

**Step 5: Internal Audit and Mock Recall** *(1 Mark)*
Conduct at least **one full internal audit per year** and perform a **mock recall** to verify **100% traceability within 4 hours**.

**Step 6: Engage Certification Body (CB)** *(1 Mark)*
Select an **SQFI-accredited CB** (e.g., DNV, SGS, Bureau Veritas, Intertek, TUV) and schedule Stage 1 (documentation review) and Stage 2 (on-site audit).

**Step 7: Certification, Surveillance & Recertification** *(1 Mark)*
Receive SQF certificate valid for **12 months**, undergo surveillance audits (typically 6-month or 12-month intervals depending on CB), and continuously improve.

(7 marks — 1 mark per step with description)

## ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cause Mark Deductions in KTU SQF 2000 Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing SQF 2000 with SQF Edition 7/8/9** — "2000" is the **manufacturing tier code**, NOT a version. Do not write "SQF 2000 Edition".
> 2. **Forgetting the parent organization** — SQF was developed by **AGWEST (1994)** and is now owned by **SQFI/FMI (2003 onwards)**. Examiners expect both timelines.
> 3. **Missing the GFSI benchmark mention** — SQF 2000 is GFSI-benchmarked. Forgetting this costs at least 1 mark.
> 4. **Writing 5 or 12 principles of HACCP** — Codex has **7 Principles + 12 Preliminary Steps**. Mixing them up is a common error.
> 5. **Confusing thresholds** — Level 1 = **60%**, Level 2 = **70%**, Level 3 = **80%**. Examiners test this every year.
> 6. **No mention of "SQF Practitioner"** — A role **mandatory** under SQF. Forgetting this in an implementation question is a 1-mark loss.
> 7. **Skipping the mock recall** — A **mandatory annual exercise** for traceability validation. Examiners often include it in 7-mark sub-questions.

- ✅ **Audit Score Scale**: **0 to 5 per clause** → weighted average % score

- ✅ **Major Audit Bodies**: DNV, SGS, Bureau Veritas, Intertek, TUV

- ✅ **Key Differentiator**: SQF is the **only GFSI standard with a built-in Quality Module** (Level 3)

- ✅ **Relation to Other Standards**: SQF 2000 > HACCP, complements ISO 22000, alternative to BRCGS and FSSC 22000
